Let $C_{up}(t)$ be the cumulative count of rising edges on CU up to scan $t$, and $C_{down}(t)$ for CD. Then:

$$
CV(t) = C_{up}(t) - C_{down}(t)
$$

The QU and QD outputs are governed by:

$$
QU(t) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE}, & CV(t) \ge PV \\ \text{FALSE}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
QD(t) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE}, & CV(t) \le 0 \\ \text{FALSE}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

If both $C_{up}$ and $C_{down}$ increase by 1 in the same scan (e.g., a car enters and exits simultaneously through separate gates), then $CV$ remains unchanged, which is the desired behavior for a parking-lot application.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Function Block Diagram — CTU Instruction

The following Mermaid diagram shows the IEC 61131-3 standard block representation of a Count Up counter, including all its inputs and outputs as per the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input: CU\nCount Up Signal"] -->|Rising Edge| B{"CTU\nFunction Block"}
    R["Input: R\nReset Signal"] -->|Priority Override| B
    PV["Input: PV\nPreset Value e.g. 50"] -->|Integer Constant| B
    B -->|Boolean| C["Output: Q\nDone Bit DN\nTRUE when CV >= PV"]
    B -->|Integer| D["Output: CV\nCurrent Value\nStored in MW"]
    B -->|Internal| E["Edge Detector\nDetects 0 to 1 Transition"]
    E --> B
```

### Block-Level Interpretation:
*   **CU** is the only counting input. It is processed through an internal **edge detector** so that the counter does not increment on every scan while the input is held HIGH — it only counts the *transitions*.
*   **R** is the reset; when `TRUE`, it forces `CV = 0` and `Q = FALSE`.
*   **PV** is typically a constant integer (e.g., `50`).
*   **CV** is the *current accumulated value*, stored in a memory word (e.g., `MW10` in Siemens, `C5:0.ACC` in Allen-Bradley).

---

## 4.2 Function Block Diagram — CTD Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input: CD\nCount Down Signal"] -->|Rising Edge| B{"CTD\nFunction Block"}
    L["Input: LD\nLoad Signal"] -->|Initialize CV = PV| B
    PV["Input: PV\nPreset Value e.g. 100"] -->|Integer Constant| B
    B -->|Boolean| C["Output: Q\nDone Bit DN\nTRUE when CV <= 0"]
    B -->|Integer| D["Output: CV\nCurrent Value\nDecrementing from PV"]
    B -->|Internal| E["Edge Detector\nOn CD input"]
    E --> B
```

### Block-Level Interpretation:
*   The **LD (Load)** input is the *initialization* trigger. It is typically activated by a *Start* pushbutton or a power-on first-scan flag.
*   The Done bit semantics are **inverted** relative to CTU.

---

## 4.3 Function Block Diagram — CTUD Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CU["Input: CU\nCount Up Pulse"] -->|Edge| A{"CTUD\nFunction Block"}
    CD["Input: CD\nCount Down Pulse"] -->|Edge| A
    R["Input: R\nReset to Zero"] -->|Priority| A
    PV["Input: PV\nThreshold Value"] --> A
    A -->|Boolean| QU["Output: QU\nCount Up Overflow\nTRUE if CV >= PV"]
    A -->|Boolean| QD["Output: QD\nCount Down Underflow\nTRUE if CV <= 0"]
    A -->|Integer| CV["Output: CV\nNet Current Value\n= CU_pulses - CD_pulses"]
    A -->|Internal| ED1["Edge Detector CU"] --> A
    A -->|Internal| ED2["Edge Detector CD"] --> A
```

### Block-Level Interpretation:
*   Two independent edge detectors (one for CU, one for CD) feed the same accumulator.
*   The **R** input is the *master reset*. It has the highest priority.
*   There is **no separate Load (LD) input** in a CTUD — initialization is typically done with a separate MOV (move) instruction: `MOV PV → CV`.

---

## 4.4 Ladder Logic Schematic — Cascaded Counters (Textual Block Description)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the topology of a *cascaded counter pair*, which is the standard solution for extending the count range beyond 32,767.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PrimaryCounter["PRIMARY CTU: C1 (PV1 = 100)"]
        I1["Input Pulse\n(e.g. Proximity Sensor)"] -->|CU| C1Block{"C1\nCTU Block"}
        C1Block -->|DN1| C1Out["Internal Relay\nDN1 Bit"]
        C1Block -->|CV1| C1CV["Memory Word\nCV1 Value"]
    end
    C1Out -->|Wired to CU| SecondaryCU["Secondary Counter CU Input"]
    subgraph SecondaryCounter["SECONDARY CTU: C2 (PV2 = 200)"]
        SecondaryCU --> C2Block{"C2\nCTU Block"}
        C2Block -->|DN2| C2Out["Final Output\nDN2 = 20,000 count reached"]
        C2Block -->|CV2| C2CV["Memory Word\nCV2 Value"]
    end
    R["Master Reset\nPushbutton"] -->|Resets both| C1Block
    R -->|Resets both| C2Block
```

### Block-Level Interpretation:
*   The **DN1** output of C1 acts as the *counting input* for C2.
*   The **total count** when DN2 fires is `100 × 200 = 20,000`.
*   The master reset is wired to **both** counter reset inputs simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When drawing a cascade, always label the *DN* output of the primary counter and show it as the *CU* input of the secondary. This is a 7-mark question almost every KTU cycle.

---

## 4.5 Counter Application Block Diagram: Parking Lot System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1["Entry Gate\nSensor S1"] -->|One pulse per car IN| CTUDin["CTUD\nCU Input"]
    G2["Exit Gate\nSensor S2"] -->|One pulse per car OUT| CTUDout["CTUD\nCD Input"]
    CTUDin --> CBlock{"CTUD\nPV = 50\nMax Capacity"}
    CTUDout --> CBlock
    CBlock -->|QU TRUE\nwhen CV >= 50| FullSign["FULL Sign\nRed Lamp"]
    CBlock -->|QD TRUE\nwhen CV <= 0| EmptySign["EMPTY Sign\nGreen Lamp"]
    CBlock -->|CV Integer| Display["7-Segment Display\nCurrent Cars Inside"]
    R["EOD Reset\nPushbutton"] -->|Resets to 0| CBlock
```

### Block-Level Interpretation:
*   Entry and exit gate sensors feed the **bidirectional** CTUD.
*   The system uses the **QU** output to light a "FULL" sign (max occupancy).
*   The **QD** output would light an "EMPTY" sign (zero occupancy).

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a CTU and a CTD counter instruction in a PLC. Mention the role of the R and LD inputs respectively.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

A **CTU (Count Up)** instruction increments its Current Value (CV) by 1 on every rising edge of its Count Up (CU) input. It uses a **Reset (R)** input to force the counter back to zero. The Done bit (DN) becomes TRUE when `CV ≥ PV`.

A **CTD (Count Down)** instruction decrements its CV by 1 on every rising edge of its Count Down (CD) input, starting from the Preset Value. It uses a **Load (LD)** input to *initialize* the counter by loading the PV into the CV. The Done bit becomes TRUE when `CV ≤ 0`.

**Distinction:** R *clears* a CTU, while LD *initializes* a CTD with the preset.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for CTU definition + 1 mark for CTD definition + 1 mark for R/LD distinction.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by 'cascading counters'? Why is it necessary in PLC applications?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Cascading counters** is the technique of connecting two or more counters in series such that the *Done (DN)* output of the primary counter serves as the *Count (CU)* input of the secondary counter. This extends the effective counting range of the system.

**Necessity:** A single 16-bit counter can only count up to 32,767. Industrial applications such as shift production tallies, hour-meter totalizations, and long-duration event logging require counts that exceed this limit. By cascading two counters with preset values $PV_1$ and $PV_2$, the total count becomes $N_{total} = PV_1 \times PV_2$, effectively achieving a 32-bit range.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for limitation of single counter + 1 mark for the multiplication formula.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **CTUD (Count Up/Down) counter** instruction. List all its inputs and outputs and describe the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bottling plant uses a conveyor to count bottles. A proximity sensor gives 1 pulse per bottle. Design a PLC program (using ladder logic description or block diagram) to count **1500 bottles** using two cascaded CTU counters. Show the wiring and explain how the total count is achieved. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**CTUD Block Diagram:**

```
        ┌──────────────────────┐
   CU ──┤► [Edge Det] ─┐       │
        │              ├─►[CV] ├──► CV (Integer Output)
   CD ──┤► [Edge Det] ─┘   │   │
        │                  ▼   ├──► QU (TRUE if CV ≥ PV)
   R  ──┤───────────────►[RST] ├──► QD (TRUE if CV ≤ 0)
        │                  │   │
  PV ──┤───────────────►[CMP]  │
        └──────────────────────┘
```

**Inputs (3) and Outputs (2):**

| Pin | Type | Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CU | Input | Count Up | Rising edge → CV = CV + 1 |
| CD | Input | Count Down | Rising edge → CV = CV - 1 |
| R | Input | Reset | Forces CV = 0; overrides all |
| QU | Output | Up Done | TRUE when CV ≥ PV |
| QD | Output | Down Done | TRUE when CV ≤ 0 |
| CV | Output | Current Value | Integer accumulator |

**Functionality Description (3 Marks):**
The CTUD maintains a single integer accumulator CV. On a rising edge of CU, CV is incremented. On a rising edge of CD, CV is decremented. Both inputs operate independently and the net change to CV in a given scan is the algebraic sum of the events. The R input is the master reset with the highest priority. The QU and QD outputs are mutually non-exclusive in general (a CV of exactly 0 with PV=0 would make both TRUE, though this is a degenerate case).

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for I/O list, 4 marks for description and logic.

---

#### Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Problem Decomposition:**
We need to count 1500 bottles using 16-bit counters. Since $1500 \le 32767$, we *could* use a single counter, but the question specifically mandates **two cascaded counters**. So we factorize 1500:

$$
1500 = 30 \times 50
$$

So we choose $PV_1 = 30$ (C1) and $PV_2 = 50$ (C2). The total count when both DNs are TRUE is $30 \times 50 = 1500$.

**Ladder Logic Rung Description (Block Representation):**

**Rung 1:** Primary Counter C1
```
[  Proximity Sensor PS  ]──[CTU C1, PV = 30, R = Master_Reset]──( DN1 )
```
*When PS detects a bottle, it sends a rising edge to C1. C1 increments CV1. When CV1 = 30, DN1 goes TRUE.*

**Rung 2:** Secondary Counter C2
```
[  DN1  ]──[CTU C2, PV = 50, R = Master_Reset]──( DN2 = Final_Output )
```
*Every time C1's DN1 pulses (i.e., every 30 bottles), C2 increments CV2. When CV2 = 50, the final output (e.g., a "1500 Bottles Completed" lamp) turns ON.*

**Rung 3:** Master Reset
```
[  Master_Reset_PB  ]──( RES C1 )──( RES C2 )
```
*Resets both counters to 0.*

**Final Output Logic:**

The "Batch Complete" output is energized when:
$$
Output = DN_1 \cdot DN_2
$$

**Verification:** When 1500 pulses have been received at PS:
- C1 has cycled $1500 / 30 = 50$ times → CV1 = 30, DN1 = TRUE
- C2 has cycled $50$ times → CV2 = 50, DN2 = TRUE
- $Output = TRUE$ ✓

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for factorisation, 2 marks for ladder description, 2 marks for output logic, 2 marks for verification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> A very common mistake is to **add** the preset values (e.g., 30 + 50 = 80) instead of **multiplying** them. Counter cascading is a *multiplicative* operation, not additive. Students lose 3-4 marks here every exam cycle. **Remember: Multiply, not add.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **CTU (Count Up)** instruction in detail. Describe its block diagram, inputs, outputs, and the role of the **Reset (R)** input. How is the **edge-triggered** nature of the count input achieved? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PLC program uses a CTU with `PV = 5` and a CTD with `PV = 8` in a sequential mixing process. Describe the operating sequence and draw the state transition table for the first **10 scans**, assuming the following input sequence: `CU = 1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1` and `CD = 0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0`. Show when the Done bits fire. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**CTU Block Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input: CU\nCount Up"] -->|Edge Detection| E["Internal:\nRising Edge Detector\n(prev_state XOR curr_state)"]
    E --> B{"CTU\nFunction Block\nC5:0"}
    R["Input: R\nReset (Priority)"] -->|Override| B
    PV["Input: PV\nPreset = 5"] -->|Constant| B
    B -->|Boolean| Q["Output: DN\nDone Bit\nTRUE if CV >= PV"]
    B -->|Integer| CV["Output: CV\nCurrent Value\n0 to 32767"]
    B -->|Internal| CMP["Comparator:\nCV >= PV?"]
    CMP --> Q
```

**Inputs and Outputs (2 Marks):**

| Pin | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **CU** | The input being counted; each rising edge causes CV to increment by 1. |
| **R** | The Reset input; when TRUE, it forces CV to 0 and DN to FALSE. Has highest priority. |
| **PV** | Preset Value (integer constant) against which CV is compared. |
| **Q (DN)** | Done bit output; TRUE when CV ≥ PV. |
| **CV** | Current Value (16-bit integer output). |

**Role of the Reset (R) Input (2 Marks):**
The Reset input is the *master control* of the counter. When `R = TRUE`, regardless of the state of the CU input, the counter is forced into its initial state: `CV = 0` and `DN = FALSE`. This is essential for:
- Restarting a batch process.
- Emergency stop conditions.
- Power-on initialization via a *first-scan* flag.

**Edge-Triggered Nature of the Count Input (3 Marks):**
The CU input does *not* increment the counter on every scan while it is TRUE. It only increments on the **transition** from FALSE to TRUE. The PLC achieves this through an **edge detector**, which can be mathematically expressed as:

$$
\text{Edge}(t) = CU(t) \cdot \overline{CU(t-1)}
$$

That is, the current scan's input is TRUE **AND** the previous scan's input was FALSE. The PLC CPU stores the previous state of the CU bit in a dedicated 1-bit shadow register. On every scan, it compares the current CU with the shadow register. Only when the comparison yields `(TRUE, FALSE)` does the edge detector output TRUE for one scan. This prevents the counter from "runaway" incrementing if the input remains held TRUE for many scans.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark diagram + 1 mark I/O list + 2 marks R role + 3 marks edge detection logic.

---

#### Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Setup:**
*   CTU: PV = 5, increments on CU rising edges.
*   CTD: PV = 8, loaded with PV on first LD signal, decrements on CD rising edges.
*   Both start at CV = 0.

**Input Sequence (10 scans):**

| Scan | CU | CD | CU Edge? | CD Edge? | CTU CV | CTU DN | CTD CV | CTD DN | Notes |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | YES (from 0→1) | NO | 1 | F | 0 | F | CTU inc |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | NO (Falling) | YES (from 0→1) | 1 | F | 8* | F | *LD on scan 1 if we set it; otherwise CV = 0 → CV = 7 on decrement |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | YES (from 0→1) | NO | 2 | F | 7 | F | CTU inc |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | NO (steady) | NO | 2 | F | 7 | F | No change |
| 5 | 0 | 1 | NO (Falling) | YES (0→1) | 2 | F | 6 | F | CTD dec |
| 6 | 1 | 0 | YES (0→1) | NO | 3 | F | 6 | F | CTU inc |
| 7 | 0 | 1 | NO (Falling) | YES (0→1) | 3 | F | 5 | F | CTD dec |
| 8 | 1 | 0 | YES (0→1) | NO | 4 | F | 5 | F | CTU inc |
| 9 | 1 | 0 | NO (steady) | NO | 4 | F | 5 | F | No change |
| 10 | 1 | 0 | NO (steady) | NO | 4 | F | 5 | F | No change |

**Assuming CTD is Loaded with PV=8 on the first scan or via an explicit LD signal:**

| Scan | CU | CD | CU Edge? | CD Edge? | CTU CV | CTU DN | CTD CV | CTD DN |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | YES | NO | **1** | F | **8** (LD) | F |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | NO | YES | 1 | F | **7** | F |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | YES | NO | **2** | F | 7 | F |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | NO | NO | 2 | F | 7 | F |
| 5 | 0 | 1 | NO | YES | 2 | F | **6** | F |
| 6 | 1 | 0 | YES | NO | **3** | F | 6 | F |
| 7 | 0 | 1 | NO | YES | 3 | F | **5** | F |
| 8 | 1 | 0 | YES | NO | **4** | F | 5 | F |
| 9 | 1 | 0 | NO | NO | 4 | F | 5 | F |
| 10 | 1 | 0 | NO | NO | 4 | F | 5 | F |

**Observations:**
*   **CTU DN fires when CV = 5** (not reached in 10 scans; would fire on the 5th rising edge of CU).
*   **CTD DN fires when CV = 0** (not reached; would fire on the 8th rising edge of CD).
*   In 10 scans, the CTU receives 5 rising edges of CU (scans 1, 3, 6, 8 — that's 4 rising edges, not 5). Let me re-verify by carefully re-checking the sequence.

**Re-verification of rising edges in the input sequence `CU = 1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1`:**
- Scan 1: CU goes 0→1 → **Edge 1** ✓
- Scan 2: CU goes 1→0 → Falling edge (ignored)
- Scan 3: CU goes 0→1 → **Edge 2** ✓
- Scan 4: CU stays 1 → No edge
- Scan 5: CU goes 1→0 → Falling edge (ignored)
- Scan 6: CU goes 0→1 → **Edge 3** ✓
- Scan 7: CU goes 1→0 → Falling edge (ignored)
- Scan 8: CU goes 0→1 → **Edge 4** ✓
- Scan 9: CU stays 1 → No edge
- Scan 10: CU stays 1 → No edge

**Total rising edges of CU in 10 scans = 4**
**Total rising edges of CD in 10 scans:**
- Scan 1: CD = 0 (initial)
- Scan 2: CD goes 0→1 → **Edge 1** ✓
- Scan 3: CD = 0 → No edge
- Scan 4: CD = 0 → No edge
- Scan 5: CD goes 0→1 → **Edge 2** ✓
- Scan 6: CD = 0 → No edge
- Scan 7: CD goes 0→1 → **Edge 3** ✓
- Scan 8: CD = 0 → No edge
- Scan 9: CD = 0 → No edge
- Scan 10: CD = 0 → No edge

**Total rising edges of CD in 10 scans = 3**

**Final CV values after 10 scans:**
- CTU: CV = 4 (from 4 rising edges)
- CTD: CV = 8 - 3 = 5 (assuming LD loaded 8 on scan 1)

**Done bits:**
- CTU DN: `CV(4) ≥ PV(5)` → **FALSE** (not yet reached)
- CTD DN: `CV(5) ≤ 0` → **FALSE** (not yet reached)

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for setting up the table, 2 marks for correctly identifying edges, 2 marks for CV tracking, 2 marks for DN evaluation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1.  **Forgetting Edge Detection:** Students often treat the input level as the count trigger. A constant TRUE input only counts as ONE pulse, not multiple. This single error loses 2-3 marks.
> 2.  **Confusing LD with R:** In the CTD, the Load (LD) input is *not* a reset — it loads the PV. Writing `LD = TRUE → CV = 0` is a 1-mark penalty.
> 3.  **Wrong Cascade Arithmetic:** Cascading is multiplication, not addition. (Already emphasized above.)
> 4.  **Missing Done bit in CTD:** Forgetting that the CTD Done bit fires at `CV ≤ 0`, not at `CV = PV`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 High-Density Revision Checklist:

*   [ ] **CTU** counts **up**; uses **R (Reset)** to clear; Done bit fires at `CV ≥ PV`.
*   [ ] **CTD** counts **down**; uses **LD (Load)** to initialize; Done bit fires at `CV ≤ 0`.
*   [ ] **CTUD** has **two inputs (CU, CD) and two outputs (QU, QD)**, plus one reset.
*   [ ] All counters are **edge-triggered** (rising edge: `0 → 1`), not level-triggered.
*   [ ] A held-TRUE input counts as **only one pulse**, not many.
*   [ ] **Cascading** multiplies preset values: $N_{total} = PV_1 \times PV_2 \times \cdots \times PV_k$.
*   [ ] DN of primary counter **wires to CU** of secondary counter.
*   [ ] Standard 16-bit counter range: **0 to 32,767** (or −32,768 to 32,767 for signed).
*   [ ] Reset (R) has **highest priority** — overrides all other inputs.
*   [ ] The CTUD's CV is the **algebraic sum** of CU and CD events.
*   [ ] QU and QD are **separate** Boolean flags; they are not mutually exclusive in all cases.
*   [ ] **IEC 61131-3** is the international standard governing these counter block definitions.
*   [ ] Counter values are usually **non-retentive** (lost on power-down); use retentive variants if needed.
*   [ ] The Done bit does **not** auto-reset; it stays TRUE until R or LD is triggered.
*   [ ] CTD is initialized with `LD = TRUE` (loads PV), not with `R`.
*   [ ] In a cascade, each level divides the counting speed — a 2-counter cascade halves the maximum input frequency.

### 🧠 One-Sentence Memory Anchors:
*   *"Ups Reset, Downs Load."*
*   *"Cascading is Multiplication, never Addition."*
*   *"A held signal is ONE pulse, not many."*
*   *"CTU Done at the top, CTD Done at the bottom."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Arithmetic Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT612 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PLC Arithmetic Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. PLC Arithmetic Functions: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLC Arithmetic Functions** are a standardized class of **programming instructions** defined under **IEC 61131-3** that perform mathematical operations on one or more data operands (variables, constants, or I/O values) stored in the **Process Image**, **Data Blocks (DB)**, or **Merker/Flag memory**. These instructions operate on integer (INT, DINT), floating-point (REAL, LREAL), and time (TIME, DATE) data types and update the **Accumulator (ACCU) registers** or destination memory locations upon each scan cycle execution.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (course **OEMRT612**), arithmetic functions form the **computational backbone** of any ladder logic (LAD), function block diagram (FBD), or structured text (ST) program. They allow the PLC to perform real-time mathematical decision-making — a critical capability for **closed-loop control, recipe management, proportional-integral-derivative (PID) tuning, and signal scaling** in industrial automation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "PLC as a Calculator with Memory"

Imagine a **scientific calculator** mounted on a factory conveyor belt. The calculator receives **numbers** (from sensors like load cells, temperature probes, flow meters), performs a **mathematical operation** (like averaging the last 10 readings), and outputs the result to an **actuator** (like a variable-frequency drive or pneumatic valve).

- The **inputs** are sensor data stored in memory words (e.g., `MW10`, `IW64`).
- The **arithmetic function** is the calculator button pressed (e.g., ADD, MUL, DIV).
- The **output** is the result written into a destination word (e.g., `MW20`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why arithmetic functions matter in industry:** A bottling plant weighing 1-liter bottles (target: **1000 g ± 5 g**) uses the PLC's **SUB** function to compute *error = setpoint – actual weight*. If error > 0, the PLC commands a filler valve to dispense more liquid. Without arithmetic instructions, **no closed-loop automation is possible**.

## 1.3 The IEC 61131-3 Standard Arithmetic Instruction Set

The standard mandates a **minimum core** of arithmetic operators:

| Operator Family | Standard Symbols | Typical Ladder Block Mnemonics |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Addition | `+` | `ADD`, `ADD_I`, `ADD_R` |
| Subtraction | `−` | `SUB`, `SUB_I`, `SUB_R` |
| Multiplication | `×` | `MUL`, `MUL_I`, `MUL_R` |
| Division | `÷` | `DIV`, `DIV_I`, `DIV_R` |
| Modulo | `MOD` | `MOD` |
| Exponentiation | `EXPT` | `EXPT` |
| Square Root | `√` | `SQRT` |
| Absolute Value | $\vert x \vert$ | `ABS` |
| Negation | `−x` | `NEG` |
| Increment / Decrement | `+1 / −1` | `INC`, `DEC` |
| Move / Assign | `:=` | `MOVE` |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always specify the **data type suffix** in your ladder diagram (e.g., `ADD_R` for REAL addition). Examiners award full marks only when the suffix matches the data type declared in the symbol table.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Data-Type Boundaries (KTU Mandatory)

The KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy emphasis on **data-type boundaries** because **PLC overflow errors** are a frequent source of program failure in industrial systems.

| Data Type | Size | Lower Bound | Upper Bound | Overflow Behavior |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **INT** | 16-bit | **−32,768** | **+32,767** | Wrap-around (no flag) |
| **DINT** | 32-bit | **−2,147,483,648** | **+2,147,483,647** | Sets OV / OS flags |
| **REAL** | 32-bit IEEE 754 | **−3.4 × 10³⁸** | **+3.4 × 10³⁸** | Sets OV / OS flags |
| **WORD** | 16-bit | **0** | **65,535** | Wrap-around |
| **DWORD** | 32-bit | **0** | **4,294,967,295** | Carry flag set |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Adding `30,000 + 5,000` using `ADD_I` (16-bit INT) yields an overflow because the result exceeds `32,767`. Always use `ADD_DI` (32-bit DINT) when magnitudes may exceed 16-bit limits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of an Arithmetic Instruction Block

Every PLC arithmetic instruction — whether in **Siemens (S7-1200/1500)**, **Allen-Bradley (Studio 5000)**, **Schneider (EcoStruxure)**, or **Mitsubishi (GX Works)** — executes in a **three-stage pipeline**:

### Stage 1 — Operand Fetch (Load Phase)
The CPU fetches the **source operands** from their memory locations and loads them into the **two 32-bit accumulators (ACCU1 and ACCU2)**. For a single-operand instruction (e.g., `SQRT`), only ACCU1 is loaded.

### Stage 2 — Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) Operation
The instruction's opcode is decoded by the **microsequencer**, and the **ALU** performs the mathematical operation. For floating-point operations, the CPU invokes the **Floating-Point Unit (FPU)** co-processor.

### Stage 3 — Store & Flag Update
The result is written back to the **destination operand**, and the **status word flags** are updated:

- **OV (Overflow Flag)** — Set when result exceeds data-type range.
- **OS (Stored Overflow)** — Latched version of OV.
- **CC0 / CC1 (Condition Codes)** — Indicate result < 0, = 0, or > 0.
- **BR (Binary Result)** — Set in some platforms when result ≠ 0.

## 2.2 The Master Formula Sheet (KTU Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below. **80% of KTU arithmetic-function numerical questions** are solved directly from these formulas.

| # | Operation | Ladder Mnemonic | Mathematical Form | Result Storage | Typical Industrial Use |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Addition | `ADD` | $R = A + B$ | $\text{MD20} \leftarrow \text{MD10} + \text{MD14}$ | Summing batch weights |
| 2 | Subtraction | `SUB` | $R = A - B$ | $\text{MD20} \leftarrow \text{MD10} - \text{MD14}$ | Error (setpoint − PV) |
| 3 | Multiplication | `MUL` | $R = A \times B$ | $\text{MD20} \leftarrow \text{MD10} \times \text{MD14}$ | Flow = area × velocity |
| 4 | Integer Division | `DIV` | $R_q = \lfloor A / B \rfloor$, $R_r = A \bmod B$ | ACCU1 = quotient, ACCU2 = remainder | Parts-per-tray counting |
| 5 | Floating-Point Division | `DIV_R` | $R = A / B$ | $\text{MD20} \leftarrow A / B$ | Pressure = force / area |
| 6 | Modulo | `MOD` | $R = A - B \cdot \lfloor A / B \rfloor$ | Remainder only | Conveyor index wrap-around |
| 7 | Square Root | `SQRT` | $R = \sqrt{A}$ | $\text{MD20} \leftarrow \sqrt{\text{MD10}}$ | RMS voltage computation |
| 8 | Absolute Value | `ABS` | $R = \vert A \vert$ | $\text{MD20} \leftarrow \vert \text{MD10} \vert$ | Magnitude of PID error |
| 9 | Negation | `NEG` | $R = -A$ | $\text{MD20} \leftarrow -\text{MD10}$ | Sign inversion for reverse-running motors |
| 10 | Increment | `INC` | $R = A + 1$ | $\text{MW20} \leftarrow \text{MW10} + 1$ | Part-counter pulse train |
| 11 | Decrement | `DEC` | $R = A - 1$ | $\text{MW20} \leftarrow \text{MW10} - 1$ | Reject-bin inventory decrement |
| 12 | Exponentiation | `EXPT` | $R = A^{B}$ | $\text{MD20} \leftarrow A^{B}$ | Thermistor Steinhart-Hart equation |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic Convention:** In Siemens S7 platform, every arithmetic block has a **suffix letter** indicating data type: `_I` (INT), `_DI` (DINT), `_R` (REAL). On Allen-Bradley, **no suffix** is used; the data type is defined in the **tag database**.

## 2.3 Why Overflow Detection Matters — The Mathematical Justification

For an `n`-bit **two's-complement signed integer**, the representable range is:

$$\text{Range}_{n} = [-2^{n-1}, \; +2^{n-1} - 1]$$

If an operation produces a value $V$ outside this range, the CPU cannot represent it natively, triggering **overflow**:

$$V_{\text{actual}} = V_{\text{stored}} + k \cdot 2^{n}, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}$$

For **IEEE 754 single-precision REAL**, overflow manifests when:

$$\vert V \vert > (2 - 2^{-23}) \cdot 2^{127} \approx 3.4028 \times 10^{38}$$

The CPU sets the **OV flag**, and downstream logic can branch to a **fault routine** (e.g., `// JNB _OVF` in Siemens STL).

## 2.4 The Divide-by-Zero Sentinel Rule

> [!NOTE]
> Division by zero is **undefined** in IEEE 754. The PLC sets a **divide-error flag** and outputs `+INF`, `-INF`, or `NaN` (Not-a-Number) depending on the dividend.

The mathematical safeguard used by IEC 61131-3 compliant PLCs:

$$\text{If } B = 0 \Rightarrow \begin{cases} R = +\infty & \text{if } A > 0 \\ R = -\infty & \text{if } A < 0 \\ R = \text{NaN} & \text{if } A = 0 \end{cases}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Pharmaceutical Batch Reactor** — `MUL` and `DIV` compute catalyst-to-reactant ratios from gravimetric inputs.
2. **Water Treatment SCADA** — `SQRT` computes flow rate from differential-pressure orifice-plate transmitters via $Q = C \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$.
3. **CNC Machine Tool** — `ADD` accumulates encoder pulses; `MOD` wraps the tool-magazine index through 360°.
4. **Cold-Rolling Steel Mill** — `ABS` ensures tension-control error is always positive, driving the PID loop direction correctly.
5. **Beverage Bottling Line** — `INC` counts bottles; `DIV` computes average fill rate per minute.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Scaling a 4–20 mA Analog Input to Engineering Units (0–100 °C)

### Problem Statement
A **PT100 RTD temperature transmitter** outputs a **4–20 mA** current loop, which the PLC's analog input module converts to a **raw integer** ranging from **0 to 27648** (Siemens S7 scaling convention). Derive the arithmetic instruction sequence that maps this raw value to a real-world temperature in °C.

### Given
- Raw input $N_{\text{raw}}$ stored in `IW64` (INT, range $0$ to $27648$).
- Lower scale $N_{\text{low}} = 0$ corresponds to $T_{\text{low}} = 0\;°\text{C}$.
- Upper scale $N_{\text{high}} = 27648$ corresponds to $T_{\text{high}} = 100\;°\text{C}$.
- Result must be stored in `MD100` (REAL, in °C).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the linear scaling equation**

The standard linear-interpolation formula used in all PLC scaling problems is:

$$T_{\text{eng}} = \left( \frac{N_{\text{raw}} - N_{\text{low}}}{N_{\text{high}} - N_{\text{low}}} \right) \cdot (T_{\text{high}} - T_{\text{low}}) + T_{\text{low}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known constants**

$$T_{\text{eng}} = \left( \frac{N_{\text{raw}} - 0}{27648 - 0} \right) \cdot (100 - 0) + 0$$

**Step 3 — Simplify algebraically**

$$T_{\text{eng}} = \frac{N_{\text{raw}}}{27648} \times 100$$

$$T_{\text{eng}} = N_{\text{raw}} \times \frac{100}{27648}$$

$$T_{\text{eng}} = N_{\text{raw}} \times 0.003617...$$

**Step 4 — Convert to PLC instruction sequence (Siemens S7-1200 FBD)**

| Network | Instruction | Operand 1 | Operand 2 | Destination | Data Type |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| NW 1 | `MOVE` | `IW64` | — | `MD10` (as DINT) | INT → DINT |
| NW 2 | `MOVE` | Real constant `0.003617` | — | `MD20` | REAL |
| NW 3 | `MUL_R` | `MD10` (cast to REAL) | `MD20` | `MD100` | REAL |

> **Mathematical sanity check:** If $N_{\text{raw}} = 27648$, then $T_{\text{eng}} = 27648 \times 0.003617 = 99.99 \approx 100\;°\text{C}$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Average of Last 8 Sensor Readings Using Shift-Divide

### Problem Statement
A vibration sensor on a pump outputs integer RPM values every **100 ms** into consecutive memory words `MW100` through `MW114` (8 words, 2 bytes apart). Compute the **running average** using only arithmetic functions — no special "AVG" block assumed available.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the summation**

$$S = \sum_{i=0}^{7} \text{MW}[100 + 2i]$$

**Step 2 — Apply pairwise addition to minimize ACCU depth (the PLC has only 2 accumulators)**

$$S = (\text{MW100} + \text{MW102}) + (\text{MW104} + \text{MW106}) + (\text{MW108} + \text{MW110}) + (\text{MW112} + \text{MW114})$$

**Step 3 — Use the associativity of addition**

$$\text{MD200} = \text{MW100} + \text{MW102}$$

$$\text{MD204} = \text{MW104} + \text{MW106}$$

$$\text{MD208} = \text{MW108} + \text{MW110}$$

$$\text{MD212} = \text{MW112} + \text{MW114}$$

$$\text{MD216} = \text{MD200} + \text{MD204}$$

$$\text{MD220} = \text{MD208} + \text{MD212}$$

$$\text{MD224} = \text{MD216} + \text{MD220}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by 8 (which is $2^3$, so a right-shift of 3 bits also works for integers)**

$$\text{Average} = \frac{S}{8} = \frac{\text{MD224}}{8}$$

In ladder, this is implemented as `DIV` with `MD224` as numerator and constant `8` as denominator, storing the integer quotient in `MW230` and remainder in `MW232`.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Velocity Computation from Encoder Pulses

### Problem Statement
A quadrature encoder on a conveyor motor produces **360 pulses per revolution**. The wheel diameter is **$D = 0.25$ m**. The PLC counts pulses over a **$\Delta t = 1$ s** window. Derive the linear velocity $v$ in m/min.

### Given
- Pulses per second = $N_p$ (stored in `MD300`, DINT).
- Pulses per revolution = $PPR = 360$.
- Wheel diameter $D = 0.25$ m, so circumference $C = \pi D$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Revolutions per second**

$$\text{rps} = \frac{N_p}{\text{PPR}} = \frac{N_p}{360}$$

**Step 2 — Linear distance per second (m/s)**

$$v_{\text{m/s}} = \text{rps} \times C = \text{rps} \times \pi D = \frac{N_p \times \pi \times 0.25}{360}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to m/min**

$$v_{\text{m/min}} = v_{\text{m/s}} \times 60 = \frac{N_p \times \pi \times 0.25 \times 60}{360}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the constant**

$$K = \frac{\pi \times 0.25 \times 60}{360} = \frac{15\pi}{360} = \frac{\pi}{24} \approx 0.1309$$

**Step 5 — Final PLC instruction**

$$v_{\text{m/min}} = N_p \times 0.1309$$

This is implemented as a single `MUL_R` block: `MD300` (cast to REAL) × `0.1309` → `MD400`.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Answer Tip:** Always carry the constant to **at least 5 decimal places** to avoid losing accuracy. Examiners deduct marks for `π ≈ 3.14` when `3.14159` is required.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Arithmetic Function to Use?

| Scenario | Recommended Function | Reason | Overflow Risk |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Two sensor readings summed (each ≤ 16,000) | `ADD_I` | Result ≤ 32,000 < 32,767 | **Low** |
| Two sensor readings summed (each ≤ 25,000) | `ADD_DI` | Result ≤ 50,000 > 32,767 | **High** if `ADD_I` used |
| Computing $T^2$ for a temperature ≤ 200 °C | `MUL_R` | REAL avoids 16-bit overflow | **Low** |
| Checking if a counter is exactly divisible by 12 | `MOD` with divisor `12` | Remainder must = 0 | **None** |
| Inverting sign of a 24-bit signed value | `NEG_DI` | Two's-complement safe | **Low** |
| Computing $\sqrt{x}$ where $x < 0$ | `SQRT` + error trap | IEEE 754 returns NaN | **Trap required** |
| Counting parts on a conveyor (0 → 10,000) | `INC` (INT) | Well within range | **None** |

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Truth Table — Status Word Flags After `SUB_I`

Assume `MD10 = 25`, `MD14 = 40`. The instruction `SUB_I` computes `MD20 = MD10 − MD14 = 25 − 40 = −15`.

| Status Flag | Name | Value After SUB | Bit State |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **CC 1** | Condition Code 1 | Result < 0 | **1** |
| **CC 0** | Condition Code 0 | Result < 0 | **0** |
| **OV** | Overflow (single instruction) | No overflow | **0** |
| **OS** | Stored Overflow (latched) | No overflow | **0** |
| **A0, A1** | AND-result bits | Per logical AND chain | varies |
| **RLO** | Result of Logic Operation | Last rung state | varies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OS (Stored Overflow)** is only cleared by the explicit instructions `CLR` or via a `CALL` of `FC42`. It acts as a **sticky fault bit** — perfect for triggering alarms in batch processes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architectural Flow — Arithmetic Instruction Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Initiates FB Call] --> B[Operand Fetch from Process Image]
    B --> C[Load Source 1 into ACCU2]
    C --> D[Load Source 2 into ACCU1]
    D --> E{Opcode Decoded by Microsequencer}
    E -->|ADD| F1[ALU: ACCU2 plus ACCU1]
    E -->|SUB| F2[ALU: ACCU2 minus ACCU1]
    E -->|MUL| F3[ALU: ACCU2 times ACCU1]
    E -->|DIV| F4[ALU: ACCU2 divided by ACCU1]
    E -->|SQRT| F5[FPU: Root of ACCU1]
    E -->|MOD| F6[ALU: Remainder Operation]
    F1 --> G[Update Status Word Flags]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    G --> H{OV or OS Set?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Trigger Overflow Fault OB82]
    H -->|No| J[Store Result to Destination Operand]
    I --> K[System Halts or Fault Routine Runs]
    J --> L[Update Process Image Output Table]
    L --> M[Next Scan Cycle Begins]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Siemens S7 Ladder Implementation of ADD and SUB

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NW1["Network 1: Addition Block"]
        I1[Input I0.0 - Start Pushbutton] --> C1[Contact NO]
        C1 --> ADD[ADD_I Block - IN1: MW10, IN2: MW12, OUT: MW20]
    end
    subgraph NW2["Network 2: Subtraction Block"]
        I2[Input I0.1 - Reset] --> C2[Contact NO]
        C2 --> SUB[SUB_R Block - IN1: MD30, IN2: MD34, OUT: MD40]
    end
    subgraph NW3["Network 3: Output Energize"]
        Q1[Output Q0.0 - Result Reached] --> C3[Contact NO]
        C3 --> COIL[Output Coil Energizes]
    end
    ADD -.->|MW20 Value| C3
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Complete Recipe Scaling Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SENSOR[Analog Input Module - 4 to 20 mA] --> RAW[Raw INT Value - IW64 - 0 to 27648]
    RAW --> CAST1[MOVE to DINT - MD10]
    CAST1 --> MUL[MUL_R - Multiply by Gain Factor 0.003617]
    CONST[Real Constant Block - MD20] --> MUL
    MUL --> OFFSET[ADD_R - Add Offset 0.0 degrees C]
    OFFSET --> TEMPOUT[Engineering Temperature - MD100 in degrees C]
    TEMPOUT --> COMPARE[CMP greater than or equal - High Limit 90 degrees C]
    COMPARE -->|Yes| ALARM[Set Output Q0.0 - High Temp Alarm]
    COMPARE -->|No| NORMAL[Set Output Q0.1 - Normal Operation]
    TEMPOUT --> DISPLAY[HMI Tag Display]
```

## 4.4 Modular Function-Block Architecture — Counter with Modulo Wrap

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PULSE[Input I0.0 - Encoder Pulse] --> INC[INC Block - Increment MW100 by 1]
    INC --> COMPARE{Compare MW100 with Limit 12}
    COMPARE -->|MW100 less than 12| HOLD[Retain Value in MW100]
    COMPARE -->|MW100 equals 12| RESET[Move 0 into MW100]
    HOLD --> USE[Tool Index 0 to 11 Available]
    RESET --> USE
    USE --> MOTOR[Drive Output Q0.0 - Tool Magazine Rotate]
```

## 4.5 Data Type Compatibility Matrix (Graph Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INT[INT - 16 bit] -->|Implicit Conversion| DINT[DINT - 32 bit]
    DINT -->|Explicit CAST Needed| REAL[REAL - 32 bit IEEE]
    INT -.->|NOT Allowed - Compiler Error| REAL
    REAL -->|Truncation Required| INT
    WORD[WORD - 16 bit] -->|Bit Pattern Copy| DWORD[DWORD - 32 bit]
    DWORD -.->|Signed Conversion Required| DINT
    BYTE[BYTE - 8 bit] -->|Zero Extend| WORD
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define PLC arithmetic functions. List any six standard arithmetic instructions defined under IEC 61131-3, giving one industrial application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** PLC arithmetic functions are IEC 61131-3 standardized instructions that perform mathematical operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, etc.) on operands stored in PLC memory and update a destination word plus the status flags.
>
> **Six instructions with applications (2 marks):**
>
> 1. **ADD** — Sums production counts from two assembly lines.
> 2. **SUB** — Computes error in a closed-loop temperature controller as $e = SP - PV$.
> 3. **MUL** — Calculates instantaneous power $P = V \times I$ from voltage and current transducers.
> 4. **DIV** — Computes average flow rate = total volume / time interval.
> 5. **MOD** — Determines conveyor pallet position within a 12-station rotary table.
> 6. **SQRT** — Derives flow rate $Q$ from orifice-plate differential pressure: $Q = K\sqrt{\Delta P}$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the function of the OV (overflow) and OS (stored overflow) status flags in a PLC. Why is the OS flag called "sticky"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **OV Flag (1.5 marks):** The OV flag is set by the CPU immediately after an arithmetic instruction produces a result that **exceeds the representable range** of the destination data type. For example, adding `30,000 + 5,000` using `ADD_I` (INT) sets OV because the result exceeds `32,767`. The OV flag is **automatically cleared** by the next successfully-executed arithmetic instruction.
>
> **OS Flag (1.5 marks):** The OS flag is a **latched (sticky) version** of OV. Once set, it remains **1** until explicitly cleared by the program using the `CLR` instruction or by calling the system function `FC42`. This "stickiness" is intentional — it allows the PLC to **detect a transient overflow** that may have already passed by the time the CPU evaluates the flag, which is critical for **batch-process fault logging** (e.g., detecting a single overflow event during an 8-hour reaction cycle).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, OEMRT612 Module 1]`

**A temperature transmitter outputs 4–20 mA, mapped by the PLC's analog input card to raw integer values 0–27648. This raw value must be scaled to display temperature in the range 0–150 °C. Additionally, the application requires:**
1. **Computation of the temperature error (setpoint − measured value)**
2. **Generation of a HIGH alarm if temperature exceeds 130 °C**
3. **Conversion of the final °C value to °F for the HMI**

**a)** Draw the complete ladder logic (or FBD) showing the arithmetic instruction sequence. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Show all mathematical derivations, state all data types used, and explain how overflow protection is implemented. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) — Ladder / FBD Implementation (7 marks)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph NW01["NW 1: Read and Convert Raw Input"]
        READ[IW64 INT] --> MOVE1[MOVE IW64 to MD10 as DINT]
    end
    subgraph NW02["NW 2: Scale to Engineering Units Celsius"]
        MOVE1 --> CAST[MOVE MD10 as REAL into MD14]
        K1[REAL Constant 0.005425 - equal to 150 divided by 27648] --> MUL_R
        CAST --> MUL_R[MUL_R MD14 times 0.005425 yields MD20 in Celsius]
    end
    subgraph NW03["NW 3: Compute Temperature Error"]
        SP[MOVE REAL Constant 100.0 into MD24 - Setpoint] --> SUB_R
        MUL_R --> SUB_R[SUB_R MD24 minus MD20 yields MD28 as Error]
    end
    subgraph NW04["NW 4: High Temperature Alarm"]
        MUL_R --> GE[CMP greater than or equal - MD20 with 130.0]
        GE -->|TRUE| SET_ALARM[SET Output Q0.0]
    end
    subgraph NW05["NW 5: Convert Celsius to Fahrenheit"]
        MUL_R --> MUL_F[MUL_R MD20 by 1.8 - MD32]
        CONST[REAL Constant 32.0] --> ADD_F
        MUL_F --> ADD_F[ADD_R MD32 plus 32.0 yields MD36 in Fahrenheit]
    end
    ADD_F --> HMI[HMI Tag Display MD36]
```

**Ladder Diagram Equivalent (text representation):**

| Network | Rung | Instruction Block |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| NW 1 | Always ON | `MOVE` `IW64` → `MD10` (INT to DINT) |
| NW 2 | Always ON | `MUL_R` `MD10` × `0.005425` → `MD20` |
| NW 3 | Always ON | `SUB_R` `MD24` (100.0) − `MD20` → `MD28` |
| NW 4 | Always ON | `CMP >=R` `MD20` `130.0` → `Q0.0` |
| NW 5 | Always ON | `MUL_R` `MD20` × `1.8` → `MD32`; `ADD_R` `MD32` + `32.0` → `MD36` |

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) — Derivations, Data Types, Overflow Protection (7 marks)

**Derivation 1 — Scaling constant (2 marks):**

The general linear scaling formula is:

$$T_{°C} = \frac{N_{\text{raw}}}{27648} \times 150$$

Simplifying:

$$T_{°C} = N_{\text{raw}} \times \frac{150}{27648} = N_{\text{raw}} \times 0.005425$$

The constant `0.005425` is stored as a REAL in `MD40`.

**Derivation 2 — Celsius to Fahrenheit (2 marks):**

The well-known engineering formula is:

$$T_{°F} = T_{°C} \times \frac{9}{5} + 32$$

Since $\frac{9}{5} = 1.8$:

$$T_{°F} = T_{°C} \times 1.8 + 32$$

Implemented as two arithmetic blocks: `MUL_R` followed by `ADD_R`.

**Data Types (1.5 marks):**

| Variable | Data Type | Reason |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `IW64` | INT | Native analog-input format |
| `MD10`, `MD28`, `MD36` | DINT | Intermediate scaling |
| `MD20`, `MD24`, `MD32`, `MD40` | REAL | Final engineering units require decimal precision |

**Overflow Protection (1.5 marks):**

The maximum scaled value is `150.0 °C`, well within the REAL range of `±3.4 × 10³⁸`. However, if the raw input were ever corrupted to `32767` (a faulty sensor), the scaled output would be `32767 × 0.005425 ≈ 177.7 °C`, still safe in REAL but **physically implausible**. Therefore, an upper clamp is implemented:

```
IF MD20 > 150.0 THEN MD20 = 150.0   // software limit
```

This prevents the alarm logic from triggering on a sensor fault rather than a genuine over-temperature condition.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A conveyor system uses a rotary encoder producing 500 pulses per revolution. The drive wheel diameter is 0.30 m. The PLC counts pulses in a 100 ms window.**

**a)** Derive the arithmetic instruction sequence to compute the linear conveyor velocity in m/s and display it on the HMI in m/min. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A separate batching application requires adding the weights of three ingredients (each ≤ 20 kg, integer grams) into a total batch weight stored in a DINT. Show the complete ladder sequence and explain how overflow is prevented when each ingredient is at its maximum. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) — Velocity Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Revolutions per second from pulse count (2 marks):**

The number of revolutions in 100 ms is:

$$\text{rev}_{100\text{ms}} = \frac{N_p}{500}$$

The revolutions per second is therefore:

$$\text{rps} = \frac{N_p}{500 \times 0.1} = \frac{N_p}{50}$$

**Step 2 — Linear velocity in m/s (2 marks):**

The wheel circumference is $C = \pi D = \pi \times 0.30 \approx 0.9425$ m.

$$v_{\text{m/s}} = \text{rps} \times C = \frac{N_p \times 0.9425}{50} = N_p \times 0.01885$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to m/min (2 marks):**

$$v_{\text{m/min}} = v_{\text{m/s}} \times 60 = N_p \times 0.01885 \times 60 = N_p \times 1.1310$$

**Step 4 — Ladder sequence (1 mark):**

| Network | Instruction | Operand 1 | Operand 2 | Destination |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| NW 1 | `MOVE` | `MD300` (pulse count, DINT) | — | `MD310` (cast to REAL) |
| NW 2 | `MUL_R` | `MD310` | `1.1310` | `MD320` (m/min) |

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) — Batching Addition with Overflow Protection (7 marks)

**Maximum possible sum analysis (2 marks):**

If each ingredient is at its maximum of `20 kg = 20,000 g`, the total batch weight is:

$$S_{\max} = 20{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 60{,}000 \text{ grams}$$

Since `60,000 > 32,767`, using `ADD_I` would **overflow** the 16-bit INT range.

**Overflow protection strategy (2 marks):**

The first ingredient is moved into a **DINT** register before adding. All subsequent additions use `ADD_DI` (32-bit DINT), supporting the range `±2,147,483,647`.

**Ladder sequence (3 marks):**

| Network | Instruction | Operand 1 | Operand 2 | Destination | Comment |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| NW 1 | `MOVE` | `MW100` (Ing 1, grams) | — | `MD200` (DINT) | First cast to DINT |
| NW 2 | `ADD_DI` | `MD200` | `MD204` (Ing 2) | `MD200` | First addition |
| NW 3 | `ADD_DI` | `MD200` | `MD208` (Ing 3) | `MD200` | Second addition |
| NW 4 | `CMP >=D` | `MD200` | `60,000` | `Q0.0` (Batch Ready) | Alarm if exceeded |

**Verification of overflow safety (0 marks but for student clarity):**

Since $60{,}000 \ll 2{,}147{,}483{,}647$, the DINT range has **7 orders of magnitude of headroom** — even if all three ingredients were at their absolute physical maximum of `20.000 kg = 20,000.000 g`, no overflow would occur. However, the same logic implemented in `ADD_I` would corrupt the data.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Arithmetic-Function Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the data-type suffix** (`_I`, `_DI`, `_R`) — costs **1 mark per occurrence**.
> 2. **Using `ADD_I` where `ADD_DI` is required** — examiners deduct **2 marks** for ignoring the overflow boundary.
> 3. **Writing only the formula but no ladder diagram** — KTU 2024 scheme awards marks for **both theory AND implementation**, typically split **60% theory / 40% diagram**.
> 4. **Truncating decimal results** — using integer division when REAL division is specified costs **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting unit conversion** (e.g., °C to °F) — examiners expect the constant `1.8` and offset `32` explicitly, not assumed.
> 6. **Not drawing the memory addresses** in the ladder diagram — KTU requires `MW`, `MD`, `IW`, `Q` prefixes clearly visible.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **PLC arithmetic functions are IEC 61131-3 standard** instructions: `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `MOD`, `SQRT`, `ABS`, `NEG`, `INC`, `DEC`.
- ✅ **Three execution stages:** Operand Fetch → ALU/FPU Operation → Store & Flag Update.
- ✅ **Data-type suffixes** (`_I`, `_DI`, `_R`) are **mandatory** in Siemens-style ladder; data types are **tag-defined** in Allen-Bradley.
- ✅ **INT range is ±32,767** — exceeding this in `ADD_I` triggers **OV flag** and silent wrap-around.
- ✅ **DINT range is ±2,147,483,647** — use `ADD_DI` whenever totals may exceed 16-bit boundaries.
- ✅ **REAL uses IEEE 754 single-precision** with range ±3.4 × 10³⁸ and supports fractional values.
- ✅ **OV flag** is cleared by the next successful arithmetic instruction; **OS flag is sticky** until `CLR` or `FC42`.
- ✅ **Division by zero** yields `±INF` or `NaN` in REAL — always trap with a pre-divide check.
- ✅ **The standard scaling equation** is:
$$Y = \left(\frac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}\right)(Y_{\max} - Y_{\min}) + Y_{\min}$$
- ✅ **Celsius-to-Fahrenheit conversion** uses `Y = X × 1.8 + 32`.
- ✅ **MOD** returns the remainder of integer division — critical for **rotary-indexing** and **conveyor-station-selection** logic.
- ✅ **Square-root blocks** are used to linearize **orifice-plate flowmeters** per Bernoulli's principle.
- ✅ **In ladder diagrams, always show** the full tag names (e.g., `MW10`, `MD20`, `IW64`, `Q0.0`) — never use generic `IN1`, `OUT1` labels in KTU answers.
- ✅ **Accumulator depth = 2** — for multi-operand sums, use **binary-tree addition** to avoid ACCU overflow.
- ✅ **REAL-to-INT conversion requires explicit `ROUND`, `TRUNC`, or `CEIL` instructions** — implicit conversion is a compiler error in STRICT IEC 61131-3 mode.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Master Control Reset - Skip - Jump and Move Instruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT612 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Master Control Reset - Skip - Jump and Move Instruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Master Control Reset (MCR) Instruction

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Master Control Reset (MCR)** is a programmable controller instruction used in ladder logic programming to **disable (turn OFF) a defined zone of outputs** within a rung program when a specific condition is met. It creates a "fenced zone" demarcated by an MCR start condition and an MCR end condition, enabling or disabling the execution of all non-retentive outputs within that zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** MCR is classified as a **conditional program control instruction** in the IEC 61131-3 standard and is widely implemented in Allen-Bradley RSLogix 500/5000, Siemens TIA Portal, and Schneider Modicon platforms.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine an **emergency stop button in a chemical plant**. When pressed, you don't want to shut down the entire factory — just a specific process line (e.g., the mixing tank and its heaters). The MCR instruction acts exactly like this: it creates a **virtual boundary** around a section of logic. When activated, anything inside that boundary (non-retentive outputs) is forced OFF, while the rest of the program continues to run unaffected.

Think of MCR like a **floodgate in a canal system**:
- **MCR rung (false/active)** → Gate OPEN → Water (logic execution) flows normally.
- **MCR rung (true/active reset)** → Gate CLOSED → Water (outputs) is blocked/reset.

### Standard Metrics and Boundaries
- **MCR Zone** = A defined ladder segment between MCR start and MCR end instructions.
- **Retention Rule** = Only **retentive outputs** (e.g., latched coils `L`/`U` or retentive timers) survive an MCR condition. **Non-retentive outputs** (`O`/`OTE`) are forced OFF.
- **Boundary Length** = Typically spans **multiple rungs** but must be enclosed properly to avoid syntax errors.

---

## 1.2 Skip Instruction

### Formal Definition
The **Skip (SKP)** instruction, when its input condition is TRUE, causes the processor to **bypass the remaining instructions in the current scan cycle** and advance to the next scan cycle's start, effectively ignoring subsequent rungs until the end of the program file.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Skip is platform-specific — primarily found in **Allen-Bradley PLC-5 and SLC 500** controllers using RSLogix 5/500. In modern ControlLogix platforms, this functionality is replicated using the **JMP** (Jump) instruction with a corresponding label.

### Conceptual Analogy
Consider a **library self-checkout machine**: If a book has a damaged barcode, the machine *skips* that book and moves directly to the next transaction. The rest of the checkout process resumes only when a fresh scan begins. Skip works identically — once triggered, the controller ignores the remaining logic of the current scan and waits for the next cycle.

---

## 1.3 Jump Instruction (JMP) and Label (LBL)

### Formal Definition
The **Jump (JMP)** instruction, paired with a **Label (LBL)** instruction, causes the processor to **bypass a defined section of the ladder program** when the JMP condition is TRUE, transferring program execution control to the specified label rung. Unlike Skip, Jump is **targeted** — it knows exactly where to resume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Jump instructions are essential for **scan time optimization**, **fault isolation routines**, and implementing **conditional subroutines** without using physical subroutine calls.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **book's table of contents with chapters**: When you "jump" to Chapter 5, you completely skip Chapters 2, 3, and 4. The reader does not execute those skipped chapters — they directly land on Chapter 5 (the label). Similarly, the PLC skips over a block of rungs and resumes processing at the label location.

---

## 1.4 Move Instruction (MOV)

### Formal Definition
The **Move (MOV)** instruction copies the contents of a **source word/register** into a **destination word/register** without altering the source. It performs a direct data transfer and is the most fundamental **data manipulation instruction** in PLC programming.

> [!NOTE]
> **Variants include:**
> - **MOV** — Standard word-level move.
> - **MVM** — Masked Move (moves only specific bits defined by a mask).
> - **BTD** — Bit Field Distribute (used in SLC 500).
> - **MOVB** — Move Block.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a **photocopier machine**: You place an original document (source) on the glass, press copy, and an identical copy appears (destination). The original is untouched. The Move instruction behaves identically — it duplicates the bit pattern from one memory location to another without erasing the original.

---

## 1.5 Master Control Reset — Skip — Jump — Move: High-Level Comparison

| Instruction | Function | Scope | Common Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MCR** | Disables outputs in a zone | Fenced zone | Emergency stop, fault zones |
| **SKP** | Bypasses rest of scan | End of program | Conditional scan skipping |
| **JMP** | Jumps to a label | Targeted (LBL) | Scan time optimization |
| **MOV** | Copies data between registers | Word/Register level | Data transfer, scaling |

---

## 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Typical Scan Time Reduction** by Jump: **20%–40%** in large programs.
- **Default Word Size** for MOV: **16 bits** (single word); double-word = 32 bits.
- **MCR Zone Limit**: No theoretical limit, but recommended ≤ **50 rungs** per zone for readability.
- **Standard Register Size**: **1 word = 16 bits = 2 bytes**.

---

## 1.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These four instructions form the **backbone of structured PLC programming**. MCR is critical in **safety instrumented systems (SIS)** following IEC 61508. JMP is used in **high-speed packaging machinery** to optimize scan times. MOV is fundamental to **SCADA/HMI data transfers** and **recipe management** in process industries.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Control Reset (MCR) — Operational Logic

### Step-by-Step Operating Principle

1. **Definition of MCR Rung**: An MCR zone begins when an output coil labeled `MCR` appears in a rung. The MCR instruction itself is treated as a **conditional output relay**.

2. **Evaluation of the MCR Condition**:
   - If the input logic preceding the MCR coil is **TRUE** → MCR is **enabled (active)** → outputs within the zone are **controlled normally**.
   - If the input logic is **FALSE** → MCR is **reset (inactive)** → outputs within the zone are **forced OFF**.

3. **Inside the MCR Zone**:
   - **Non-retentive outputs** (OTE — Output Energize) → Forced OFF when MCR is inactive.
   - **Retentive outputs** (OTL — Output Latch, OTU — Output Unlatch) → Retain their last state.
   - **Timers** → Reset to zero (non-retentive TON).
   - **Counters** → Not affected if retentive (CTU/CTD with retentive attribute).

4. **Termination of the Zone**: An `MCR` instruction used as an input contact (or a dedicated `END MCR` coil) marks the **end of the MCR zone**.

### Why MCR Works This Way
The MCR logic uses a **master relay simulation**. The processor virtually "de-energizes" the master relay, which in turn de-energizes all downstream outputs. This mimics the behavior of an electromechanical master control relay in pre-PLC relay logic panels.

### Implementation Across Platforms
- **Allen-Bradley SLC 500**: Use a regular output coil named `MCR`.
- **Allen-Bradley ControlLogix**: Uses explicit MCR function block or "Zone" instruction.
- **Siemens S7**: Uses `---( MCR< )` and `---( MCRA )` / `---( MCRD )` instructions.

---

## 2.2 Skip Instruction — Operational Logic

### Step-by-Step Operating Principle

1. **Detection**: When the input condition to the SKP instruction is TRUE.
2. **Action**: The processor immediately terminates further ladder execution for the **current scan**.
3. **Resume Point**: Execution restarts at the **first rung** of the program file in the **next scan**.
4. **Use Case**: SKP is primarily used in time-critical applications where partial scan execution must be terminated early.

### Why Skip Is Rare in Modern Systems
Modern scan times are fast enough (<10 ms typical) that skipping is rarely necessary. JMP provides more **control granularity** and is therefore preferred.

---

## 2.3 Jump (JMP) and Label (LBL) — Operational Logic

### Step-by-Step Operating Principle

1. **Placement of LBL**: A Label instruction is placed at the rung where execution should **resume** if the JMP is triggered.

2. **Placement of JMP**: The Jump instruction is placed at the rung containing the **jump condition**. The JMP references the same label number as the LBL.

3. **Execution Flow**:
   - If JMP condition is **FALSE** → Processor executes rungs **linearly** (normal flow).
   - If JMP condition is **TRUE** → Processor **skips all rungs** between JMP and the matching LBL, resuming execution at the LBL rung.

4. **Multiple JMPs**: Multiple JMP instructions can reference the **same label** (fan-in).

5. **Nesting**: Jump zones **cannot be nested** within other jump zones in most platforms (a syntax/program error).

### Why Jump Optimizes Scan Time
By bypassing non-critical logic (e.g., a slow heater control loop that runs only once every 5 seconds), the processor reduces the **effective scan time** of the routine, allowing faster response to critical I/O updates.

### Critical Pitfall
> [!WARNING]
> **Always ensure the LBL is placed AFTER the JMP**, never before. A forward jump (LBL preceding JMP) is a **logic error** and may produce undefined behavior.

---

## 2.4 Move (MOV) — Operational Logic

### Step-by-Step Operating Principle

1. **Source Identification**: The MOV instruction specifies a **source address** (e.g., `N7:0`, an analog input register, or a constant).

2. **Destination Identification**: It also specifies a **destination address** (e.g., `N7:10`, a timer preset, or an output word).

3. **Trigger Condition**: When the rung input condition is TRUE, the processor copies the **entire 16-bit word** from source to destination in **one scan**.

4. **Source Preservation**: The source register is **not modified** — only the destination is overwritten.

5. **One-Shot Behavior**: If the rung remains TRUE, the move occurs **every scan** (continuous transfer). To execute once, use a **One-Shot Rising (OSR)** or an **ONS** instruction.

### Mathematical Representation

For a standard MOV instruction:

$$
D[t] = S[t]
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Destination word at scan time $t$
- $S$ = Source word at scan time $t$

### Masked Move (MVM)

$$
D[t] = (S[t] \text{ AND } M) \text{ OR } (D[t] \text{ AND } \overline{M})
$$

Where:
- $M$ = Mask word (16 bits)
- $\overline{M}$ = Inverted mask (complement)
- AND/OR = bitwise logical operations

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Boundary | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| MCR Output Force | $O_{non-ret} = 0$ when MCR FALSE | Boolean (0/1) | Retentive outputs unaffected |
| MCR Zone Limit | $\leq 50$ rungs | Rungs | Best practice readability |
| JMP Scan Time Savings | $\Delta t_{scan} = t_{skipped} \cdot N_{scans}$ | milliseconds (ms) | $t_{skipped}$ = skipped rung execution time |
| MOV Transfer | $D = S$ | 16-bit word | No source modification |
| MVM Transfer | $D = (S \text{ AND } M) \text{ OR } (D \text{ AND } \overline{M})$ | 16-bit word | Bitwise mask operation |
| SKP Scan Reduction | $T_{eff} = T_{scan} - T_{skipped}$ | ms | Only last partial scan affected |
| Memory Address Length | $1 \text{ Word} = 16 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ bytes}$ | bits/bytes | Standard PLC word size |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

### Industry Applications

- **MCR in Process Industries**: Used to implement **permissive interlocks** in refinery control (e.g., disabling reactor feed pumps if temperature exceeds safe limits).
- **JMP in High-Speed Packaging**: Skips slow HMI update routines to maintain **sub-millisecond servo loop response**.
- **MOV in Recipe Management**: Transfers recipe parameters (temperature, pressure, flow rates) from HMI-defined data files into process control registers.
- **SKP in Legacy Systems**: Found in **Allen-Bradley PLC-5** installations in automotive assembly lines.

### Why These Instructions Matter in the KTU Curriculum
> [!IMPORTANT]
> These instructions represent the **transition from basic I/O logic to structured programming** in PLCs. KTU examiners frequently test the **correct pairing of JMP/LBL**, **scope of MCR zones**, and **source/destination data types** in MOV — making them **high-yield topics** for university examinations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Truth Table for MCR Zone Behavior

| MCR Condition (Input) | Inside Zone Output Type | Output State | Mathematical Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|
| TRUE | Non-retentive (OTE) | Controlled normally | $O = f(X)$ |
| FALSE | Non-retentive (OTE) | Forced OFF | $O = 0$ |
| TRUE | Retentive (OTL/OTU) | Controlled normally | $O = f(X)$ |
| FALSE | Retentive (OTL/OTU) | Holds last state | $O = O_{prev}$ |
| TRUE | Non-retentive Timer (TON) | Counts normally | $T_{acc} = T_{acc} + \Delta t$ |
| FALSE | Non-retentive Timer (TON) | Reset to zero | $T_{acc} = 0$ |
| TRUE | Retentive Timer | Counts normally | $T_{acc} = T_{acc} + \Delta t$ |
| FALSE | Retentive Timer | Holds accumulated value | $T_{acc} = T_{prev}$ |

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Jump Scan Time Optimization

### Problem Statement
A PLC program contains a **critical fast loop** of 20 rungs and a **slow HMI update block** of 80 rungs. The processor must execute the fast loop within **5 ms**. Determine the scan time saving achieved by using a JMP instruction to bypass the HMI block.

### Given Parameters
- Total program rungs = $20 + 80 = 100$ rungs
- Average execution time per rung = $t_r = 0.05$ ms
- HMI update frequency = once every **5 scans** (only needs to run every 5th scan)
- Critical fast loop must run every scan

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate the full scan time without JMP.**

$$
T_{full} = N_{total} \cdot t_r
$$

Substituting values:

$$
T_{full} = 100 \times 0.05 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{full} = 5.0 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the scan time with JMP (only fast loop executes).**

$$
T_{JMP} = N_{fast} \cdot t_r
$$

Substituting values:

$$
T_{JMP} = 20 \times 0.05 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{JMP} = 1.0 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the scan time reduction percentage.**

$$
\Delta T\% = \frac{T_{full} - T_{JMP}}{T_{full}} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\Delta T\% = \frac{5.0 - 1.0}{5.0} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta T\% = \frac{4.0}{5.0} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta T\% = 0.8 \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta T\% = 80\%
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**
By using the JMP instruction to bypass the HMI block, the **effective scan time is reduced by 80%**, allowing the critical fast loop to execute in 1 ms instead of 5 ms — a **5× performance improvement**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of MOV Bit-Level Operation

### Problem Statement
A MOV instruction transfers data from source register `N7:0` to destination register `N7:10`. The source contains the binary value `1011 0100 1100 0010`. Determine the destination value and explain the bit-by-bit behavior.

### Given
- Source = `N7:0 = 1011 0100 1100 0010` (Binary)
- Destination = `N7:10` (initial value = `0000 0000 0000 0000`)
- Operation = MOV (standard move)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the bit positions (15 to 0).**

$$
\text{Bit positions: } b_{15}, b_{14}, b_{13}, \ldots, b_1, b_0
$$

**Step 2: Assign source bits.**

$$
b_{15} = 1, \quad b_{14} = 0, \quad b_{13} = 1, \quad b_{12} = 1
$$

$$
b_{11} = 0, \quad b_{10} = 1, \quad b_9 = 0, \quad b_8 = 0
$$

$$
b_7 = 1, \quad b_6 = 1, \quad b_5 = 0, \quad b_4 = 0
$$

$$
b_3 = 0, \quad b_2 = 0, \quad b_1 = 1, \quad b_0 = 0
$$

**Step 3: Apply the MOV operation.**

$$
D_i = S_i \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 15
$$

**Step 4: Compute destination value.**

$$
D = 1011\,0100\,1100\,0010
$$

**Step 5: Confirm source is unchanged.**

$$
S_{after} = S_{before} = 1011\,0100\,1100\,0010
$$

**Step 6: Verification using decimal.**

$$
S_{decimal} = 1 \cdot 2^{15} + 0 \cdot 2^{14} + 1 \cdot 2^{13} + 1 \cdot 2^{12} + 0 \cdot 2^{11} + 1 \cdot 2^{10} + 0 \cdot 2^9 + 0 \cdot 2^8
$$

$$
+ 1 \cdot 2^7 + 1 \cdot 2^6 + 0 \cdot 2^5 + 0 \cdot 2^4 + 0 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0
$$

$$
S_{decimal} = 32768 + 8192 + 4096 + 1024 + 128 + 64 + 2 = 46274
$$

Therefore, $N7:10 = 46274$ (decimal) after MOV execution.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Masked Move (MVM) Bit Selection

### Problem Statement
Use MVM to copy only **bits 0, 4, and 8** from source `N7:5` to destination `N7:20`. The mask must isolate these three bits.

### Given
- Source `N7:5` = `1101 0110 1010 1101`
- Destination `N7:20` = `0000 0000 0000 0000`
- Required bits to copy: $b_0$, $b_4$, $b_8$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Construct the mask $M$.**

Set bits 0, 4, and 8 to 1; all others to 0:

$$
M = 0000\,0001\,0001\,0001
$$

**Step 2: Compute $S$ AND $M$.**

$$
S \text{ AND } M = (1101\,0110\,1010\,1101) \text{ AND } (0000\,0001\,0001\,0001)
$$

Bit-by-bit AND:

$$
S \text{ AND } M = 0000\,0000\,0000\,1101
$$

(Only bits 0, 2, 3 from source are preserved — but in source, only $b_0 = 1$, $b_2 = 1$, $b_3 = 1$.)

Wait — let us correct the source mapping. Source bits:

$$
b_{15}=1,\, b_{14}=1,\, b_{13}=0,\, b_{12}=1,\, b_{11}=0,\, b_{10}=1,\, b_9=1,\, b_8=0
$$

$$
b_7=1,\, b_6=0,\, b_5=1,\, b_4=0,\, b_3=1,\, b_2=1,\, b_1=0,\, b_0=1
$$

Recompute $S$ AND $M$:

$$
S \text{ AND } M = 0000\,0001\,0001\,0001
$$

(Only $b_0 = 1$ survives; $b_4 = 0$ and $b_8 = 0$ contribute 0.)

**Step 3: Compute $D$ AND $\overline{M}$.**

$$
\overline{M} = 1111\,1110\,1110\,1110
$$

$$
D \text{ AND } \overline{M} = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000
$$

**Step 4: Compute final destination.**

$$
D_{new} = (S \text{ AND } M) \text{ OR } (D_{old} \text{ AND } \overline{M})
$$

$$
D_{new} = 0000\,0001\,0001\,0001 \text{ OR } 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000
$$

$$
D_{new} = 0000\,0001\,0001\,0001
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**
The destination now contains only the bits that were selected by the mask. All other destination bits are preserved as their original values (0 in this case).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: JMP vs. SKP vs. MCR Comparison

| Parameter | JMP | SKP | MCR |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Targeted bypass?** | Yes (uses LBL) | No (end of scan) | Yes (uses zone boundary) |
| **Nesting allowed?** | No | N/A | Limited (sequential zones only) |
| **Output forcing?** | No (skipped, not reset) | No | Yes (forces non-retentive OFF) |
| **Scan time effect** | Reduces per-scan time | Truncates current scan | No effect (still executed) |
| **Data integrity** | Skipped rungs untouched | Skipped rungs untouched | Non-retentive outputs reset |

---

## 3.6 Detailed Logic Flow for MCR Implementation

### Step-by-Step Logic Sequence

1. **Define MCR Condition**: Identify the safety/permissive signal (e.g., Emergency Stop = `I:1/5`).
2. **Construct MCR Rung**: Place the MCR coil in a rung energized by the condition.
3. **Define Zone**: Enclose all dependent outputs between MCR start and end.
4. **Test Outputs**: Verify that only retentive outputs (latches, retentive timers) survive the MCR OFF condition.
5. **Validate Fault Behavior**: Simulate fault scenarios to confirm safe shutdown of all MCR-controlled equipment.

### Worked Example

Consider a conveyor system with:
- Motor (non-retentive, OTE)
- Heater (retentive, OTL — must stay on if MCR trips)
- Indicator lamp (non-retentive, OTE)

If MCR input `I:1/5` = FALSE:
- Motor → OFF (forced)
- Heater → stays ON (retentive latch)
- Lamp → OFF (forced)

This is a typical safety pattern where critical heating elements are latched ON to prevent material solidification during emergency shutdown.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
