> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMRT612 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** PLC Analog Signal Processing - Case Study of Tank Level Control System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of PLC Analog Signal Processing > [!IMPORTANT] > **PLC Analog Signal Processing** is the methodology by which a Programmable Logic Controller receives, conditions, digitizes, scales, and mathematically manipulates **continuously variable (analog) electrical signals** from field sensors — and conversely, generates continuously variable analog output signals to drive final control elements such as variable-speed drives, control valves, and proportional actuators. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, analog signal processing bridges the gap between the **physical world (continuous in time and amplitude)** and the **binary, discrete computational domain of the PLC CPU**. The principal hardware components that enable this bridge are: | Component | Function | Standard Form | |-----------|----------|---------------| | **Analog Input Module (AI)** | Receives field signals, performs A/D conversion | 4–20 mA, 0–10 V, ±10 V, RTD, Thermocouple | | **Analog Output Module (AO)** | Receives digital word from CPU, performs D/A conversion | 4–20 mA, 0–10 V | | **Transducer/Sensor** | Converts physical variable to electrical signal | Level, Pressure, Temperature, Flow | | **Signal Conditioner** | Filters, amplifies, linearizes raw transducer output | Isolation, amplification, cold-junction compensation | | **Final Control Element** | Receives analog command from PLC | VFD, Control Valve, Variable Pump | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bathroom Shower Imagine a standard bathroom shower. You have **two valves** — one for hot water, one for cold. You do not simply turn them ON or OFF (which would be digital, like a switch). You continuously rotate them to fine-tune the temperature and flow rate. - The **temperature knob position** is a continuous variable — this is your **analog input**. - The **water temperature at the showerhead** is a continuous output that must be regulated — this is the **process variable (PV)**. - Your **hand and brain** form a closed-loop controller — adjusting the knob based on whether the water is too hot or too cold. This is exactly what a **PID controller inside the PLC** does for the tank level. - The **mixing valve** is the **final control element (analog output)** that throttles continuously between 0 % and 100 %. > [!NOTE] > In PLC analog processing, the **input (sensor reading) is continuous**, but the **PLC's internal CPU processes it in discrete digital numbers**. The word "analog" therefore refers to the *electrical nature of the I/O signal*, not the internal CPU computation. ## 1.3 Case Study Introduction — The Tank Level Control System A cylindrical process tank is used in a chemical mixing plant. The objective is to maintain the **liquid level** inside the tank at a desired **setpoint (SP)** despite variations in: - **Inflow** (raw material feed pump) - **Outflow** (drain valve to next stage) - **Disturbances** (evaporation, leaks, operator action) | Parameter | Value (Typical) | Unit | |-----------|----------------|------| | Tank height | 5 | **meters (m)** | | Tank diameter | 2 | **meters (m)** | | Setpoint level | 3.5 | **m** | | Level sensor range | 0 – 5 | **m** | | Sensor output | 4 – 20 | **mA (milliampere)** | | Control valve | Pneumatic, 4 – 20 mA | — | | Inflow pump (VFD) | 0 – 50 Hz, 4 – 20 mA command | — | The PLC must: 1. **Read** the level sensor (analog input). 2. **Compare** with the setpoint (math operation). 3. **Compute** an error-driven control signal (PID algorithm). 4. **Output** a continuously variable signal to the inlet control valve (analog output). ## 1.4 Why Analog (and not just Digital)? > [!IMPORTANT] > A digital (on/off) level control would simply open or close the inlet valve. This causes **oscillation, overshoot, and hunting** — the level would swing wildly above and below the setpoint. Continuous (analog) control allows **smooth, proportional, and stable** regulation, which is essential in process industries (chemical, water treatment, oil & gas, food, pharma). ## 1.5 Key Terminology Glossary (Mandatory for KTU Answers) | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Transducer** | Device that converts a physical quantity into an electrical signal | | **Sensor** | A transducer that specifically *senses* a physical variable | | **Transmitter** | A transducer with built-in signal conditioning, producing a **standardized output** (4–20 mA) | | **Resolution** | Smallest change in input signal that the ADC can detect, expressed in **bits** | | **Span** | The difference between the maximum and minimum of the measured range (e.g., 5 m − 0 m = 5 m) | | **Zero** | The lower limit of the range (0 m in our case study) | | **Engineering Units (EU)** | The real-world physical units (meters, °C, bar, L/min) used in the control logic | | **Raw Counts** | The integer value (0 to 2ⁿ − 1) output by the ADC | | **Scaling** | Conversion between raw counts and engineering units | | **Normalize** | Conversion of raw counts into a percentage (0 % to 100 %) | | **Setpoint (SP)** | Desired value of the process variable | | **Process Variable (PV)** | Measured, instantaneous value of the controlled variable | | **Manipulated Variable (MV)** | Output of the controller sent to the final control element | | **Error (e)** | SP − PV (or PV − SP, depending on controller action) | | **Closed Loop** | Control system where the PV is fed back to the controller | | **Open Loop** | Control system with no feedback — output is not corrected for disturbances | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Standard Industrial Analog Signal Standards > [!NOTE] > **4–20 mA current loop** is the **dominant industrial standard** because current signals are **immune to voltage drops over long cable runs** and **resistant to electrical noise**. The "live zero" at 4 mA also enables **open-circuit / cable-break fault detection** — a true 0 mA indicates a broken wire. | Standard | Range | Application | |----------|-------|-------------| | **4 – 20 mA** | Current loop | Industry standard, fault-detectable | | **0 – 20 mA** | Current loop | Legacy, no fault detection | | **0 – 10 V** | Voltage | Short-distance, laboratory | | **±10 V** | Bipolar voltage | Motion control, drives | | **1 – 5 V** | Voltage | Derived from 4–20 mA across 250 Ω resistor | ## 2.2 Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) Fundamentals The ADC inside the analog input module samples the continuous input signal and produces a **discrete integer** between 0 and (2ⁿ − 1), where **n** is the resolution in bits. **Quantization Step Size (Resolution in Engineering Units):** For an ADC with *n* bits and a full-scale input range of (Vmax − Vmin): $$Q = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^{n} - 1}$$ For our case study — a 12-bit ADC (n = 12) reading a 4–20 mA signal across a 250 Ω resistor (yielding 1–5 V): $$Q = \frac{5\text{ V} - 1\text{ V}}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{4\text{ V}}{4095} \approx 0.977 \text{ mV per count}$$ ## 2.3 Scaling Equations — The Heart of Analog Processing > [!IMPORTANT] > Every KTU question involving analog I/O will require you to apply the **linear scaling equation**. Memorize the two forms below — they are interchangeable. ### Form 1 — Raw Counts to Engineering Units $$EU = \left(\frac{Raw - Raw_{min}}{Raw_{max} - Raw_{min}}\right) \times (EU_{max} - EU_{min}) + EU_{min}$$ ### Form 2 — Current/Voltage to Engineering Units $$EU = \left(\frac{I - I_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}}\right) \times (EU_{max} - EU_{min}) + EU_{min}$$ ### Inverse — Engineering Units to Raw Counts (for analog output) $$Raw = \left(\frac{EU - EU_{min}}{EU_{max} - EU_{min}}\right) \times (Raw_{max} - Raw_{min}) + Raw_{min}$$ ### Case Study Numerical Example Given: Level sensor with 4–20 mA output, range 0–5 m, ADC produces raw count of 20475 for a 12-bit module. Wait — for a 12-bit module, raw range is 0 to 4095. The value 20475 is for a 16-bit module. Let us assume a **16-bit ADC** (n = 16, range 0–65535): $$EU = \left(\frac{20475 - 0}{65535 - 0}\right) \times (5 - 0) + 0$$ $$EU = 0.3125 \times 5 = 1.5625 \text{ m}$$ So the level in the tank is **1.5625 meters** at that instant. ## 2.4 The PID Controller — Mathematical Core > [!IMPORTANT] > For a tank level control case study, the PLC's PID instruction computes the **Manipulated Variable (MV)** based on the **Proportional + Integral + Derivative** action on the error signal. KTU regularly asks for the **PID equation** and the **effect of each term**. The **ideal (non-interacting) PID equation** in continuous form is: $$u(t) = K_{c}\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{T_{i}}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)d\tau + T_{d}\frac{de(t)}{dt}\right]$$ Where: - $u(t)$ = Controller output (manipulated variable) - $K_{c}$ = Proportional gain (dimensionless) - $T_{i}$ = Integral time (seconds, also called *reset time*) - $T_{d}$ = Derivative time (seconds, also called *rate time*) - $e(t) = SP - PV$ = Error signal The **discrete (PLC-implementable) form** using the velocity (incremental) algorithm is: $$\Delta u_{n} = K_{c}\left[\left(e_{n} - e_{n-1}\right) + \frac{T_{s}}{T_{i}}e_{n} + \frac{T_{d}}{T_{s}}\left(e_{n} - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2}\right)\right]$$ $$u_{n} = u_{n-1} + \Delta u_{n}$$ Where $T_{s}$ is the **scan period (loop update time)** in seconds. ### Effect of Each Term | Term | Action | Effect on System | Too High → Consequence | |------|--------|------------------|------------------------| | **P (Proportional)** | Output ∝ instantaneous error | Reduces rise time, increases speed | Oscillation, instability | | **I (Integral)** | Output ∝ accumulated past error | Eliminates steady-state (offset) error | Overshoot, slow response | | **D (Derivative)** | Output ∥ rate of change of error | Anticipates future error, adds damping | Noise amplification, chattering | ## 2.5 Open-Loop Transfer Function of a Tank A simple cylindrical tank with a single outflow obeys a **first-order differential equation**. Let: - $A$ = Cross-sectional area of tank (m²) - $H$ = Liquid level (m) - $Q_{in}$ = Inflow rate (m³/s) - $Q_{out}$ = Outflow rate (m³/s) Mass (volume) balance: $$A\frac{dH}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$$ For a linearized outflow $Q_{out} = \frac{H}{R}$ (where $R$ is the *flow resistance* in s/m²): $$A\frac{dH}{dt} + \frac{H}{R} = Q_{in}$$ Taking the Laplace transform (with zero initial condition): $$A \cdot s \cdot H(s) + \frac{H(s)}{R} = Q_{in}(s)$$ $$\frac{H(s)}{Q_{in}(s)} = \frac{R}{ARs + 1}$$ This is a **first-order lag** with: - **Time constant** $\tau = AR$ (seconds) - **Process gain** $K_{p} = R$ (m per m³/s) - **DC gain** (steady-state level for unit step inflow) = $R$ ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}-1}$ | ADC quantization step (resolution) | V/count or mA/count | | 2 | $EU = \left(\dfrac{Raw - Raw_{lo}}{Raw_{hi} - Raw_{lo}}\right) \cdot Span + EU_{lo}$ | Linear scaling, raw → engineering | EU | | 3 | $EU_{span} = EU_{max} - EU_{min}$ | Engineering span | EU | | 4 | $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{EU - EU_{min}}{EU_{max} - EU_{min}}$ | EU → current (4–20 mA) | mA | | 5 | $u(t) = K_{c}\left[e + \dfrac{1}{T_{i}}\int e\,dt + T_{d}\dfrac{de}{dt}\right]$ | Ideal continuous PID | % or mA | | 6 | $H(s) / Q_{in}(s) = R / (ARs + 1)$ | Tank first-order TF | m per m³/s | | 7 | $\tau_{process} = A \cdot R$ | Tank process time constant | seconds | | 8 | $K_{u} = 4d / (\pi \cdot a)$ | Ultimate gain (Ziegler-Nichols closed-loop) | — | | 9 | $P_{u} \approx 0.8 \tau_{process}$ | Ultimate period estimate (ZN step test) | seconds | | 10 | $Hysteresis = \dfrac{EU_{max} - EU_{min}}{2^{n}-1} \times 100\%$ | % of full scale | % | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application of PLC Analog Level Control | |----------|------------------------------------------| | **Water Treatment** | Clearwell level control using PID on chlorination + filtration rate | | **Oil & Gas** | Knockout drum level control to prevent liquid carryover to compressor | | **Pharmaceutical** | Reactor vessel level for batch process repeatability | | **Food & Beverage** | Mixing tank level for recipe-based batching | | **Power Plant** | Deaerator level control — critical to prevent boiler tube damage | | **Chemical** | Neutralization tank pH/level cascade control | > [!NOTE] > The tank level control case study is a **canonical KTU problem** because it touches every aspect of analog signal processing: **sensor interfacing, signal conditioning, scaling, PID algorithm, and analog output to final control element**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Full Scaling Equation from First Principles We wish to derive the relationship between the **raw ADC count** $N$ and the **engineering unit value** $EU$ (level in meters). ### Step 1: Define the endpoints The sensor spans 0 m to 5 m. The corresponding raw counts are: - $N_{lo} = 0$ (when level = 0 m, sensor outputs 4 mA → 1 V → 0 counts) - $N_{hi} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65535$ (when level = 5 m, sensor outputs 20 mA → 5 V → 65535 counts) - $EU_{lo} = 0$ m - $EU_{hi} = 5$ m ### Step 2: Establish linear proportionality We assume the sensor and ADC chain are **linear**, which is true for the working range. Thus: $$\frac{EU - EU_{lo}}{EU_{hi} - EU_{lo}} = \frac{N - N_{lo}}{N_{hi} - N_{lo}}$$ This is a "similar triangles" relationship on the transfer characteristic curve. ### Step 3: Solve for $EU$ Cross-multiplying: $$(EU - EU_{lo})(N_{hi} - N_{lo}) = (EU_{hi} - EU_{lo})(N - N_{lo})$$ Dividing by $(N_{hi} - N_{lo})$: $$EU - EU_{lo} = \frac{(EU_{hi} - EU_{lo})(N - N_{lo})}{N_{hi} - N_{lo}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{EU = \left(\frac{N - N_{lo}}{N_{hi} - N_{lo}}\right) \cdot (EU_{hi} - EU_{lo}) + EU_{lo}}$$ ### Step 4: Numerical instantiation $$EU = \left(\frac{N - 0}{65535 - 0}\right) \cdot (5 - 0) + 0$$ $$EU = \frac{5N}{65535}$$ For $N = 32768$ (mid-scale): $$EU = \frac{5 \times 32768}{65535} = \frac{163840}{65535} \approx 2.50019 \text{ m}$$ This corresponds to half the full-scale level, confirming the linearity. The 0.00019 m deviation is the **quantization error** (≤ ½ LSB). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resolution in Engineering Units ### Step 1: Start with quantization step in voltage $$Q_{V} = \frac{V_{hi} - V_{lo}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{5 - 1}{4095} = \frac{4}{4095} = 0.000977 \text{ V/count}$$ ### Step 2: Convert to current Since $V = I \times R_{shunt}$ and $R_{shunt} = 250\ \Omega$: $$Q_{I} = \frac{Q_{V}}{R_{shunt}} = \frac{0.000977}{250} = 3.906 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} = 3.906\ \mu\text{A/count}$$ ### Step 3: Convert to engineering units (meters) $$Q_{EU} = Q_{I} \times \frac{EU_{span}}{I_{span}} = 3.906 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{5\text{ m}}{16 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A}}$$ $$Q_{EU} = 3.906 \times 10^{-6} \times 312.5 = 1.221 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.221 \text{ mm}$$ Thus, the PLC can resolve level changes as small as **1.221 mm**. In a 5 m tank, this represents: $$\frac{1.221 \times 10^{-3}}{5} \times 100\% = 0.0244\% \text{ of full scale}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse Scaling for Analog Output When the PID controller outputs a manipulated variable (e.g., 60 % of valve opening), we must convert this to a raw count and then to a 4–20 mA signal to drive the control valve. ### Step 1: Percentage to engineering units (if needed) For valve opening, $EU_{lo} = 0\ \%$ and $EU_{hi} = 100\ \%$: $$EU = MV = 60\ \%$$ ### Step 2: Engineering units to raw count $$N = \left(\frac{EU - EU_{lo}}{EU_{hi} - EU_{lo}}\right) \times (N_{hi} - N_{lo}) + N_{lo}$$ $$N = \left(\frac{60 - 0}{100 - 0}\right) \times 65535 = 0.6 \times 65535 = 39321$$ ### Step 3: Raw count to current $$I = \left(\frac{N - N_{lo}}{N_{hi} - N_{lo}}\right) \times (I_{hi} - I_{lo}) + I_{lo}$$ $$I = \left(\frac{39321}{65535}\right) \times 16 + 4 = 0.6 \times 16 + 4 = 13.6 \text{ mA}$$ The control valve receives **13.6 mA**, opening it 60 %. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — PID Tuning via Ziegler-Nichols Step Response Method For the tank transfer function $\dfrac{H(s)}{Q_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{R}{ARs + 1}$, with parameters $A = \pi (1)^2 = \pi\ \text{m}^2$ and $R = 2\ \text{s/m}^2$: ### Step 1: Determine the process reaction curve parameters Apply a step change $\Delta Q_{in} = 0.5\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ and observe the level response. The maximum slope of the response is: $$R_{max} = \frac{\Delta Q_{in}}{A} = \frac{0.5}{\pi} = 0.159\ \text{m/s}$$ ### Step 2: Determine apparent dead time $L$ and process gain $K_{p}$ For a pure first-order system (no dead time), $L \approx 0$. The process gain is: $$K_{p} = \frac{\Delta H_{ss}}{\Delta Q_{in}} = R = 2\ \text{m per m}^3/\text{s}$$ ### Step 3: Compute Ziegler-Nichols PID parameters (using the open-loop method) The Z-N open-loop tuning formulas are: $$K_{c} = \frac{1.2 \cdot \tau}{K_{p} \cdot L}$$ For a tank with negligible dead time, we use the modified approach (Cohen-Coon or direct synthesis). Taking a practical engineering approximation: $$K_{c} = \frac{\tau}{K_{p} \cdot L_{eff}}$$ With $L_{eff} = 0.5\ \text{s}$ (sensor/valve transport delay), $\tau = AR = \pi \times 2 = 6.283\ \text{s}$: $$K_{c} = \frac{6.283}{2 \times 0.5} = 6.283$$ $$T_{i} = 2 L_{eff} = 1.0\ \text{s}$$ $$T_{d} = 0.5 L_{eff} = 0.25\ \text{s}$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cause–Effect of Tuning Changes | Symptom Observed in Level Response | Probable Cause | Recommended Action | |-----------------------------------|----------------|--------------------| | Steady-state offset (level stuck below SP) | Integral time $T_{i}$ too long or P-only control | **Decrease** $T_{i}$ (more integral action) | | Slow rise time to setpoint | $K_{c}$ too low | **Increase** $K_{c}$ | | Large overshoot above setpoint | Excessive integral or $K_{c}$ | **Decrease** $K_{c}$ or **increase** $T_{i}$ | | Oscillation / hunting around SP | $K_{c}$ too high, $T_{d}$ too low | **Decrease** $K_{c}$, **increase** $T_{d}$ | | Noisy, jittery valve movement | $T_{d}$ amplifies measurement noise | **Decrease** $T_{d}$ or add **input filter** | | Sluggish response to disturbances | Insufficient derivative or P | **Increase** $K_{c}$, add derivative | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Analog Signal Processing Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical Variable
Liquid Level H] --> B[Level Sensor
Hydrostatic / Ultrasonic] B --> C[Transmitter
4 to 20 mA] C --> D[Signal Conditioner
Filter and Amplify] D --> E[Analog Input Module
ADC 16 bit] E --> F[PLC CPU
Scaling and PID] F --> G[Analog Output Module
DAC 12 bit] G --> H[Control Valve
Pneumatic 4 to 20 mA] H --> I[Process
Tank Inflow] I -.feedback loop.-> A F --> J[HMI Display
Level in meters] F --> K[Data Acquisition
Trending and Logging] ``` ## 4.2 PID Controller Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM1((+)) PV[Process Variable PV] --> SUM2((+)) SUM2 --> MINUS(( - )) MINUS --> E[Error e equals SP minus PV] E --> P[Proportional Kc times e] E --> I1[Integrator 1 over Ti times integral of e] E --> D1[Differentiator Td times de by dt] P --> SUM3((+)) I1 --> SUM3 D1 --> SUM3 SUM3 --> MV[Manipulated Variable u of t] MV --> LIMIT[Output Limiter 0 to 100 percent] LIMIT --> VALVE[Control Valve Position] VALVE --> PROC[Tank Process] PROC --> SENSOR[Level Sensor] SENSOR --> PV ``` ## 4.3 Ladder Logic Rung — Analog Level Scaling and Alarm ```mermaid flowchart TD RUNG1[Rung 1: Read AI Module] --> MOVE1[AIW0 → N7:0 16-bit integer] MOVE1 --> SCALE[SCL Instruction
N7:0 → Level in meters
Input: 0 to 32767
Scale: 0.0 to 5.0] SCALE --> STORE[Store result in F8:0] STORE --> RUNG2 RUNG2[Rung 2: High Level Alarm] --> GRT[GRT F8:0 4.5] GRT --> ALARM[Output O:0/0 High Level Alarm] STORE --> RUNG3 RUNG3[Rung 3: Low Level Alarm] --> LES[LES F8:0 0.5] LES --> ALARM2[Output O:0/1 Low Level Alarm] STORE --> RUNG4 RUNG4[Rung 4: PID Compute] --> PID[PID Instruction
PV = F8:0
SP = 3.5 m
Output to N7:10] PID --> AO[Write to Analog Output O:0.0] ``` ## 4.4 Signal Flow and Scaling Reference Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FIELD[Field Layer] TANK[Tank Level 0 to 5 m] SENSE[Sensor Output 4 to 20 mA] end subgraph IO[IO Layer] ADC[ADC Raw 0 to 65535] DAC[DAC Raw 0 to 32767] end subgraph CPU[CPU Layer] SCALED[Scaled Value 0.0 to 5.0 m] PID[PID Output 0 to 100 percent] end subgraph ACT[Actuator Layer] VALVE[Valve Current 4 to 20 mA] end TANK --> SENSE SENSE --> ADC ADC --> SCALED SCALED --> PID PID --> DAC DAC --> VALVE VALVE -. controls inflow .-> TANK ``` ## 4.5 Detailed Tank Control System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SENSING[Sensing Subsystem] LS1[Hydrostatic Level Sensor
0 to 5 m H2O] LT1[Level Transmitter
4 to 20 mA Loop Powered] end subgraph CTRL[Control Subsystem] AIM[Analog Input Module
16 channel 16 bit] PLC[PLC CPU
Rack Based] PID[PID Algorithm
Scan Time 100 ms] AOM[Analog Output Module
8 channel 12 bit] end subgraph ACTUATION[Actuation Subsystem] CV1[Control Valve
Pneumatic Air to Open] PMP1[Inflow Pump VFD
0 to 50 Hz] end LS1 --> LT1 LT1 -->|Two wire loop| AIM AIM -->|Process image table| PLC PLC --> PID PID -->|MV 0 to 100 percent| AOM AOM -->|4 to 20 mA| CV1 AOM -->|Backup speed ref| PMP1 CV1 -->|Modulates inflow| TANK2[Process Tank] PMP1 -->|Backup or parallel flow| TANK2 TANK2 -->|Hydrostatic pressure| LS1 PLC --> NET[Ethernet to SCADA] PLC --> HMI[Touchscreen HMI] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]** **Define analog signal processing in the context of a PLC. List any two standard industrial analog signal ranges.** **Model Answer:** > Analog signal processing in a PLC refers to the acquisition, conditioning, conversion (A/D), mathematical manipulation (scaling, filtering, PID), and regeneration (D/A) of **continuously variable electrical signals** that represent real-world physical quantities such as level, pressure, temperature, or flow. **Two standard industrial analog signal ranges:** 1. **Current loop: 4 to 20 mA** (industry standard, fault-detectable "live zero") 2. **Voltage: 0 to 10 V DC** (short-distance, laboratory/EU applications) > [!NOTE] > Other acceptable answers: 0 to 20 mA, 1 to 5 V, ±10 V, RTD (Pt100), Thermocouple (J, K, S type). --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]** **What is meant by "scaling" of an analog input? Why is it necessary?** **Model Answer:** Scaling is the mathematical conversion of a **raw integer count** produced by the PLC's Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) into a **meaningful engineering unit (EU)** such as meters, °C, bar, or L/min. It is necessary because: 1. The ADC produces only raw integers (e.g., 0 to 65535 for 16-bit). These numbers have no physical meaning to the operator or to the control algorithm. 2. The control logic, HMI display, alarms, and PID algorithm must all operate in real engineering units. 3. Scaling ensures **portability** — the same program works with any sensor as long as the scaling parameters are updated. 4. It provides **human-readable values** for display and trending on SCADA/HMI. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q3(a) and Q3(b)]** **Q3(a) [7 Marks]** — A hydrostatic level sensor is used in a cylindrical water tank of height 5 m. The sensor outputs 4 mA at 0 m level and 20 mA at 5 m level. The PLC analog input module has a 12-bit ADC and uses a 250 Ω shunt resistor. Calculate: - (i) The voltage across the shunt at 0 m and 5 m level. - (ii) The ADC raw count at 3.2 m level. - (iii) The smallest level change that the system can detect (resolution in mm). **Model Solution:** **(i) Voltage across shunt:** At 0 m, current = 4 mA: $$V_{0} = I \times R = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 = 1.0 \text{ V}$$ At 5 m, current = 20 mA: $$V_{5} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 = 5.0 \text{ V}$$ **(ii) ADC raw count at 3.2 m level:** First, find current at 3.2 m: $$I = 4 + 16 \times \frac{3.2 - 0}{5 - 0} = 4 + 16 \times 0.64 = 4 + 10.24 = 14.24 \text{ mA}$$ Voltage: $$V = 14.24 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 = 3.56 \text{ V}$$ Raw count for 12-bit ADC (range 0 to 4095): $$N = \frac{V - V_{lo}}{V_{hi} - V_{lo}} \times (N_{max} - N_{min}) + N_{min}$$ $$N = \frac{3.56 - 1.0}{5.0 - 1.0} \times (4095 - 0) + 0$$ $$N = \frac{2.56}{4.0} \times 4095 = 0.64 \times 4095 = 2620.8 \approx 2621$$ **(iii) Resolution in mm:** Quantization step in voltage: $$Q_{V} = \frac{4.0 \text{ V}}{4095} = 9.77 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/count}$$ Convert to current: $$Q_{I} = \frac{Q_{V}}{R_{shunt}} = \frac{9.77 \times 10^{-4}}{250} = 3.906 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/count} = 3.906\ \mu\text{A/count}$$ Convert to engineering units: $$Q_{EU} = Q_{I} \times \frac{EU_{span}}{I_{span}} = 3.906 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{5}{16 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$Q_{EU} = 3.906 \times 10^{-6} \times 312.5 = 1.221 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.221 \text{ mm}$$ **The smallest detectable level change is 1.221 mm.** --- **Q3(b) [7 Marks]** — Explain the function of a PID controller in a PLC-based tank level control system. Write the continuous-time PID equation and describe the effect of increasing each of the three tuning parameters ($K_{c}$, $T_{i}$, $T_{d}$) on the level response. **Model Solution:** In a PLC-based tank level control system, the **PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative) controller** is implemented as a software instruction in the CPU. Its function is to compute a **manipulated variable (MV)** — typically the valve opening position (0 to 100 %) — based on the **error** between the desired setpoint (SP) and the measured process variable (PV, the actual level). **Continuous-time PID equation:** $$u(t) = K_{c}\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{T_{i}}\int_{0}^{t}e(\tau)d\tau + T_{d}\frac{de(t)}{dt}\right]$$ **Effect of each tuning parameter:** | Parameter | Increase → | Decrease → | |-----------|-----------|-----------| | $K_{c}$ (Proportional gain) | Faster response, smaller steady-state error, but **more overshoot and oscillation**; if too high, system becomes **unstable** | Slower response, larger steady-state error, more stable but sluggish | | $T_{i}$ (Integral time, "reset time") | **Slower** integral action, less overshoot, but offset persists longer | **Faster** integral action, eliminates offset more quickly, but **increases overshoot**; too small → instability | | $T_{d}$ (Derivative time, "rate time") | **More damping**, reduced overshoot, anticipates future error; if too high → **amplifies measurement noise** → valve chattering | Less damping, more overshoot and oscillation, but smoother valve action | **Summary of physical intuition:** - **P** reacts to *how far* the level is from SP. - **I** reacts to *how long* the level has been away from SP (eliminates offset). - **D** reacts to *how fast* the level is approaching or leaving SP (adds damping). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:** > 1. Writing the PID equation in the **wrong order of operations** — the proportional, integral, and derivative terms must be **summed** after multiplication by $K_{c}$. Do not put $K_{c}$ only on the P term. > 2. Confusing **$T_{i}$ (reset time)** with **$1/T_{i}$ (reset rate)**. Industrial controllers like Honeywell and Yokogawa use $T_{i}$; some textbooks use $K_{i} = 1/T_{i}$. > 3. Forgetting that **derivative acts on the rate of change**, not the value itself. > 4. Saying "increase $T_{i}$ → more integral action" — this is **WRONG**. Larger $T_{i}$ means **slower** integral action (it takes longer to "reset"). > 5. Failing to mention that derivative action should be applied to the **PV**, not the error, to avoid **derivative kick** on setpoint changes. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative for Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q4(a) and Q4(b)]** **Q4(a) [7 Marks]** — With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the PLC-based closed-loop level control system of a tank. Identify the role of each block. **Model Solution:** ```mermaid flowchart LR SP[Setpoint Input
3.5 m] --> SUMMER((Comparator
Summing Junction)) PV[Level Sensor Feedback
Actual Level] --> SUMMER SUMMER --> ERROR[Error Signal e] ERROR --> PID[PID Controller
PLC CPU Block] PID --> AO[Analog Output Module
4 to 20 mA] AO --> VALVE[Control Valve
Final Control Element] VALVE --> TANK[Process Tank
Manipulated Variable Q_in] TANK --> SENSOR[Level Transmitter
Physical to Electrical] SENSOR --> PV ``` **Role of each block:** | Block | Function | |-------|----------| | **Setpoint** | The desired value of level (e.g., 3.5 m), entered via HMI or programmed as constant | | **Comparator (Summing Junction)** | Computes $e = SP - PV$ | | **PID Controller (PLC CPU)** | Executes the PID algorithm each scan to generate MV | | **Analog Output Module** | D/A converts the digital MV into 4–20 mA | | **Control Valve** | Throttles the inflow pipe — the physical actuator | | **Process Tank** | The plant — the actual level that must be controlled | | **Level Sensor + Transmitter** | Measures the level and outputs a 4–20 mA signal proportional to height | | **Feedback Path** | Closes the loop; without it, the system would be open-loop and unable to correct for disturbances | **Operating principle:** If the level drops below SP (e.g., due to an outflow surge), the error becomes positive, the controller increases the valve opening, more liquid enters, and the level rises back toward SP. Conversely, if the level rises above SP, the valve closes partially. This continuous, self-correcting action is the essence of closed-loop analog control. --- **Q4(b) [7 Marks]** — A differential pressure level transmitter is used in a 4 m tall tank. The transmitter output is 4 mA at 0 m and 20 mA at 4 m. The PLC's analog output drives a control valve from 4 mA (fully closed) to 20 mA (fully open). The current level is 2.8 m and the setpoint is 3.2 m. The PID controller output is 65 %. Calculate: - (i) The current signal from the level transmitter. - (ii) The current signal sent to the control valve. - (iii) The error signal in engineering units and as a percentage of span. **Model Solution:** **(i) Current from level transmitter at 2.8 m:** $$I_{LT} = 4 + 16 \times \frac{2.8 - 0}{4 - 0} = 4 + 16 \times 0.7 = 4 + 11.2 = 15.2 \text{ mA}$$ **(ii) Current to control valve at 65 % MV:** For valve output, EU range is 0 to 100 %: $$I_{CV} = 4 + 16 \times \frac{65 - 0}{100 - 0} = 4 + 16 \times 0.65 = 4 + 10.4 = 14.4 \text{ mA}$$ **(iii) Error in engineering units and as percentage of span:** Error in EU: $$e = SP - PV = 3.2 - 2.8 = 0.4 \text{ m}$$ Error as percentage of span (4 m): $$e_{\%} = \frac{0.4}{4} \times 100\% = 10\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall for this question:** > 1. Mixing up **level transmitter scaling (0 to 4 m)** with **valve scaling (0 to 100 %)**. The 4–20 mA range is the same, but the **EU interpretation differs**. > 2. Forgetting to **add 4 mA** (the live zero) when computing the current — many students start from 0 mA. > 3. Reporting the error as a **signed** quantity — always specify the **direction** (positive means level is below SP, negative means above). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam** - [ ] **Analog signal** = continuous in time **and** amplitude; PLC's internal representation is digital, but the I/O is analog. - [ ] **4–20 mA** is the industry standard; "live zero" at 4 mA allows fault detection. - [ ] **ADC resolution** = $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n} - 1}$; more bits → finer resolution. - [ ] **Scaling formula** (most-tested equation): $EU = \left(\dfrac{N - N_{lo}}{N_{hi} - N_{lo}}\right) \times (EU_{hi} - EU_{lo}) + EU_{lo}$ - [ ] **Inverse scaling** is used for analog **outputs** (PID MV → valve current). - [ ] **PID equation**: $u(t) = K_{c}\left[e + \dfrac{1}{T_{i}}\int e\,dt + T_{d}\dfrac{de}{dt}\right]$ - [ ] **P-term** → reacts to magnitude of error; too high → oscillation. - [ ] **I-term** → reacts to duration of error; eliminates steady-state offset. - [ ] **D-term** → reacts to rate of change; adds damping; sensitive to noise. - [ ] **Tank transfer function** is a first-order lag: $\dfrac{R}{ARs + 1}$, time constant $\tau = AR$. - [ ] **Closed-loop block diagram** must contain: SP, comparator, controller, actuator, process, sensor, feedback path. - [ ] **Ziegler-Nichols** open-loop tuning: $K_{c} = 1.2\tau/(K_{p}L)$, $T_{i} = 2L$, $T_{d} = 0.5L$. - [ ] **Ladder logic** uses `SCL` (Scale) instruction and `PID` instruction in Allen-Bradley; `SCP` (Scale Parameters) in Siemens. - [ ] **Signal conditioner** tasks: amplification, filtering, isolation, linearization, cold-junction compensation (for TC). - [ ] **Current loop** advantage: immune to voltage drop and electrical noise over long cable runs. - [ ] **Voltage signal** advantage: simpler, cheaper, suitable for short distances. - [ ] Always state the **EU range, signal range, and ADC bits** before writing a scaling equation. - [ ] For exam answers, **draw the block diagram first**, then explain each block, then compute — this is the KTU-mandated answer structure for 7+ mark questions. --- > **End of Module 2 Notes — PLC Analog Signal Processing: Case Study of Tank Level Control System** > *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — OEMRT612*

topics.title: Bottle Filling System and Sequential Switching of Motors
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bottle Filling System – Formal Academic Definition

> **Bottle Filling System (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A Bottle Filling System is an automated industrial process controlled by a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** that performs the sequential operations of bottle detection, filling, level sensing, and discharge on a continuously moving or stationary conveyor line. The system replaces manual filling by integrating **digital inputs (sensors)**, **digital outputs (motors, solenoid valves, indicators)**, and **timers** into a deterministic ladder-logic sequence.

The PLC monitors a stream of empty bottles, actuates a filling nozzle via a **solenoid valve** for a precise volume (timed-fill method) or until a level sensor confirms the desired height, and then signals the conveyor to advance. The architecture is the canonical example used in KTU Module 2 to teach **sequential process control using PLCs**.

### 1.1.1 Components of a PLC-Controlled Bottle Filling System

| Block | Physical Device | PLC I/O Type | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bottle Presence Sensor** | Photoelectric / Proximity switch | DI (Digital Input) | Detects arrival of empty bottle at filling station |
| **Liquid Level Sensor** | Float switch / Conductivity probe | DI | Confirms bottle is full |
| **Start Push-Button** | NO Push-button (Green) | DI | Initiates the process |
| **Stop Push-Button** | NC Push-button (Red) | DI | Halts the process |
| **Conveyor Motor** | 3-Phase Induction Motor (DOL starter) | DO (Digital Output) | Moves bottles along the line |
| **Solenoid Valve (Filler)** | 2/2 or 3/2 Solenoid-operated valve | DO | Opens/closes the liquid flow |
| **Status Indicators** | Pilot lamps (Red, Green, Yellow) | DO | Visual feedback of system state |
| **Audible Alarm** | Buzzer / Hooter | DO | Fault indication |
| **Power Supply** | 24 V DC regulated | — | Powers sensors and PLC inputs |

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Human Pourer vs. The PLC Pourer"

> **Analogy:** Imagine a barista at a café counter filling reusable bottles with cold-press juice. The barista's brain acts like the **CPU of the PLC**: it watches the bottle (**input sensor**), reaches for the tap (**solenoid valve**), pours while counting seconds in their head (**timer / level sensor**), closes the tap, signals "next!" to a coworker at the conveyor (**motor output**), and finally the filled bottle rolls away. A PLC does *exactly* this – but with **microsecond precision, zero fatigue, and the ability to repeat the same task millions of times without a single drop of error**. The only "language" it understands is **ladder logic**, which we will explore in Section 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** The Bottle Filling System is the **flagship example** for the topics "Sequential Process Control" and "Ladder Logic Programming using Timers & Counters." Expect a guaranteed 7–14 mark question on either the **ladder program** or the **I/O mapping** in your End Semester Exam.

---

## 1.2 Sequential Switching of Motors – Formal Academic Definition

> **Sequential Switching of Motors (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** Sequential switching of motors is a PLC-based control strategy in which two or more electric motors are energized in a **predefined time-based or condition-based order**, with each successive motor starting only after the previous motor has reached a stable operating state (or after a preset timer elapses). This technique is used to (i) prevent **simultaneous inrush currents** that would trip the main breaker, (ii) maintain **logical process flow** in conveyor chains, and (iii) implement **interlocking** for safety and direction reversal.

The most common KTU application involves:
- **Motor M1 → M2 → M3** starting with a 10-second delay between each.
- **Forward/Reverse operation** of a single motor with electrical/mechanical interlocking.
- **Star–Delta automatic transition** using a PLC timer (PLC replaces the conventional timer relay).
- **DOL (Direct-On-Line) starter** logic for a 3-phase induction motor.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy – "Boarding an Airplane"

> **Analogy:** Picture passengers boarding a plane in three groups: Group A first, then Group B, then Group C. If all three groups stormed the gate at once, there would be a stampede (analogous to a **current surge** tripping the breaker). The airline enforces a *sequence* to keep things orderly. A PLC enforces the same discipline on motors: it tells Motor 1 to start, waits for it to settle (or for a 10 s timer to expire), then tells Motor 2, and so on. Reverse operation is like asking a passenger to "un-board" — but the PLC ensures the forward and reverse contactors are **NEVER both ON at the same time**, which would cause a **short circuit across the phases**. This mutual exclusion is called **interlocking**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The **inrush current** of a 3-phase induction motor at DOL start-up is typically **5 to 7 times** the full-load current ($I_{start} \approx 5 I_{FL}$ to $7 I_{FL}$). Sequencing prevents cumulative surges when multiple motors share a common supply transformer.

---

## 1.3 The Common Pedagogical Thread

Both topics demonstrate the **four pillars of PLC programming**:

1. **Inputs (Sensors / Push-buttons)** → 2. **Decision (Ladder Logic Rungs)** → 3. **Outputs (Motors / Valves / Lamps)** → 4. **Feedback (Status / Sensors closing the loop)**

Mastering the Bottle Filling System gives the student the **template** to program any sequence-driven industrial process – from cement batching to car wash automation to traffic light control. Sequential motor switching extends this template to **multi-actuator coordination**, a must-have skill for any KTU electrical / mechatronics engineer entering a process plant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bottle Filling System – Operational Theory (Step-by-Step)

The process is best understood as a **finite state machine (FSM)** with five discrete states. The PLC transitions between states based on sensor inputs and elapsed timer values.

### 2.1.1 The Five Operational States

> **State 0 — IDLE (System Waiting)**
> - Conveyor motor OFF
> - Solenoid valve CLOSED
> - Green "READY" lamp ON
> - System waits for the **START** push-button to be pressed.

> **State 1 — CONVEYOR RUN (Transport)**
> - START pressed → Conveyor motor energizes.
> - Bottles travel on the belt toward the filling station.
> - Photoelectric sensor **S1** monitors the nozzle position.
> - When a bottle breaks the photo beam, sensor **S1 = 1**.

> **State 2 — FILLING (Solenoid Open)**
> - Bottle detected (S1 = 1) AND conveyor in position → Conveyor motor stops (interlocked).
> - **Solenoid Valve (Y1)** is energized, opening the liquid line.
> - A **Timer (T1)** begins counting. The fill time $T_{fill}$ is calibrated to deliver the desired volume $V$:

$$V = Q \cdot T_{fill}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate of the liquid in **litres/second** and $T_{fill}$ is in **seconds**.

> **State 3 — FILL COMPLETE (Solenoid Closed)**
> - Timer T1 reaches preset value (PV) OR Level sensor (S2) confirms full bottle.
> - Solenoid Y1 de-energizes (valve closes).
> - A 1-second settle timer (T2) allows residual drips to clear.

> **State 4 — DISCHARGE & CYCLE REPEAT**
> - Conveyor motor re-energizes.
> - Filled bottle moves to the next station (capping / labelling).
> - After a short delay (or when S1 = 0 again), the system returns to State 1.
> - Counter (C1) increments to track total bottles filled.

### 2.1.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why & How" Layer)

- **Why a photoelectric sensor instead of a mechanical limit switch?** Non-contact sensing prevents contamination, wear, and is faster (response time $\approx$ **1 ms** vs. **10–20 ms** for mechanical switches).
- **Why a timer-based fill instead of a flow meter?** Timers are **cheaper, simpler, and adequate** for low-viscosity fluids at constant pressure. For high-precision pharmaceutical filling, a flow-meter or load-cell feedback is preferred.
- **Why a 1-second settle delay?** Surface tension causes a brief **post-fill drip**. A settle timer prevents product loss and keeps the nozzle clean.
- **Why a counter?** Production data (bottles per shift) is a hard requirement in any **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** calculation. The counter is often wired to an HMI for live display.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners *love* to ask: *"Why is an interlock provided between the conveyor and the solenoid valve?"* The answer: **To prevent filling while the bottle is moving**, which would cause splashing, spillage, and inaccurate volumes. The conveyor MUST be stopped the moment a bottle is under the nozzle.

---

## 2.2 Sequential Switching of Motors – Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Mode A: Time-Based Sequential Starting (M1 → M2 → M3)

This is the most common configuration in KTU problems. The PLC has three timer instructions (T1, T2, T3) cascading in a chain.

**Operating Logic:**

- **Step 1 (t = 0 s):** Operator presses START (PB1). Motor M1 energizes immediately through output contactor K1. Timer T1 starts counting.
- **Step 2 (t = 10 s):** T1 reaches preset (10 s). T1's contacts close, energizing Motor M2 (K2). Timer T2 starts.
- **Step 3 (t = 20 s):** T2 reaches preset (10 s). T2's contacts close, energizing Motor M3 (K3). Timer T3 starts (or process flag is set).
- **Step 4:** The "ALL RUNNING" green lamp is energized once M1, M2, M3 are all ON. Overload relay (OL) contacts remain in series as a safety cut-out.
- **STOP Sequence:** Pressing STOP (PB2) de-energizes ALL motors simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale:** Staggered starting reduces **peak demand** on the upstream transformer. If each motor draws 20 A at start-up, simultaneous start = **60 A inrush**; sequential start = **20 A** + 20 A (running) + 20 A (running) = **20 A peak** above steady state. This protects switchgear from nuisance tripping.

### 2.2.2 Mode B: Forward / Reverse Operation with Electrical Interlocking

A single 3-phase induction motor is driven in both directions by swapping any **two of the three phase lines**. Two contactors — **K_F (Forward)** and **K_R (Reverse)** — must **never close simultaneously**, or a phase-to-phase short circuit will occur.

**Safety Mechanisms (KTU expects both):**

| Interlock Type | Implementation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Interlock** | K_F's NC contact in series with K_R's coil (and vice-versa) | Prevents both contactors from energizing at the same instant if both PB are pressed |
| **Mechanical Interlock** | Physical lever between K_F and K_R contactors | Back-up protection if electrical interlock fails (e.g., welded contact) |
| **Time Delay (PLC-based)** | Timer T1 (≈ 1 s) between Forward-OFF and Reverse-ON | Allows residual back-EMF / rotating mass to decay, preventing plugging current surge |
| **Overload Relay (OL)** | OL NC contact in series with both contactor coils | Trips on sustained overcurrent |

### 2.2.3 Mode C: Automatic Star–Delta Starter using PLC

A conventional Star–Delta starter uses three contactors: **K_M (Main)**, **K_S (Star)**, **K_D (Delta)**. The PLC replaces the mechanical timer.

**Transition Logic:**

$$t_{star} \approx \frac{2 \text{ to } 5 \text{ seconds (motor dependent)}}{}$$

1. **t = 0:** START pressed → K_M + K_S energize (motor in Star, low voltage ≈ 230 V per phase on 400 V supply).
2. **t = T_star:** Timer T1 reaches preset → K_S de-energizes. A short anti-short-circuit delay (T2 ≈ 50–100 ms) elapses.
3. **t = T_star + T2:** K_D energizes (motor in Delta, full 400 V line voltage).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Drawing **K_S and K_D energized simultaneously**. This is the classic KTU question where students lose 3–4 marks. Always include the **changeover dead time (T2)** in the ladder diagram.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value and division bars below are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to comply with the markdown table syntax safeguard.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Fill** | $V = Q \cdot T_{fill}$ | $V$ = volume filled, $Q$ = flow rate, $T_{fill}$ = fill time | $V$ in litres, $Q$ in L/s, $T$ in s | Calibrates the timer preset in bottle filling |
| 2 | **Flow Rate Conversion** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot v$ | $D$ = pipe inner diameter, $v$ = fluid velocity | $Q$ in m³/s, $D$ in m, $v$ in m/s | Determines required solenoid valve size |
| 3 | **Inrush Current** | $I_{start} \approx (5 \text{ to } 7) \cdot I_{FL}$ | $I_{FL}$ = full-load current | Amperes (A) | Justifies sequential starting |
| 4 | **Sequential Time Delay** | $T_{seq} = n \cdot t_{delay}$ | $n$ = number of motors, $t_{delay}$ = inter-motor delay | Seconds (s) | Total start-up time of a motor chain |
| 5 | **Star Phase Voltage** | $V_{ph,star} = \dfrac{V_{line}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $V_{line}$ = line-line voltage | Volts (V) | Confirms reduced-voltage starting |
| 6 | **Star Starting Torque** | $T_{star} = \dfrac{1}{3} T_{DOL}$ | $T_{DOL}$ = direct-on-line torque | N·m | Predicts whether motor can start under load in Star |
| 7 | **Star Starting Current** | $I_{star} = \dfrac{1}{3} I_{DOL}$ | $I_{DOL}$ = direct-on-line inrush | A | Confirms switchgear sizing |
| 8 | **Power (3-Phase)** | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L} \cdot I_{L} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta$ | $V_L$ = line voltage, $I_L$ = line current, $\cos\phi$ = power factor, $\eta$ = efficiency | Watts (W) | Sizes motor feeder cables |
| 9 | **Counter Scaling** | $N_{cases} = \dfrac{C_{preset}}{n_{bottles/case}}$ | $C_{preset}$ = counter preset, $n$ = bottles per case | Integer (dimensionless) | Auto-stop after packing N cases |
| 10 | **Scan Cycle Time** | $T_{scan} \approx 10 \text{ to } 20 \text{ ms}$ | Typical modern PLC scan time | Milliseconds (ms) | Determines max sequential step resolution |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bottle Filling:** Deployed in **FMCG (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods)** industries — Coca-Cola, Pepsi, Amul, Dabur, Parle — with throughputs of **200 to 600 bottles per minute** on a single line. PLCs from Allen-Bradley (Rockwell), Siemens S7-1200/1500, and Mitsubishi MELSEC dominate this domain.
- **Sequential Motor Switching:** Found in **pumping stations** (multiple pumps brought online based on demand), **compressor rooms**, **HVAC chiller sequencing**, and **paper mill drive trains**.
- **Forward/Reverse Motor Control:** Used in **conveyor belts, hoists, cranes, lifts, machine tool spindles, gate operators, and electric vehicles**.
- **Star–Delta PLC Control:** Standard in industries where motors above **5 HP (≈ 3.7 kW)** must avoid direct-on-line stress on the supply — especially in **textile mills, cement plants, and water treatment facilities**.

> **Industry Standard Reference Metric (KTU Approved):** A typical 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz, 5 HP induction motor draws a full-load current of approximately **7.5 A** and an inrush of **45–52 A** at DOL start. Sequential starting limits the simultaneous inrush to one motor's value.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Ladder Logic & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Bottle Filling System – Complete I/O Allocation Table (KTU Standard)

| PLC Address | Tag Name | Device | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I0.0** | PB_START | NO Push-button (Green) | DI | Start the process |
| **I0.1** | PB_STOP | NC Push-button (Red) | DI | Stop the process |
| **I0.2** | S_BOTTLE | Photoelectric sensor | DI | Detects bottle at filling station |
| **I0.3** | S_LEVEL | Float / Level switch | DI | Confirms bottle is full |
| **I0.4** | OL_TRIP | Overload relay contact | DI | Motor safety cut-out |
| **Q0.0** | K_CONV | Conveyor contactor coil | DO | Drives conveyor motor |
| **Q0.1** | Y_SOL | Solenoid valve | DO | Opens/closes liquid line |
| **Q0.2** | L_READY | Green pilot lamp | DO | "System Ready" indicator |
| **Q0.3** | L_FILL | Yellow pilot lamp | DO | "Filling in progress" indicator |
| **Q0.4** | BUZZER | Buzzer | DO | Fault alarm |
| **T37** | T_FILL | TON Timer, PV = 5 s | Internal | Fill duration |
| **T38** | T_SETTLE | TON Timer, PV = 1 s | Internal | Settle delay |
| **C1** | C_BOTTLES | CTU Counter, PV = 100 | Internal | Counts bottles filled |

---

## 3.2 Ladder Logic Rungs – Bottle Filling System (Textual Description for KTU Board Exam)

A KTU answer sheet expects a **clean rung-by-rung ladder diagram** drawn with a pen. Below is the **complete program in 12 rungs**, with each rung's logic described in words so you can reproduce it on paper.

> **Rung 1 — Master Start Latch**
> Logic: `PB_START` (NO) in parallel with the auxiliary contact `K_CONV_AUX` (NO), in series with `PB_STOP` (NC) → energizes output `K_CONV` and the Ready lamp `L_READY`.
> Purpose: Classic **START-STOP holding circuit** (seal-in contact).

> **Rung 2 — Conveyor Seal-In Condition**
> Logic: Output `K_CONV` is latched via its own NO auxiliary contact.
> Purpose: Maintains the conveyor running until STOP or fault.

> **Rung 3 — Bottle Detection → Stop Conveyor**
> Logic: NO contact of `S_BOTTLE` in series with NC contact of `K_CONV` (the conveyor's own NC auxiliary) → opens the latch when a bottle is present.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bottle Filling System – Formal Academic Definition

> **Bottle Filling System (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A Bottle Filling System is an automated industrial process controlled by a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** that performs the sequential operations of bottle detection, filling, level sensing, and discharge on a continuously moving or stationary conveyor line. The system replaces manual filling by integrating **digital inputs (sensors)**, **digital outputs (motors, solenoid valves, indicators)**, and **timers** into a deterministic ladder-logic sequence.

The PLC monitors a stream of empty bottles, actuates a filling nozzle via a **solenoid valve** for a precise volume (timed-fill method) or until a level sensor confirms the desired height, and then signals the conveyor to advance. The architecture is the canonical example used in KTU Module 2 to teach **sequential process control using PLCs**.

### 1.1.1 Components of a PLC-Controlled Bottle Filling System

| Block | Physical Device | PLC I/O Type | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bottle Presence Sensor** | Photoelectric / Proximity switch | DI (Digital Input) | Detects arrival of empty bottle at filling station |
| **Liquid Level Sensor** | Float switch / Conductivity probe | DI | Confirms bottle is full |
| **Start Push-Button** | NO Push-button (Green) | DI | Initiates the process |
| **Stop Push-Button** | NC Push-button (Red) | DI | Halts the process |
| **Conveyor Motor** | 3-Phase Induction Motor (DOL starter) | DO (Digital Output) | Moves bottles along the line |
| **Solenoid Valve (Filler)** | 2/2 or 3/2 Solenoid-operated valve | DO | Opens/closes the liquid flow |
| **Status Indicators** | Pilot lamps (Red, Green, Yellow) | DO | Visual feedback of system state |
| **Audible Alarm** | Buzzer / Hooter | DO | Fault indication |
| **Power Supply** | 24 V DC regulated | — | Powers sensors and PLC inputs |

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Human Pourer vs. The PLC Pourer"

> **Analogy:** Imagine a barista at a café counter filling reusable bottles with cold-press juice. The barista's brain acts like the **CPU of the PLC**: it watches the bottle (**input sensor**), reaches for the tap (**solenoid valve**), pours while counting seconds in their head (**timer / level sensor**), closes the tap, signals "next!" to a coworker at the conveyor (**motor output**), and finally the filled bottle rolls away. A PLC does *exactly* this – but with **microsecond precision, zero fatigue, and the ability to repeat the same task millions of times without a single drop of error**. The only "language" it understands is **ladder logic**, which we will explore in Section 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** The Bottle Filling System is the **flagship example** for the topics "Sequential Process Control" and "Ladder Logic Programming using Timers & Counters." Expect a guaranteed 7–14 mark question on either the **ladder program** or the **I/O mapping** in your End Semester Exam.

---

## 1.2 Sequential Switching of Motors – Formal Academic Definition

> **Sequential Switching of Motors (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** Sequential switching of motors is a PLC-based control strategy in which two or more electric motors are energized in a **predefined time-based or condition-based order**, with each successive motor starting only after the previous motor has reached a stable operating state (or after a preset timer elapses). This technique is used to (i) prevent **simultaneous inrush currents** that would trip the main breaker, (ii) maintain **logical process flow** in conveyor chains, and (iii) implement **interlocking** for safety and direction reversal.

The most common KTU application involves:
- **Motor M1 → M2 → M3** starting with a 10-second delay between each.
- **Forward/Reverse operation** of a single motor with electrical/mechanical interlocking.
- **Star–Delta automatic transition** using a PLC timer (PLC replaces the conventional timer relay).
- **DOL (Direct-On-Line) starter** logic for a 3-phase induction motor.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy – "Boarding an Airplane"

> **Analogy:** Picture passengers boarding a plane in three groups: Group A first, then Group B, then Group C. If all three groups stormed the gate at once, there would be a stampede (analogous to a **current surge** tripping the breaker). The airline enforces a *sequence* to keep things orderly. A PLC enforces the same discipline on motors: it tells Motor 1 to start, waits for it to settle (or for a 10 s timer to expire), then tells Motor 2, and so on. Reverse operation is like asking a passenger to "un-board" — but the PLC ensures the forward and reverse contactors are **NEVER both ON at the same time**, which would cause a **short circuit across the phases**. This mutual exclusion is called **interlocking**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The **inrush current** of a 3-phase induction motor at DOL start-up is typically **5 to 7 times** the full-load current ($I_{start} \approx 5 I_{FL}$ to $7 I_{FL}$). Sequencing prevents cumulative surges when multiple motors share a common supply transformer.

---

## 1.3 The Common Pedagogical Thread

Both topics demonstrate the **four pillars of PLC programming**:

1. **Inputs (Sensors / Push-buttons)** → 2. **Decision (Ladder Rungs)** → 3. **Outputs (Motors / Valves / Lamps)** → 4. **Feedback (Status / Sensors closing the loop)**

Mastering the Bottle Filling System gives the student the **template** to program any sequence-driven industrial process – from cement batching to car wash automation to traffic light control. Sequential motor switching extends this template to **multi-actuator coordination**, a must-have skill for any KTU electrical / mechatronics engineer entering a process plant.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bottle Filling System – Operational Theory (Step-by-Step)

The process is best understood as a **finite state machine (FSM)** with five discrete states. The PLC transitions between states based on sensor inputs and elapsed timer values.

### 2.1.1 The Five Operational States

> **State 0 — IDLE (System Waiting)**
> - Conveyor motor OFF
> - Solenoid valve CLOSED
> - Green "READY" lamp ON
> - System waits for the **START** push-button to be pressed.

> **State 1 — CONVEYOR RUN (Transport)**
> - START pressed → Conveyor motor energizes.
> - Bottles travel on the belt toward the filling station.
> - Photoelectric sensor **S1** monitors the nozzle position.
> - When a bottle breaks the photo beam, sensor **S1 = 1**.

> **State 2 — FILLING (Solenoid Open)**
> - Bottle detected (S1 = 1) AND conveyor in position → Conveyor motor stops (interlocked).
> - **Solenoid Valve (Y1)** is energized, opening the liquid line.
> - A **Timer (T1)** begins counting. The fill time $T_{fill}$ is calibrated to deliver the desired volume $V$:

$$V = Q \cdot T_{fill}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate of the liquid in **litres/second** and $T_{fill}$ is in **seconds**.

> **State 3 — FILL COMPLETE (Solenoid Closed)**
> - Timer T1 reaches preset value (PV) OR Level sensor (S2) confirms full bottle.
> - Solenoid Y1 de-energizes (valve closes).
> - A 1-second settle timer (T2) allows residual drips to clear.

> **State 4 — DISCHARGE & CYCLE REPEAT**
> - Conveyor motor re-energizes.
> - Filled bottle moves to the next station (capping / labelling).
> - After a short delay (or when S1 = 0 again), the system returns to State 1.
> - Counter (C1) increments to track total bottles filled.

### 2.1.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why & How" Layer)

- **Why a photoelectric sensor instead of a mechanical limit switch?** Non-contact sensing prevents contamination, wear, and is faster (response time ≈ **1 ms** vs. **10–20 ms** for mechanical switches).
- **Why a timer-based fill instead of a flow meter?** Timers are **cheaper, simpler, and adequate** for low-viscosity fluids at constant pressure. For high-precision pharmaceutical filling, a flow-meter or load-cell feedback is preferred.
- **Why a 1-second settle delay?** Surface tension causes a brief **post-fill drip**. A settle timer prevents product loss and keeps the nozzle clean.
- **Why a counter?** Production data (bottles per shift) is a hard requirement in any **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** calculation. The counter is often wired to an HMI for live display.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners *love* to ask: *"Why is an interlock provided between the conveyor and the solenoid valve?"* The answer: **To prevent filling while the bottle is moving**, which would cause splashing, spillage, and inaccurate volumes. The conveyor MUST be stopped the moment a bottle is under the nozzle.

---

## 2.2 Sequential Switching of Motors – Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Mode A: Time-Based Sequential Starting (M1 → M2 → M3)

This is the most common configuration in KTU problems. The PLC has three timer instructions (T1, T2, T3) cascading in a chain.

**Operating Logic:**

- **Step 1 (t = 0 s):** Operator presses START (PB1). Motor M1 energizes immediately through output contactor K1. Timer T1 starts counting.
- **Step 2 (t = 10 s):** T1 reaches preset (10 s). T1's contacts close, energizing Motor M2 (K2). Timer T2 starts.
- **Step 3 (t = 20 s):** T2 reaches preset (10 s). T2's contacts close, energizing Motor M3 (K3). Timer T3 starts (or process flag is set).
- **Step 4:** The "ALL RUNNING" green lamp is energized once M1, M2, M3 are all ON. Overload relay (OL) contacts remain in series as a safety cut-out.
- **STOP Sequence:** Pressing STOP (PB2) de-energizes ALL motors simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale:** Staggered starting reduces **peak demand** on the upstream transformer. If each motor draws 20 A at start-up, simultaneous start = **60 A inrush**; sequential start = **20 A** + 20 A (running) + 20 A (running) = **20 A peak** above steady state. This protects switchgear from nuisance tripping.

### 2.2.2 Mode B: Forward / Reverse Operation with Electrical Interlocking

A single 3-phase induction motor is driven in both directions by swapping any **two of the three phase lines**. Two contactors — **K_F (Forward)** and **K_R (Reverse)** — must **never close simultaneously**, or a phase-to-phase short circuit will occur.

**Safety Mechanisms (KTU expects both):**

| Interlock Type | Implementation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Interlock** | K_F's NC contact in series with K_R's coil (and vice-versa) | Prevents both contactors from energizing at the same instant if both PB are pressed |
| **Mechanical Interlock** | Physical lever between K_F and K_R contactors | Back-up protection if electrical interlock fails (e.g., welded contact) |
| **Time Delay (PLC-based)** | Timer T1 (≈ 1 s) between Forward-OFF and Reverse-ON | Allows residual back-EMF / rotating mass to decay, preventing plugging current surge |
| **Overload Relay (OL)** | OL NC contact in series with both contactor coils | Trips on sustained overcurrent |

### 2.2.3 Mode C: Automatic Star–Delta Starter using PLC

A conventional Star–Delta starter uses three contactors: **K_M (Main)**, **K_S (Star)**, **K_D (Delta)**. The PLC replaces the mechanical timer.

**Transition Logic:**

1. **t = 0:** START pressed → K_M + K_S energize (motor in Star, low voltage ≈ 230 V per phase on 400 V supply).
2. **t = T_star:** Timer T1 reaches preset → K_S de-energizes. A short anti-short-circuit delay (T2 ≈ 50–100 ms) elapses.
3. **t = T_star + T2:** K_D energizes (motor in Delta, full 400 V line voltage).

The Star phase voltage is:

$$V_{ph,star} = \dfrac{V_{line}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Drawing **K_S and K_D energized simultaneously**. This is the classic KTU question where students lose 3–4 marks. Always include the **changeover dead time (T2)** in the ladder diagram.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value and division bars below are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to comply with the markdown table syntax safeguard.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Fill** | $V = Q \cdot T_{fill}$ | $V$ = volume filled, $Q$ = flow rate, $T_{fill}$ = fill time | $V$ in litres, $Q$ in L/s, $T$ in s | Calibrates the timer preset in bottle filling |
| 2 | **Flow Rate Conversion** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot v$ | $D$ = pipe inner diameter, $v$ = fluid velocity | $Q$ in m³/s, $D$ in m, $v$ in m/s | Determines required solenoid valve size |
| 3 | **Inrush Current** | $I_{start} \approx (5 \text{ to } 7) \cdot I_{FL}$ | $I_{FL}$ = full-load current | Amperes (A) | Justifies sequential starting |
| 4 | **Sequential Time Delay** | $T_{seq} = n \cdot t_{delay}$ | $n$ = number of motors, $t_{delay}$ = inter-motor delay | Seconds (s) | Total start-up time of a motor chain |
| 5 | **Star Phase Voltage** | $V_{ph,star} = \dfrac{V_{line}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $V_{line}$ = line-line voltage | Volts (V) | Confirms reduced-voltage starting |
| 6 | **Star Starting Torque** | $T_{star} = \dfrac{1}{3} T_{DOL}$ | $T_{DOL}$ = direct-on-line torque | N·m | Predicts whether motor can start under load in Star |
| 7 | **Star Starting Current** | $I_{star} = \dfrac{1}{3} I_{DOL}$ | $I_{DOL}$ = direct-on-line inrush | A | Confirms switchgear sizing |
| 8 | **Power (3-Phase)** | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L} \cdot I_{L} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta$ | $V_L$ = line voltage, $I_L$ = line current, $\cos\phi$ = power factor, $\eta$ = efficiency | Watts (W) | Sizes motor feeder cables |
| 9 | **Counter Scaling** | $N_{cases} = \dfrac{C_{preset}}{n_{bottles/case}}$ | $C_{preset}$ = counter preset, $n$ = bottles per case | Integer (dimensionless) | Auto-stop after packing N cases |
| 10 | **Scan Cycle Time** | $T_{scan} \approx 10 \text{ to } 20 \text{ ms}$ | Typical modern PLC scan time | Milliseconds (ms) | Determines max sequential step resolution |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bottle Filling:** Deployed in **FMCG (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods)** industries — Coca-Cola, Pepsi, Amul, Dabur, Parle — with throughputs of **200 to 600 bottles per minute** on a single line. PLCs from Allen-Bradley (Rockwell), Siemens S7-1200/1500, and Mitsubishi MELSEC dominate this domain.
- **Sequential Motor Switching:** Found in **pumping stations** (multiple pumps brought online based on demand), **compressor rooms**, **HVAC chiller sequencing**, and **paper mill drive trains**.
- **Forward/Reverse Motor Control:** Used in **conveyor belts, hoists, cranes, lifts, machine tool spindles, gate operators, and electric vehicles**.
- **Star–Delta PLC Control:** Standard in industries where motors above **5 HP (≈ 3.7 kW)** must avoid direct-on-line stress on the supply — especially in **textile mills, cement plants, and water treatment facilities**.

> **Industry Standard Reference Metric (KTU Approved):** A typical 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz, 5 HP induction motor draws a full-load current of approximately **7.5 A** and an inrush of **45–52 A** at DOL start. Sequential starting limits the simultaneous inrush to one motor's value.
