# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Ladder Logic & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Bottle Filling System – Complete I/O Allocation Table (KTU Standard)

| PLC Address | Tag Name | Device | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I0.0** | PB_START | NO Push-button (Green) | DI | Start the process |
| **I0.1** | PB_STOP | NC Push-button (Red) | DI | Stop the process |
| **I0.2** | S_BOTTLE | Photoelectric sensor | DI | Detects bottle at filling station |
| **I0.3** | S_LEVEL | Float / Level switch | DI | Confirms bottle is full |
| **I0.4** | OL_TRIP | Overload relay contact | DI | Motor safety cut-out |
| **Q0.0** | K_CONV | Conveyor contactor coil | DO | Drives conveyor motor |
| **Q0.1** | Y_SOL | Solenoid valve | DO | Opens/closes liquid line |
| **Q0.2** | L_READY | Green pilot lamp | DO | "System Ready" indicator |
| **Q0.3** | L_FILL | Yellow pilot lamp | DO | "Filling in progress" indicator |
| **Q0.4** | BUZZER | Buzzer | DO | Fault alarm |
| **T37** | T_FILL | TON Timer, PV = 5 s | Internal | Fill duration |
| **T38** | T_SETTLE | TON Timer, PV = 1 s | Internal | Settle delay |
| **C1** | C_BOTTLES | CTU Counter, PV = 100 | Internal | Counts bottles filled |

---

## 3.2 Ladder Logic Rungs – Bottle Filling System (Complete Program)

A KTU answer sheet expects a **clean rung-by-rung ladder diagram** drawn with a pen. Below is the **complete 14-rung program**, with each rung's logic fully described.

> **Rung 1 — Master Latch (Start/Stop)**
> Logic: NO contact `PB_START` (I0.0) **in parallel with** the auxiliary contact `K_CONV_AUX` (NO of Q0.0), the whole combination in series with NC contact `PB_STOP` (I0.1) and NC contact `OL_TRIP` (I0.4) → drives coil `K_CONV` (Q0.0).
> Purpose: Classic **START-STOP holding circuit** with overload protection.

> **Rung 2 — Ready Lamp**
> Logic: NO contact of `K_CONV` (Q0.0) → energizes `L_READY` (Q0.2).
> Purpose: Conveyor running → "Ready" lamp ON.

> **Rung 3 — Bottle Detected → Drop Conveyor (Interlock)**
> Logic: NO contact of `S_BOTTLE` (I0.2) in series with NC contact of `K_CONV` (Q0.0) → no output (this rung is an *unlatch* rung).
> Purpose: The NO `S_BOTTLE` contact, when placed in a **reset** branch of the master latch, breaks the seal-in when a bottle is detected, stopping the conveyor.

> **Rung 4 — Start Fill Timer**
> Logic: NO contact of `S_BOTTLE` (I0.2) in series with NC contact of `T_FILL.DN` (T37 done bit) → starts `T_FILL` (TON, PV = 5 s).
> Purpose: When bottle is detected and fill not yet complete, begin timing.

> **Rung 5 — Energize Solenoid Valve**
> Logic: NO contact of `S_BOTTLE` (I0.2) AND NO contact of `S_LEVEL` (I0.3) AND NC contact of `T_FILL.DN` (T37) → drives coil `Y_SOL` (Q0.1).
> Purpose: Open the liquid valve ONLY if (a) bottle is present, (b) bottle is not yet full, (c) timer has not yet timed out.

> **Rung 6 — "Filling" Yellow Lamp**
> Logic: NO contact of `Y_SOL` (Q0.1) → drives `L_FILL` (Q0.3).
> Purpose: Visual feedback: "Filling in progress."

> **Rung 7 — Settle Timer (Residual Drip)**
> Logic: NO contact of `T_FILL.DN` (T37) → starts `T_SETTLE` (TON, PV = 1 s).
> Purpose: After fill completes, wait 1 s for the nozzle to stop dripping.

> **Rung 8 — Restart Conveyor after Settle**
> Logic: NO contact of `T_SETTLE.DN` (T38) **in parallel with** the master latch `K_CONV_AUX` (NO) → re-energizes `K_CONV`.
> Purpose: Once the settle timer expires, restart the conveyor to move the filled bottle downstream.

> **Rung 9 — Counter Increment**
> Logic: NO contact of `T_FILL.DN` (T37) rising-edge → CTU counter `C_BOTTLES` (PV = 100).
> Purpose: Each completed fill increments the bottle counter; at PV = 100, the counter done bit `C1.DN` can trigger a batch-complete alarm or stop.

> **Rung 10 — Batch Complete Indicator**
> Logic: NO contact of `C1.DN` (counter done) → energizes `L_BATCH` (additional lamp, Q0.5).
> Purpose: Signals "100 bottles filled" to the operator.

> **Rung 11 — Counter Reset**
> Logic: NO contact of `PB_RESET_COUNT` (I0.5) or `C1.DN` → RES instruction on `C1`.
> Purpose: Resets the counter at the start of a new batch.

> **Rung 12 — Fault Buzzer on Overload**
> Logic: NO contact of `OL_TRIP` (I0.4) → energizes `BUZZER` (Q0.4).
> Purpose: Audible alarm if motor overload occurs.

> **Rung 13 — Emergency Stop (Hardwired Safety)**
> Logic: Hardwired NC E-Stop push-button in series with the main contactor coil, **outside the PLC**.
> Purpose: Hardwired safety to cut power even if the PLC fails.

> **Rung 14 — System Enable Master**
> Logic: A single `SYSTEM_ON` (M0.0) memory bit set by `PB_START`, reset by `PB_STOP`. All rungs include `M0.0` as an enable contact.
> Purpose: Global enable/disable flag for clean shutdown.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation – Fill Time Calculation

**Problem (KTU Standard):** A bottle filling station must deliver **500 mL** of water per bottle. The flow rate of the solenoid valve is **$Q = 2.5$ L/min**. Calculate the required PLC timer preset $T_{fill}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the given quantities.**
- Target volume: $V = 500$ mL $= 0.5$ L
- Flow rate: $Q = 2.5$ L/min

**Step 2 — Apply the volumetric equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= Q \cdot T_{fill} \\
T_{fill} &= \dfrac{V}{Q} \\
T_{fill} &= \dfrac{0.5 \text{ L}}{2.5 \text{ L/min}} \\
T_{fill} &= 0.2 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to seconds (PLC timer base).**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{fill} &= 0.2 \text{ min} \times 60 \text{ s/min} \\
T_{fill} &= 12 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Set PLC Timer Preset.**

Since most PLC timers (e.g., Siemens S7-1200 TON) have a **base of 1 second** by default, the preset value is:

$$PV_{T\_FILL} = 12$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Margin.** Add a **+10% safety margin** to account for pressure variation, fluid viscosity drift, and nozzle aging:

$$PV_{T\_FILL,design} = 12 \times 1.10 = 13.2 \text{ s} \approx 13 \text{ s (rounded)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Set the PLC timer `T_FILL` to **13 seconds** for production. Always round UP for over-fill (safer than under-fill, which causes customer complaints).

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation – Sequential Motor Start Time

**Problem:** Three motors M1, M2, M3 must start sequentially with a **15 s delay** between each. Calculate the **total start-up time** and the **peak current** drawn from a 415 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase supply if each motor is 10 HP.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= 3 \text{ motors} \\
t_{delay} &= 15 \text{ s} \\
P_{motor} &= 10 \text{ HP} = 10 \times 746 \text{ W} = 7460 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Total start-up time.**

$$T_{seq} = (n - 1) \cdot t_{delay}$$

The first motor starts at $t = 0$ (no delay before it), and each subsequent motor starts after a 15 s delay. Thus:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{seq} &= (3 - 1) \times 15 \text{ s} \\
T_{seq} &= 30 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Note:** Some textbooks use $T_{seq} = n \cdot t_{delay}$ assuming a delay *before* the first motor too. The KTU-accepted convention is the **cascading** form above.

**Step 3 — Full-load current of one motor.**

Using $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta$ with $\cos\phi = 0.85$ and $\eta = 0.9$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{FL} &= \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta} \\
I_{FL} &= \dfrac{7460}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85 \times 0.9} \\
I_{FL} &= \dfrac{7460}{549.5} \\
I_{FL} &\approx 13.6 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Inrush current (DOL).**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{start} &= 6 \times I_{FL} \\
I_{start} &= 6 \times 13.6 \\
I_{start} &= 81.6 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Peak current comparison.**

- **Simultaneous start:** $I_{peak,simul} = 3 \times 81.6 = 244.8$ A
- **Sequential start:** $I_{peak,seq} = 81.6$ A (only one motor inrushes at a time)

$$\dfrac{I_{peak,simul}}{I_{peak,seq}} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{3x reduction in peak demand}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This 3× reduction is the *headline engineering justification* for sequential starting. State it explicitly in any 7- or 14-mark question.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation – Star/Delta Starting Torque and Current

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz, 10 HP induction motor has a DOL starting torque of **60 N·m** and a DOL starting current of **81.6 A**. Calculate the Star starting torque and current. Comment on whether the motor can start a load requiring **30 N·m** of starting torque.

**Step 1 — Star starting current.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{star} &= \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot I_{DOL} \\
I_{star} &= \dfrac{81.6}{3} \\
I_{star} &= 27.2 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Star starting torque.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{star} &= \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot T_{DOL} \\
T_{star} &= \dfrac{60}{3} \\
T_{star} &= 20 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with load requirement.**

$$T_{star} = 20 \text{ N·m} < T_{load,req} = 30 \text{ N·m}$$

**Conclusion:** The motor **CANNOT** start this load in Star configuration. The operator will notice a "stuck" motor that hums but does not rotate. This is a classic KTU 14-mark "comment" question.

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Remedy:** Use a **Star-Delta starter ONLY for loads with low starting torque** (e.g., fans, centrifugal pumps, unloaded compressors). For high-inertia or high-torque loads (conveyors with product on them, crushers, mixers), use a **Soft Starter** or **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** instead.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix – KTU Question Pattern Recognition

| KTU Question Pattern | Core Concept Tested | Marks | Key Keywords in Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Explain the working of a PLC-based bottle filling system with a neat ladder diagram." | Sequential control + Ladder | 7–14 | "neat ladder diagram", "working" |
| "Develop an I/O allocation table for a bottle filling system." | I/O mapping | 3–7 | "I/O allocation", "address map" |
| "Design a PLC program for sequential starting of 3 motors with 10 s delay." | Timer cascade | 7 | "sequential", "10 s delay" |
| "Explain forward/reverse operation of a 3-phase induction motor with PLC." | Interlocking | 7 | "forward/reverse", "interlock" |
| "With a neat diagram, explain automatic Star-Delta starter using PLC." | Star-Delta + Timer | 7–14 | "automatic", "Star-Delta" |
| "Compare DOL, Star-Delta, and Soft Starter." | Comparison | 3–7 | "compare", "starting methods" |
| "Calculate the fill time for a given volume and flow rate." | Numerical | 3–7 | "calculate", "fill time" |
| "A motor draws X A inrush. Justify sequential starting." | Numerical + Justification | 3–7 | "inrush", "justification" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bottle Filling System – Process Flow Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Operator Presses START]) --> B[Conveyor Motor Energizes K_CONV ON]
    B --> C[Green Ready Lamp L_READY ON]
    C --> D{Bottle Detected at Nozzle S_BOTTLE 1}
    D -- NO --> E[Conveyor Continues Running]
    E --> D
    D -- YES --> F[Stop Conveyor K_CONV OFF]
    F --> G[Energize Solenoid Valve Y_SOL ON]
    G --> H[Start Fill Timer T_FILL PV 5s]
    H --> I[Yellow Filling Lamp L_FILL ON]
    I --> J{Timer T_FILL Done OR Level Sensor S_LEVEL 1}
    J -- NO --> I
    J -- YES --> K[De-energize Solenoid Y_SOL OFF]
    K --> L[Start Settle Timer T_SETTLE PV 1s]
    L --> M[T_SETTLE Done]
    M --> N[Increment Counter C_BOTTLES]
    N --> O{Counter PV 100 Bottles}
    O -- NO --> P[Restart Conveyor K_CONV ON]
    P --> D
    O -- YES --> Q[Trigger Batch Complete Indicator L_BATCH ON]
    Q --> R([Await Operator Reset])
    R --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular block is a PLC output action; each diamond is a sensor or timer condition that drives a decision. The loop from `O -- NO --> P` ensures the cycle repeats indefinitely until the count target is met.

---

## 4.2 Sequential Motor Starting – Cascade Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A([START Push Button PB1]) --> B[Motor M1 Energized K1 ON]
    B --> C[Timer T1 Starts Preset 10s]
    C --> D{T1 Done}
    D -- NO --> C
    D -- YES --> E[Motor M2 Energized K2 ON]
    E --> F[Timer T2 Starts Preset 10s]
    F --> G{T2 Done}
    G -- NO --> F
    G -- YES --> H[Motor M3 Energized K3 ON]
    H --> I[All Running Lamp L_ALL ON]
    I --> J([STOP PB2 De-energizes All Motors])
    J --> B
    J --> E
    J --> H
```

---

## 4.3 Forward/Reverse Motor Control – Ladder Logic Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Forward PB FWD]) --> B{Stop PB NC and OL NC}
    B -- OK --> C[K_F Contactor Energized]
    C --> D[Motor Rotates Forward]
    D --> E([Release FWD PB])
    E --> F[T1 Timer Starts 1s]
    F --> G{T1 Done}
    G -- NO --> H[Reverse Blocked by Interlock]
    G -- YES --> I([Reverse PB REV Now Allowed])
    I --> J{Stop PB NC and OL NC and K_F NC}
    J -- OK --> K[K_R Contactor Energized]
    K --> L[Motor Rotates Reverse]
    L --> M([Release REV PB])
    M --> F
```

> **Key engineering feature visible in the diagram:** The `K_F NC` contact in the Reverse branch and the `K_R NC` contact in the Forward branch (not explicitly shown here for clarity) constitute the **electrical interlock** that prevents both contactors from being energized at the same time.

---

## 4.4 Star-Delta Automatic Starter – Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START PB]) --> B[K_M Main Contactor Energized]
    B --> C[K_S Star Contactor Energized]
    C --> D[Motor Runs in Star Mode]
    D --> E[Timer T1 Starts Preset 5s]
    E --> F{T1 Done}
    F -- NO --> E
    F -- YES --> G[K_S Star Contactor De-energized]
    G --> H[Timer T2 Starts Preset 100ms Dead Time]
    H --> I{T2 Done}
    I -- NO --> H
    I -- YES --> J[K_D Delta Contactor Energized]
    J --> K[Motor Runs in Delta Mode Full Voltage]
    K --> L([STOP PB De-energizes K_M and K_D])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Dead Time (T2):** The 100 ms gap between K_S OFF and K_D ON is the **anti-short-circuit delay**. Without it, K_S and K_D would both be momentarily closed, shorting two phases of the supply through the motor windings — a catastrophic failure.

---

## 4.5 Complete I/O Wiring Schematic – Symbol Legend

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `[/]` | NO Push-button (Normally Open) |
| `[/\]` | NC Push-button (Normally Closed) |
| `( )` | Output coil / relay coil |
| `-( )-` | NO contact (closed when coil is energized) |
| `-/()-` | NC contact (open when coil is energized) |
| `[TON]` | Timer ON-delay instruction |
| `[CTU]` | Counter Up instruction |
| `----[/]----` | Sensor contact (NO type) |

> All four ladder diagrams above are directly transferable to **Siemens S7-1200 (TIA Portal)**, **Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1400**, or **Mitsubishi GX Works** programming environments with only minor address-mapping differences.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Q: Define sequential switching of motors. Why is it preferred over simultaneous starting in industrial applications?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Sequential switching of motors** is a PLC-based control strategy in which two or more electric motors are energized in a predefined time-based or condition-based order, with each successive motor starting only after the previous motor has reached a stable operating state **(1 mark)**. It is preferred over simultaneous starting because (i) simultaneous starting of multiple motors causes a **cumulative inrush current** (5–7 times full-load current per motor) that can **trip the main circuit breaker** or **overload the supply transformer**; (ii) sequential starting limits the peak inrush to **one motor at a time**, reducing the peak demand by a factor equal to the number of motors; and (iii) it maintains a **logical process flow** in conveyor chains and pumping stations, ensuring downstream equipment only operates after upstream equipment is ready **(2 marks)**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q: List any four components of a PLC-based bottle filling system and state the function of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Photoelectric sensor** — detects the presence of an empty bottle at the filling nozzle (digital input) **(0.75 mark)**.
> 2. **Solenoid valve** — controls the flow of liquid into the bottle (digital output) **(0.75 mark)**.
> 3. **Conveyor motor** (with DOL starter) — transports bottles along the filling line (digital output) **(0.75 mark)**.
> 4. **PLC timer** — sets the precise fill duration to deliver the required volumetric dose (internal instruction) **(0.75 mark)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option 1)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]
**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a PLC-based automatic bottle filling system. Draw the ladder logic diagram and state the function of each rung.

**(b) [7 marks]** A bottle filling station must deliver **750 mL** of milk per bottle. The solenoid valve flow rate is **3 L/min**. Calculate the required PLC timer preset value in seconds. Also explain what happens if the timer preset is set **higher** than the calculated value.

> **Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid flowchart. The system consists of a **photoelectric sensor (S1)** at the filling station, a **solenoid valve (Y1)** for the milk line, a **conveyor motor (M)** driven by a DOL contactor, a **float level switch (S2)** inside the bottle, **start/stop push-buttons**, and the **PLC** (CPU + I/O modules + 24 V DC power supply).
>
> **Working (2 marks):**
> 1. Operator presses **START** → master latch sets → **conveyor motor M** energizes → bottles travel along the belt.
> 2. **Photoelectric sensor S1** detects a bottle at the nozzle → S1 = 1 → conveyor interlocks OFF → bottle is positioned.
> 3. **Solenoid Y1** energizes → milk begins to flow. A **timer T_FILL** (TON) starts counting to a preset value (calculated in part b).
> 4. When T_FILL reaches its preset (or S2 confirms full level) → Y1 de-energizes → valve closes.
> 5. A **settle timer T_SETTLE (1 s)** allows drips to clear → conveyor restarts → filled bottle moves downstream.
> 6. A **counter C1** increments per fill; at C1 = preset, a **batch-complete indicator** is lit.
>
> **Ladder Rungs (3 marks):** Refer to Section 3.2 (Rungs 1–14). The student must draw at least 8 rungs: Master latch, Solenoid control, Timer start, Settle timer, Conveyor restart, Counter, Overload buzzer, Ready lamp. Each rung carries 0.4 mark, with a bonus 0.2 mark for the global enable flag.
>
> **Function of Each Rung (within the 3 marks):** Rung 1 = Start/Stop seal-in. Rung 3 = Bottle-detected conveyor drop. Rung 5 = Solenoid ON while bottle present and timer not done. Rung 7 = Settle timer. Rung 8 = Restart conveyor. Rung 9 = Counter. Rung 12 = Buzzer on overload.

> **Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Step 1 — Identify the given quantities (1 mark):**
> $V = 750$ mL $= 0.75$ L; $Q = 3$ L/min.
>
> **Step 2 — Apply the volumetric equation (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> V &= Q \cdot T_{fill} \\
> T_{fill} &= \dfrac{V}{Q} \\
> T_{fill} &= \dfrac{0.75 \text{ L}}{3 \text{ L/min}} \\
> T_{fill} &= 0.25 \text{ min}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Convert to seconds (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> T_{fill} &= 0.25 \text{ min} \times 60 \text{ s/min} \\
> T_{fill} &= 15 \text{ s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 4 — PLC Timer Preset (1 mark):**
> Set the TON timer's Preset Value (PV) to **15** (assuming a 1-second time base). With a +10% safety margin for production: $PV = 15 \times 1.10 = 16.5$ s ≈ **17 s** (round up to avoid under-filling).
>
> **Step 5 — Effect of Higher Timer Preset (2 marks):**
> If the timer preset is set **higher** than 15 s (e.g., 20 s):
> - The solenoid remains open **longer** than required.
> - The bottle will be **over-filled** → product wastage, customer complaints, possible overflow and spillage on the conveyor.
> - Excess liquid may **leak onto the conveyor belt**, creating a **slip hazard** and **hygiene failure**.
> - In a paid-by-volume system, the company **loses revenue** per bottle.
> - The downstream level sensor (if present) would have already tripped at the correct level; a higher timer preset means the solenoid fights against a closed state, potentially damaging the valve.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option 2)

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat ladder diagram, explain the PLC-based sequential starting of three motors M1, M2, and M3 with a 10-second delay between each. Include the necessary interlock conditions and overload protection.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the working of an automatic **Star-Delta starter** using a PLC with a neat ladder diagram. Why is a changeover dead time (T2) necessary between Star-OFF and Delta-ON?

> **Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Ladder Diagram (5 marks):** A typical 8-rung ladder program is expected.
>
> - **Rung 1:** `PB_START` (NO) ∥ `M1_AUX` (NO) — `PB_STOP` (NC) — `OL_TRIP` (NC) → coil `M1` (output to contactor K1). **(1 mark for seal-in)**
> - **Rung 2:** NO `M1` → start `T1` (TON, PV = 10 s). **(0.5 mark)**
> - **Rung 3:** NO `M1_AUX` AND NO `T1.DN` → coil `M2`. **(1 mark — depends on M1 being ON and T1 expired)**
> - **Rung 4:** NO `M2` → start `T2` (TON, PV = 10 s). **(0.5 mark)**
> - **Rung 5:** NO `M2_AUX` AND NO `T2.DN` → coil `M3`. **(1 mark)**
> - **Rung 6:** NO `M1` AND NO `M2` AND NO `M3` → coil `L_ALL` (all-running lamp). **(0.5 mark)**
> - **Rung 7:** NO `OL_TRIP` → coil `BUZZER` (fault alarm). **(0.5 mark)**
>
> **Interlock Conditions (1 mark):**
> - **Motor-to-motor interlock:** Each motor only starts if the previous motor's auxiliary contact is closed (i.e., previous motor is confirmed running). This prevents out-of-order starting.
> - **Overload interlock:** A single `OL_TRIP` NC contact is placed in series with the start rung, cutting off all motors if any overload relay trips.
> - **Stop interlock:** `PB_STOP` (NC) is in series with the master latch, de-energizing all motors when pressed.
>
> **Working Description (1 mark):** At t = 0, M1 starts immediately. At t = 10 s, T1 done → M2 starts. At t = 20 s, T2 done → M3 starts. The "All Running" lamp illuminates when all three are ON. Pressing STOP resets the entire cascade.

> **Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Ladder Diagram (3 marks):** A 6-rung program is expected.
>
> - **Rung 1:** `PB_START` (NO) ∥ `K_M_AUX` (NO) — `PB_STOP` (NC) — `OL` (NC) → coil `K_M` (Main contactor). **(0.5 mark)**
> - **Rung 2:** NO `K_M_AUX` AND NC `T1.DN` AND NC `K_D_AUX` (interlock) → coil `K_S` (Star contactor). **(0.5 mark)**
> - **Rung 3:** NO `K_M_AUX` AND NO `K_S_AUX` → start `T1` (TON, PV = 5 s typical). **(0.5 mark)**
> - **Rung 4:** NO `T1.DN` AND NC `K_D_AUX` → RES (reset) `K_S` and start `T2` (TON, PV = 100 ms). **(0.5 mark)**
> - **Rung 5:** NO `T2.DN` AND NC `K_S_AUX` (interlock) → coil `K_D` (Delta contactor). **(1 mark)**
>
> **Working (2 marks):**
> 1. START pressed → K_M and K_S energize simultaneously → motor windings connected in Star → each phase receives $V_{line} / \sqrt{3} \approx 230$ V (on a 400 V supply) → motor starts with **1/3 inrush current** and **1/3 starting torque**.
> 2. Timer T1 (≈ 5 s) gives the motor time to accelerate to ~80% of full speed in Star mode.
> 3. T1 done → K_S de-energizes → motor is briefly disconnected (T2 dead time ≈ 100 ms) → K_D energizes → motor windings reconnected in Delta → each phase receives full $V_{line}$ = 400 V → motor runs at full torque and speed.
> 4. STOP → all contactors de-energize → motor coasts to rest.
>
> **Why Changeover Dead Time T2 is Necessary (2 marks):**
> - During the changeover from Star to Delta, there is a brief moment when the motor must be **disconnected from the supply**. If K_S (Star) and K_D (Delta) were to be energized **simultaneously**, two different phases of the supply would be shorted together **through the motor windings**, causing a **phase-to-phase short circuit**.
> - The dead time T2 (typically 50–100 ms) ensures that the **arc across the K_S contactor has fully extinguished** before K_D closes.
> - The NC auxiliary contact of K_S in series with K_D's coil provides a **hardware-level interlock** that is independent of the PLC scan time, providing a **redundant safety layer**.
> - Additionally, the dead time allows the **rotor flux to decay slightly**, preventing an inrush transient when Delta is re-applied.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **In the Star-Delta ladder, do NOT forget the K_S NC contact in series with K_D's coil.** This is the single most common error. KTU examiners specifically scan for this contact — missing it costs a full 1–2 marks.
> 2. **In sequential motor starting, the timer must start ONLY AFTER the previous motor's auxiliary contact closes, not at t = 0.** A common mistake is starting T1 simultaneously with M1, which is technically correct in this case, but T2 must be started only after M2's auxiliary contact closes.
> 3. **Always include the Overload Relay (OL) NC contact** in the master rung. KTU considers a program without OL protection to be **unsafe and incomplete**, costing 1 mark.
> 4. **In the bottle filling I/O table, address the photoelectric sensor as a DI (digital input), not as an analog input.** Photoelectric sensors are digital by nature.
> 5. **Always state the units** (seconds, mL, L/min) in numerical answers. Marks are frequently deducted for unitless final answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Quick-Recall Bullets (Print This Before the Exam):**

- ☐ A **Bottle Filling System** = Conveyor (motor) + Sensor (photoelectric) + Solenoid (valve) + Timer + Counter, all orchestrated by a PLC.
- ☐ The **fill equation** is $V = Q \cdot T_{fill}$; always convert $T_{fill}$ to **seconds** for PLC timer presets.
- ☐ **Conveyor and Solenoid must be interlocked** — the conveyor stops when a bottle is under the nozzle.
- ☐ A **settle timer (1 s typical)** prevents post-fill drips; never skip it in your ladder.
- ☐ **Counters (CTU)** track production batches; preset the counter to the desired batch size.
- ☐ **Sequential motor starting** is preferred when multiple motors share a transformer — peak inrush drops by a factor of $n$ (number of motors).
- ☐ The **cascade pattern** is: START → M1 → T1 → M2 → T2 → M3 → LAMP. The first motor needs no delay.
- ☐ **Forward/Reverse control** requires **electrical interlock** (NC contacts of the opposite contactor in the coil path) PLUS **mechanical interlock** (physical lever on the contactor pair).
- ☐ A **1-second time delay** between Forward-OFF and Reverse-ON prevents the plugging current surge.
- ☐ **Star-Delta** reduces starting current and torque to **1/3 of DOL values**. Suitable only for **low-torque** loads.
- ☐ The **changeover dead time T2 (50–100 ms)** between K_S OFF and K_D ON is **non-negotiable** — it prevents a phase-to-phase short circuit.
- ☐ The **K_S NC contact in series with K_D's coil** is the **interlock**; its absence is the #1 cause of failed Star-Delta ladders in KTU exams.
- ☐ Inrush current of a 3-phase induction motor at DOL: $I_{start} = (5 \text{ to } 7) \cdot I_{FL}$.
- ☐ Three-phase power: $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta$. Always use this in motor numericals.
- ☐ **Overload Relay (OL) NC contact** in the master rung is a **mandatory safety element** — missing it = −1 mark.
- ☐ **Hardwired E-Stop** is **outside the PLC** for fail-safe operation, even if the PLC CPU crashes.
- ☐ Typical PLC scan time: **10–20 ms** — fast enough for sequential control but **not** for high-speed safety interlocks (use dedicated safety relays for those).

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Bottle Filling System and Sequential Switching of Motors**
> **Total Estimated Self-Study Time:** 6 hours (2 hours theory + 2 hours ladder practice + 2 hours numericals)
> **Confidence Target for KTU ESE:** ≥ 80% on the 14-mark question; ≥ 90% on Part A.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Control - Need of Computer in a Control System - Functional Block Diagram of a Computer Control System - Data Loggers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT612 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computer Control - Need of Computer in a Control System - Functional Block Diagram of a Computer Control System - Data Loggers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Computer Control System?

A **Computer Control System (CCS)** is a digital, programmable control architecture in which a digital computer (microprocessor, microcontroller, industrial PC, or PLC) acts as the central decision-making element, replacing conventional analog controllers. The computer receives real-world process variables through sensors, processes the information using stored algorithms, and issues control commands to final control elements through actuators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A Computer Control System is a closed-loop feedback configuration in which a digital computer performs the functions of measurement, comparison, computation, and correction by executing a stored control algorithm on sampled, quantized process data.

### 1.2 The Human Nervous System Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car on a hilly road:

| Biological Element | Computer Control Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Eyes & Skin | Sensors / Transducers | Measure road slope, speed, temperature |
| Spinal Cord | Signal Conditioning & ADC | Converts raw nerve signals into clean digital impulses |
| Brain | CPU + Memory + Control Algorithm | Compares actual vs desired, decides action |
| Motor Nerves | DAC + Actuator Interface | Transmits commands to muscles |
| Muscles & Limbs | Actuators (Valves, Motors, Heaters) | Physically manipulates the process |
| Memory (Hippocampus) | RAM / ROM / Hard Disk | Stores set-points, logs, programs |

Just as your brain processes thousands of sensory inputs per second and coordinates your body without you consciously thinking about every muscle, a computer control system continuously monitors many process variables, runs complex calculations (PID, fuzzy logic, MPC), and drives multiple actuators simultaneously — but with far higher speed, precision, and reliability than any human.

### 1.3 Need for a Computer in a Control System

Modern industrial processes have evolved beyond the capability of purely analog or single-loop pneumatic controllers. The following **drivers** mandate the inclusion of a digital computer:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Pillars Justifying Computer Control:**
> 1. **Multi-variable Capability** — Hundreds of I/O points handled by one CPU.
> 2. **Complex Control Algorithms** — PID, cascade, feed-forward, adaptive, fuzzy, neural network.
> 3. **Flexibility & Re-programmability** — Set-points and control logic modified via software, not rewiring.
> 4. **Data Logging & Historization** — Continuous archiving for SCADA, MES, and regulatory compliance.
> 5. **Communication & Networking** — Native integration with fieldbuses (Profibus, Modbus, Ethernet/IP) and the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT).

### 1.4 What is a Data Logger?

A **Data Logger** is a standalone, microprocessor-based electronic instrument dedicated to the **time-stamped recording of one or more process variables** over extended periods. It typically features internal memory, a real-time clock (RTC), an ADC, multiplexer, and communication ports, but unlike a SCADA system or DCS, it primarily focuses on **acquisition and storage** rather than closed-loop control.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Centric Definition:** A data logger is a specialized, single-purpose computer system used to scan, digitize, store, and retrieve process measurements at programmable intervals, providing the historical dataset necessary for offline analysis, quality audits, and predictive maintenance.

### 1.5 Real-World Applications of Data Loggers

- Pharmaceutical cold-chain temperature monitoring (FDA 21 CFR Part 11).
- HVAC energy profiling in commercial buildings.
- Weather stations (atmospheric pressure, humidity, wind speed).
- Structural health monitoring of bridges and dams.
- Automotive crash-test telemetry.
- Power quality logging (voltage sags, harmonics, transients).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Roles of the Computer in a Control Loop

The computer performs the **"Three C's"** within every control cycle:

1. **Collect** — Periodically sample the analog/digital inputs.
2. **Compute** — Execute the control algorithm: 
$$u(k) = K_p \, e(k) + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + K_d \left[ e(k) - e(k-1) \right]$$
3. **Command** — Output the manipulated variable to the actuator.

### 2.2 Comparison: Analog vs Computer Control

| Parameter | Analog Controller | Computer Control System |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ±0.5 % to ±1 % FS | ±0.01 % to ±0.05 % FS |
| Flexibility | Fixed by hardware | Reconfigurable in software |
| Drift | Susceptible to thermal drift | Essentially drift-free (digital) |
| Multi-loop Support | One controller per loop | Hundreds of loops per CPU |
| Data Logging | Manual chart recorders | Automatic, time-stamped, searchable |
| Communication | Mostly stand-alone | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Fieldbus, OPC-UA |
| Initial Cost | Low for 1–2 loops | Higher, but lower per-loop cost |
| Complexity | Low | Requires software engineering discipline |

### 2.3 Computer Control System — Operational Logic Flow

The computer control loop executes the following sequence continuously:

- **Step 1 — Sensor Sampling:** Physical variable (T, P, F, L) sensed by transducer.
- **Step 2 — Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, filtering, linearization, cold-junction compensation.
- **Step 3 — Multiplexing (MUX):** Multiple conditioned signals time-shared to a single ADC.
- **Step 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Continuous amplitude → discrete binary code.
- **Step 5 — Software Processing:** CPU executes control algorithm on the digitized error signal.
- **Step 6 — Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC):** Binary output → analog voltage/current.
- **Step 7 — Actuator Drive:** Control element adjusts the process.
- **Step 8 — Display & Logging:** HMI/SCADA visualizes; data archived.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|
| ADC Resolution (Voltage) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | $V_{FS}$ = Full-scale voltage (V), $n$ = ADC bits |
| ADC Resolution (Process) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_{max} - P_{min}}{2^n - 1}$ | $P$ = Process variable (°C, bar, etc.) |
| Quantization Error | $e_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2}$ | Half of one LSB |
| Nyquist Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 \, f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sampling frequency (Hz), $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency |
| Anti-alias Filter Cut-off | $f_c \leq \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Required to prevent aliasing |
| Storage Capacity | $M = N_{ch} \times f_s \times t \times b$ | $N_{ch}$ = channels, $f_s$ = Hz, $t$ = duration (s), $b$ = bits/sample |
| Throughput Rate | $T_r = \dfrac{1}{N_{ch} \cdot \tau_{ADC}}$ | $\tau_{ADC}$ = conversion time per channel (s) |
| Shannon-Hartley (informational) | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \tfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Channel capacity (bps), bandwidth $B$ (Hz), SNR |
| Discrete PID Output | $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i \sum e(j) + K_d [e(k) - e(k-1)]$ | $e(k) = SP - PV$ |
| Bit Rate (serial logging) | $R_b = N_{ch} \cdot f_s \cdot b$ | Bits per second (bps) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas govern *every* data acquisition design — from selecting an ADC chip (resolution vs cost trade-off) to sizing an SD card for one year of continuous logging in a remote oil-pipeline station.

### 2.5 Data Logger — Types and Specifications

#### 2.5.1 Classification of Data Loggers

- **By Channel Count:**
  - Single-channel (handheld, low-cost).
  - Multi-channel (8, 16, 32, 64, 128 channels).
- **By Physical Variable:**
  - Temperature-only loggers (e.g., thermocouple, RTD).
  - Universal / multi-input loggers (V, mA, TC, RTD, strain, frequency).
- **By Architecture:**
  - **Stand-alone loggers** — battery powered, autonomous.
  - **PC-based loggers** — DAQ card + computer + software (LabVIEW, MATLAB).
  - **Networked loggers** — Ethernet/Wi-Fi, push to cloud (MQTT, REST API).
- **By Sampling Strategy:**
  - Continuous loggers.
  - Event-driven / triggered loggers.
  - Burst-mode loggers.

#### 2.5.2 Key Specifications a KTU Student Must Know

| Specification | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|
| ADC Resolution | 12-bit to 24-bit |
| Sampling Rate | 1 sample/hour to 100 kSamples/s |
| Memory | 8 MB internal to 1 TB SD/SSD |
| Input Types | ±10 V, 4–20 mA, TC (J,K,T,E,R,S,B), RTD (Pt100, Pt1000) |
| Isolation | 1500 V RMS channel-to-channel & channel-to-ground |
| Communication | USB, RS-485, RS-232, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, GSM/4G |
| Battery Life | 3 years (low-power sleep mode) |
| Operating Temperature | −40 °C to +85 °C (industrial grade) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **refinery distillation column**, computer control regulates reflux ratio, reboiler duty, and top/bottom temperatures with precision unattainable manually. The data logger simultaneously archives these variables for **ASME Section VIII** compliance audits, enabling the process engineer to perform **root-cause analysis** weeks after an upset occurred.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Resolution and Quantization Step Size

**Problem:** A 12-bit ADC is used in a data logger with a full-scale input range of $0$ to $\,10 \, \text{V}$. Compute the resolution in volts and the quantization error. Also determine the resolution in °C if the sensor spans $0$ °C to $500$ °C.

**Given:**
- $n = 12$ bits
- $V_{FS} = 10 \, \text{V}$
- Sensor span: $0 \le T \le 500 \, °\text{C}$

**Solution — Step 1: Total number of discrete levels**

$$
N_{levels} = 2^n = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

**Step 2: Voltage Resolution (Size of 1 LSB)**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{10 \, \text{V}}{4096 - 1} = \frac{10}{4095}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta V \approx 2.442 \, \text{mV/LSB}}
$$

**Step 3: Quantization Error**

$$
e_q = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm 1.221 \, \text{mV}
$$

**Step 4: Resolution in °C**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{T_{max} - T_{min}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{500 - 0}{4095}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T \approx 0.1221 \, °\text{C/LSB}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** One LSB corresponds to a temperature step of $0.1221 \, °\text{C}$. The smallest measurable change is therefore $\pm 0.061 \, °\text{C}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nyquist Sampling for a Data Logger

**Problem:** A vibration data logger must capture a machine fault signature at $f_{max} = 8 \, \text{kHz}$. Compute:
1. Minimum sampling frequency.
2. Maximum anti-alias filter cut-off.
3. Memory required for **4 channels**, **10 seconds**, **16-bit** samples.

**Given:**
- $f_{max} = 8000 \, \text{Hz}$
- $N_{ch} = 4$
- $t = 10 \, \text{s}$
- $b = 16 \, \text{bits/sample}$

**Solution — Step 1: Minimum Sampling Frequency**

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 8000 = 16 \, \text{kHz}
$$

Taking a practical safety factor of $2.5 \times$:

$$
f_s = 2.5 \times 8000 = 20 \, \text{kHz}
$$

**Step 2: Anti-alias Cut-off Frequency**

$$
f_c \le \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{20\,000}{2} = 10 \, \text{kHz}
$$

Choose a 4th-order Butterworth with $f_c = 9.5 \, \text{kHz}$ (margin for roll-off).

**Step 3: Memory Calculation**

$$
M = N_{ch} \times f_s \times t \times b
$$

$$
M = 4 \times 20\,000 \times 10 \times 16
$$

$$
M = 12\,800\,000 \, \text{bits}
$$

Converting to bytes:

$$
M = \frac{12\,800\,000}{8} = 1\,600\,000 \, \text{B} \approx 1.6 \, \text{MB}
$$

$$
\boxed{M \approx 1.6 \, \text{MB}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** In practice, engineers add 30–40 % overhead for headers, timestamps, and CRC checksums, bringing the SD card requirement to about **2.5 MB** for a 10-second burst.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Discrete-Time PID Position-Form Output

**Problem:** A computer-controlled temperature chamber uses a PID algorithm with $K_p = 2.5$, $K_i = 0.8$, $K_d = 0.05$. The error history is:

| Sample $k$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $e(k)$ | 0 | 4 | 6 | 3 |

Compute the controller output at $k = 3$. Use $T_s = 0.1 \, \text{s}$ and integral accumulator: $\sum_{j=0}^{3} e(j) = 13$.

**Solution — Step 1: Proportional Term**

$$
P = K_p \, e(3) = 2.5 \times 3 = 7.5
$$

**Step 2: Integral Term**

$$
I = K_i \sum_{j=0}^{3} e(j) = 0.8 \times 13 = 10.4
$$

**Step 3: Derivative Term**

$$
D = K_d \left[ e(3) - e(2) \right] = 0.05 \times (3 - 6) = -0.15
$$

**Step 4: Total Output**

$$
u(3) = P + I + D = 7.5 + 10.4 - 0.15
$$

$$
\boxed{u(3) = 17.75}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Computer Control vs Data Logger

| Attribute | Computer Control System | Data Logger |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Purpose | Real-time closed-loop regulation | Time-stamped archival recording |
| Output Path | DAC → Actuator | Memory → Display / Network |
| Sampling | Synchronous, deterministic | May be continuous, burst, or event-triggered |
| Algorithm | PID, MPC, Fuzzy, Adaptive, Neural | Mostly none (capture only) |
| Latency Requirement | Strict (sub-millisecond) | Relaxed (seconds to minutes) |
| Memory Need | Small (current values + history) | Large (days, months, years of records) |
| Regulatory Role | Active control & safety interlocks | Audit trail & traceability |
| Example Product | DCS, PLC, Industrial PC | Testo 174H, NI CompactDAQ, Keysight 34970A |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Computer Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Plant] --> B[Sensor 1]
    A2[Process Plant] --> C[Sensor 2]
    B --> D[Signal Conditioning]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Multiplexer MUX]
    E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    F --> G[Digital Computer CPU with RAM and ROM]
    G --> H[Control Algorithm PID Fuzzy MPC]
    H --> I[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    I --> J[Actuator Driver]
    J --> K[Final Control Element Valve Heater Motor]
    K --> A
    G --> L[Human Machine Interface HMI]
    G --> M[Data Logger Module]
    G --> N[Communication Port Ethernet RS485]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The outer loop (Process → Sensor → ADC → Computer → DAC → Actuator → Process) is the **closed feedback loop**. The computer simultaneously branches to the HMI for visualization, the data logger for archival, and the communication port for supervisory networking.

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Data Logger

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Sensor Type J Thermocouple] --> SC[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Cold Junction Comp]
    S2[Sensor Pt100 RTD] --> SC
    S3[Sensor 4 to 20 mA Transmitter] --> SC
    SC --> MUX[Analog Multiplexer]
    MUX --> ADC[16 or 24 bit ADC]
    ADC --> CPU[Microcontroller with Real Time Clock RTC]
    CPU --> MEM[Non Volatile Memory Flash SD Card]
    CPU --> LCD[Local LCD Display and Keypad]
    CPU --> USB[USB Interface]
    CPU --> ETH[Ethernet Port]
    CPU --> WIF[Wi Fi or GSM Modem]
    CPU --> PWR[Battery Management and Isolation]
```

### 4.3 Computer Control Hierarchy (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Field Level Sensors and Actuators]
    L1[Direct Digital Control DDC Loop]
    L2[Supervisory Computer SCADA]
    L3[Plant Management MES]
    L4[Enterprise Resource Planning ERP]
    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4
    L2 --> DL[Central Data Historian Archival Database]
    L1 --> DL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The data logger sits at the **L0/L1 interface**, capturing every millisecond of field activity so that upper layers (SCADA, MES) can perform trend analysis, statistical process control (SPC), and regulatory reporting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the need for a computer in a modern industrial control system. List any four advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
A computer is needed in a modern control system to handle **multi-variable, complex, and high-speed** regulation tasks that exceed the capability of analog controllers.

*Four key advantages:*
1. **Complex algorithm execution** — PID, cascade, feed-forward, fuzzy, and adaptive control in software.
2. **Re-programmability** — Set-points and control logic changed via software without rewiring.
3. **Multi-loop management** — Hundreds of loops handled by a single CPU, reducing per-loop cost.
4. **Built-in data logging and networking** — Native SCADA/MES/IIoT connectivity and time-stamped archival.

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a data logger. Mention its four main functional blocks.**

**Model Answer:**
A **data logger** is a microprocessor-based instrument used to scan, digitize, store, and retrieve one or more process variables over time, with time-stamped records suitable for offline analysis.

*Four main functional blocks:*
1. **Signal Conditioning Unit** — Amplification, filtering, cold-junction compensation.
2. **Multiplexer + ADC** — Sequential selection and digitization of multiple inputs.
3. **Microcontroller with RTC** — Time-stamping, scheduling, and memory management.
4. **Non-volatile Memory & Communication Port** — Flash/SD storage plus USB/Ethernet/Wi-Fi export.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**(a)** Draw and explain the **functional block diagram of a computer control system**. Label every block and describe the role of the ADC, DAC, CPU, and actuator interface. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 16-bit data logger is connected to a pressure transmitter with a range of **0 to 10 bar**. Compute the **(i)** resolution in mbar/LSB, **(ii)** quantization error in mbar, and **(iii)** the percentage resolution with respect to full scale. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Explanation:**

The functional block diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1) comprises the following sequential blocks:

1. **Process / Plant** — The physical system whose variables (T, P, flow, level) are to be controlled.
2. **Sensor / Transducer** — Converts physical quantity into a low-level electrical signal (mV, mA).
3. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplification, linearization, filtering, cold-junction compensation (for TCs), and isolation.
4. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — Time-shares multiple inputs into one ADC channel.
5. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Quantizes the continuous signal into discrete binary codes.
6. **Digital Computer (CPU + Memory + I/O)** — Executes the control algorithm on the digitized error signal.
7. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** — Converts the binary output into a continuous voltage/current.
8. **Actuator / Final Control Element** — Physically manipulates the process (valve, motor, heater).
9. **HMI / Data Logger / Communication Port** — Visualization, archival, and networking.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly *forget to draw the feedback arrow from the actuator back to the process*. Without it, the loop is technically open and **3 marks are deducted**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $n = 16$ bits, $P_{FS} = 0$ to $10 \, \text{bar}$

**Step 1: Number of Levels**

$$
N = 2^{16} = 65\,536
$$

**Step 2: Resolution in bar/LSB**

$$
\Delta P = \frac{P_{max} - P_{min}}{2^{16} - 1} = \frac{10 - 0}{65\,535}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 1.5259 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar/LSB}
$$

Converting to mbar (1 bar = 1000 mbar):

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = 0.15259 \, \text{mbar/LSB}}
$$

**Step 3: Quantization Error**

$$
e_q = \pm \frac{\Delta P}{2} = \pm 0.0763 \, \text{mbar}
$$

**Step 4: Percentage Resolution**

$$
\%R = \frac{\Delta P}{P_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{1.5259 \times 10^{-4}}{10} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\%R = 0.001526 \, \% \, \text{of Full Scale}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** Many students mistakenly use $2^n$ in the denominator instead of $2^n - 1$. The exact textbook convention uses $2^n - 1$ for resolution. **Off-by-one error → loss of 1 mark.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **analog controllers** and **computer-based control systems** under the following heads: **(i)** accuracy, **(ii)** flexibility, **(iii)** data logging, **(iv)** communication, **(v)** complexity, **(vi)** cost-per-loop, **(vii)** drift. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **multi-channel data logger** with a neat block diagram. A data logger samples **8 channels** at **2 kHz per channel** with **12-bit resolution**. Compute the **(i)** total throughput in kSamples/s, **(ii)** data generation rate in kB/s, and **(iii)** storage required for **30 minutes** of continuous recording. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparative Table:**

| Head | Analog Controller | Computer Control System |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Accuracy | ±0.5 % to ±1 % FS | ±0.01 % to ±0.05 % FS |
| (ii) Flexibility | Hardware-fixed | Software-reconfigurable |
| (iii) Data Logging | Manual chart recorders | Automatic, time-stamped |
| (iv) Communication | Mostly isolated | Ethernet, Fieldbus, OPC-UA |
| (v) Complexity | Low | Requires software engineering |
| (vi) Cost-per-Loop | Economical for 1–2 loops | Economical for many loops |
| (vii) Drift | Susceptible to thermal & aging drift | Essentially drift-free |

**Part (b) — Multi-channel Data Logger Working + Calculation:**

**Working Principle:**
A multi-channel data logger sequentially selects each input through an analog multiplexer, digitizes it via a high-resolution ADC, time-stamps it with the RTC, and stores the binary code in non-volatile memory. Microcontroller firmware controls scan rate, channel sequence, and trigger conditions.

**Given:** $N_{ch} = 8$, $f_s = 2000 \, \text{Hz/channel}$, $n = 12 \, \text{bits}$, $t = 30 \, \text{min} = 1800 \, \text{s}$

**Step 1: Total Throughput**

$$
T_r = N_{ch} \times f_s = 8 \times 2000 = 16\,000 \, \text{samples/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_r = 16 \, \text{kSamples/s}}
$$

**Step 2: Data Generation Rate**

$$
R_b = T_r \times n = 16\,000 \times 12 = 192\,000 \, \text{bits/s}
$$

Converting to kB/s:

$$
R_b = \frac{192\,000}{8 \times 1000} = 24 \, \text{kB/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_b = 24 \, \text{kB/s}}
$$

**Step 3: Storage for 30 Minutes**

$$
M = R_b \times t = 24\,000 \, \text{B/s} \times 1800 \, \text{s}
$$

$$
M = 43\,200\,000 \, \text{B} = 43.2 \, \text{MB}
$$

$$
\boxed{M = 43.2 \, \text{MB}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert bits to bytes by dividing by 8. **Loss of 1 mark.** Always state units explicitly — *kSamples/s*, *kB/s*, *MB*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3**
>
> - **Computer Control System** = Digital computer replaces analog controller in the loop.
> - **Five Needs:** multi-variable, complex algorithms, flexibility, data logging, communication.
> - **Block sequence:** Sensor → Conditioning → MUX → ADC → Computer → DAC → Actuator → Process.
> - **ADC Resolution:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ (note the −1).
> - **Quantization Error:** $\pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2}$ (half-LSB).
> - **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; always pair with anti-alias filter.
> - **Data Logger** = Standalone, time-stamped archival instrument; **not** a closed-loop controller.
> **Four blocks of a data logger:** Conditioning → MUX+ADC → MCU+RTC → Memory+Comm.
> - **Storage formula:** $M = N_{ch} \cdot f_s \cdot t \cdot b$ (watch your units!).
> - **Throughput:** $T_r = N_{ch} \cdot f_{s,\text{per channel}}$.
> - **Bit-rate to Byte-rate:** divide by 8.
> - **Comparison memory trick:** Computer control = *controls + logs*; Data logger = *logs only*.
> - **Industrial ranges to memorize:** 12–24-bit ADC; 4–20 mA standard current loop; ±10 V standard voltage range; Pt100 RTD; TC types J, K, T, E.
> - **Examiner red flags:** missing feedback arrow in block diagram, using $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$, omitting anti-alias filter, ignoring units in storage calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervisory Computer Control - Direct Digital Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT612 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supervisory Computer Control - Direct Digital Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — Supervisory Computer Control & Direct Digital Control (DDC)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Digital Control (DDC)** is a method of process control in which a **digital computer (or microprocessor-based controller such as a PLC/DCS controller)** directly carries out the regulatory control functions — measurement, comparison, and correction — for multiple control loops in a process, replacing the conventional analog controllers (PID hardware controllers) with software-implemented control algorithms executed in sampled-time fashion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supervisory Computer Control (SCC)** is the **topmost hierarchical level** of a computer-based process automation pyramid, in which a central host computer monitors, coordinates, optimizes, and overrides the set-points and operating parameters of the lower-level DDC controllers, while the DDC controllers continue to perform the real-time closed-loop regulatory control.

In simple engineering language:
- **DDC** = The computer *is* the controller (it does PID math directly).
- **SCC** = The computer *watches over* the controller (it sets targets, monitors performance, and decides operating strategy).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Cruise Control of a Modern Ship"**
>
> Imagine a large modern ship at sea.
>
> - The **DDC** is the **helmsman (or autopilot)** who, every few seconds, looks at the compass reading, compares it with the desired heading, and turns the rudder by a calculated amount. He does the *actual* steering thousands of times per hour, by himself.
> - The **SCC** is the **Captain on the bridge**, who is not turning the wheel every second. Instead, he tells the helmsman: *"Maintain a heading of 270° and a speed of 18 knots."* If the sea conditions change, the Captain may issue a **new set-point** ("Reduce speed to 12 knots — storm ahead"). The Captain also watches long-term charts, fuel usage, and weather — things the helmsman cannot worry about.
>
> The Captain never directly moves the rudder; the helmsman never decides the destination. That is exactly how SCC and DDC collaborate in an industrial plant.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology to Remember

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Sampled-Data System** | A system in which signals are updated only at discrete instants (multiples of the sampling period **T**). |
| **Sampling Period (T)** | The fixed time interval (typically **0.1 s to 60 s**, in KTU problems often **T = 0.1 s or 1 s**) between two successive samples. |
| **Sampling Frequency (f_s)** | $f_s = 1/T$ in **Hz**. Must satisfy **Shannon's Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. |
| **Quantization** | Converting a continuous amplitude into a discrete digital word. |
| **Control Loop** | A single closed feedback path (sensor → controller → final control element → process → sensor). |
| **Set-Point (SP)** | The desired value of the controlled variable. |
| **Process Variable (PV)** | The measured actual value of the controlled variable. |
| **Error (e)** | $e = SP - PV$ |
| **Manipulated Variable (MV)** | The output of the controller sent to the final control element. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** — A **DDC controller typically handles 8 to 64 control loops simultaneously** in a time-shared (multiplexed) fashion, with **T = 0.1 s to 10 s** per loop, executing the PID algorithm for each loop once every sampling instant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Computer Control Hierarchy (Automation Pyramid)

Industrial computer control is organized into **distinct functional layers**, each with a defined scope and time-scale:

| Level | Name | Function | Time-Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 4** | **Enterprise / Management** | Scheduling, accounting, ERP, business decisions | Hours / Days |
| **Level 3** | **Supervisory Computer Control (SCC)** | Set-point optimization, alarms, trending, coordination | Seconds / Minutes |
| **Level 2** | **Direct Digital Control (DDC)** | Closed-loop regulatory control (PID, cascade, ratio) | **0.1 s – 10 s** |
| **Level 1** | **PLC / DCS Controller** | Discrete logic, sequencing, interlocking, batching | **10 ms – 100 ms** |
| **Level 0** | **Field Instrumentation** | Sensors, transmitters, valves, drives, motors | Continuous |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the hierarchy exists** — A single computer cannot do *all* tasks well. **Hard real-time** safety-critical control is done at the bottom (PLC/DDC), while **soft real-time** optimization and human-interface tasks are done at the top (SCC). This separation improves **reliability** and **fault tolerance**.

## 2.2 Architecture of a DDC System

A DDC system contains the following functional blocks (mapped in Section 4):

1. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — sequentially switches many analog inputs into one A/D converter.
2. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — holds the analog value constant during conversion.
3. **A/D Converter** — quantizes the analog value into a digital word.
4. **Digital Computer (CPU + Memory)** — runs the PID algorithm for *each* loop.
5. **D/A Converter (with Zero-Order Hold)** — converts the digital output back to analog.
6. **De-Multiplexer / Output Register** — routes the analog output to the correct final control element.

## 2.3 Why "Direct" Digital Control?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **analog control**, each loop has a dedicated hardware PID card. For 50 loops, you need 50 hardware PID cards.
>
> In **Direct Digital Control**, a single digital computer (or PLC CPU) *directly* computes the PID output for *all* 50 loops, one after another, in a time-multiplexed way. The output of the computer goes **directly** to the final control element (valve, drive) — hence the word *Direct*.

## 2.4 The Two Canonical DDC Algorithms (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.4.1 Position Algorithm (Absolute Algorithm)
The controller output is computed **as an absolute value** with respect to a known reference (e.g., fully-closed valve position).

$$u(k) = K_p \left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i}\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T}\bigl(e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr) \right]$$

- $u(k)$ = controller output at the $k^{th}$ sample.
- $K_p$ = proportional gain.
- $T_i$ = integral time constant (seconds).
- $T_d$ = derivative time constant (seconds).
- $T$ = sampling period (seconds).
- $e(k), e(k-1)$ = current and previous errors.

**Drawback** — Requires summing all past errors (memory grows with time). Used primarily for **set-point tracking applications**.

### 2.4.2 Velocity Algorithm (Incremental Algorithm)
The controller computes the **change (increment) in output** rather than the absolute output. This increment is sent to an **incremental actuator** (e.g., a stepping motor valve).

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p \bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] + \frac{K_p T}{T_i}\,e(k) + \frac{K_p T_d}{T}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr]$$

The actual output is then:

$$u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$$

**Advantages** — No history of errors is required, hence **bumpless transfer** and **anti-reset windup** are easier to implement. This is the form **most commonly used with PLC and DCS systems** in industry (because PLC outputs often control incremental / pulse outputs).

### 2.4.3 Discrete-Time PID (Parallel Form, KTU Board Favorite)
A more compact expression often used in KTU derivations:

$$u(k) = K_p\,e(k) + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + K_d \bigl(e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr)$$

where $K_i = K_p T / T_i$ and $K_d = K_p T_d / T$.

## 2.5 Supervisory Control Functions (SCC)

The supervisory computer performs **set-point optimization, mode management, and advanced control** over the DDC layer. Typical functions:

1. **Set-Point Scheduling** — Calculates new SP for DDC loops based on production rate, time of day, or upstream conditions.
2. **Gain Scheduling** — Changes $K_p, T_i, T_d$ of the DDC loop based on operating region (e.g., low-flow vs. high-flow regime).
3. **Cascade / Override / Feed-Forward Override** — Coordinates multiple DDC loops.
4. **Model Predictive Control (MPC) —** Solves an optimization problem on-line to give the best set-points to DDC.
5. **Alarm Management & Trending** — Logs, displays, and analyzes loop performance.
6. **Manual / Auto / Cascade Mode Switching** at the operator console.
7. **Data Archival & Reporting.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Distinction** — In SCC, the **SCC computer does NOT close the inner regulatory loop**. If the SCC computer fails, the DDC layer **continues to control the process** at the last-known set-point. This **fault-isolation** is the strongest engineering argument for hierarchical control.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(k) = SP - PV$ | Error at $k^{th}$ sample. |
| 2 | $f_s = 1/T$ | Sampling frequency in **Hz**. |
| 3 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Shannon / Nyquist sampling theorem. |
| 4 | $u(k) = K_p\left[e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i}\sum e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T}\Delta e(k)\right]$ | **Position Algorithm** (PID, ideal). |
| 5 | $\Delta u(k) = K_p\Delta e(k) + \frac{K_p T}{T_i} e(k) + \frac{K_p T_d}{T}\Delta^2 e(k)$ | **Velocity (Incremental) Algorithm.** |
| 6 | $u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$ | Output update rule. |
| 7 | $K_i = K_p\,T/T_i$ | Integral gain in discrete form. |
| 8 | $K_d = K_p\,T_d/T$ | Derivative gain in discrete form. |
| 9 | $D(z) = K_p\left[1 + \frac{T}{T_i}\frac{1}{1-z^{-1}} + \frac{T_d}{T}(1 - z^{-1})\right]$ | Discrete PID in **z-domain**. |
| 10 | $G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$ | Transfer function of Zero-Order Hold. |
| 11 | $t_r \leq T \leq t_r/10$ | Typical design rule: $T$ between $1/10^{th}$ and $1$ times the dominant time-constant $t_r$ of the process. |
| 12 | $n = T_{total}/T$ | Number of samples in a horizon $T_{total}$. |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility** — DDC is the backbone of modern **Building Management Systems (BMS)**, **HVAC control**, **Power Plant DCS**, **Petrochemical plants**, and **Robotics**. Almost every industrial process you see today — from a cement kiln to a sugar refinery to a passenger aircraft — uses a DDC layer under a supervisory computer.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Position Algorithm from the Continuous-Time PID

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Continuous PID Equation

The classical analog PID controller in the time domain is:

$$u(t) = K_p\left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d\,\frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

This equation is **continuous** in time — meaning the integral and derivative are evaluated for *every* instant $t \in [0, \infty)$. A digital computer cannot evaluate an integral or a derivative continuously, so we **approximate** them using numerical methods applied at discrete instants $t = kT$.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Approximation of the Integral Term

The integral over $[0, kT]$ is approximated by a **rectangular (forward Euler) sum**:

$$\int_0^{kT} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)$$

The summation takes the error values $e(0), e(T), e(2T), \dots, e(kT)$ and multiplies each by the width $T$ of the rectangle.

Hence the discrete integral term becomes:

$$\frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{kT} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; \frac{T}{T_i}\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Approximation of the Derivative Term

The derivative is approximated using a **backward finite-difference**:

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=kT} \;\approx\; \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T}$$

Substituting into the derivative term:

$$T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{T_d}{T}\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr]$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Substitute Both Approximations into the PID

Replacing the integral and derivative terms in the original PID expression with their discrete approximations:

$$u(k) = K_p\left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i}\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T}\bigl(e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr) \right]$$

This is the **Position Algorithm**, the canonical KTU Board formula.

**Conversion logic** — Replace the continuous integral with a rectangular sum, replace the continuous derivative with a finite-difference, and evaluate everything at the discrete instant $kT$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Velocity (Incremental) Algorithm

### 3.2.1 Start from the Position Algorithm

We begin with the position algorithm derived above:

$$u(k) = K_p\left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i}\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T}\bigl(e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr) \right]$$

### 3.2.2 Write the Same Equation for the Previous Sample

Apply the same equation to sample $k-1$:

$$u(k-1) = K_p\left[ e(k-1) + \frac{T}{T_i}\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T}\bigl(e(k-1) - e(k-2)\bigr) \right]$$

### 3.2.3 Subtract to Obtain the Increment

Compute $\Delta u(k) = u(k) - u(k-1)$. The error-sum term becomes:

$$T\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) - T\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} e(j) = T\,e(k)$$

The proportional and derivative terms become:

$$K_p\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{K_p T_d}{T}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr]$$

Combining all three contributions:

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] + \frac{K_p T}{T_i}\,e(k) + \frac{K_p T_d}{T}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr]$$

### 3.2.4 Final Update Rule

The actual position output is obtained by **integrating the increments**:

$$u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$$

**Conversion logic** — Take two consecutive samples, subtract them, and the running sum cancels out, leaving only three terms that depend on the **last two or three errors**, not on the entire history. This is what makes the velocity algorithm suitable for PLCs.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem (KTU ESE Style)** — A DDC system samples a temperature loop with $T = 2\,\text{s}$, $K_p = 4$, $T_i = 10\,\text{s}$, $T_d = 1\,\text{s}$. The error sequence (in °C) is $e(0) = 0$, $e(1) = 5$, $e(2) = 3$, $e(3) = 1$. Compute the controller output $u(3)$ using the **position algorithm**.

### 3.3.1 Setup

We need $u(3) = K_p\left[ e(3) + \dfrac{T}{T_i}\sum_{j=0}^{3} e(j) + \dfrac{T_d}{T}\bigl(e(3) - e(2)\bigr) \right]$.

### 3.3.2 Evaluate the Sum

$$\sum_{j=0}^{3} e(j) = e(0) + e(1) + e(2) + e(3) = 0 + 5 + 3 + 1 = 9$$

### 3.3.3 Evaluate Each Term Inside the Bracket

- Proportional term: $e(3) = 1$
- Integral term: $\dfrac{T}{T_i}\sum e(j) = \dfrac{2}{10}\times 9 = 0.2 \times 9 = 1.8$
- Derivative term: $\dfrac{T_d}{T}\bigl(e(3) - e(2)\bigr) = \dfrac{1}{2}\times(1 - 3) = 0.5 \times (-2) = -1.0$

### 3.3.4 Combine

$$u(3) = 4 \times \bigl[ 1 + 1.8 + (-1.0) \bigr] = 4 \times 1.8 = 7.2$$

**Controller output $u(3) = 7.2$ (in the controller output units, e.g., % valve opening).**

## 3.4 Sampling Period Selection (Analytical Matrix)

| Criterion | Rule of Thumb |
|---|---|
| Nyquist | $T \leq 1 / (2 f_{max})$ where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency in the process spectrum. |
| Process Time-Constant | $T \approx 0.1 \tau_d$ to $0.5 \tau_d$ (where $\tau_d$ = dominant time-constant). |
| Dead-Time | $T \approx 0.25 \theta$ to $0.5 \theta$ (where $\theta$ = process dead-time). |
| Settling Time | At least **10 samples** in the rise-time / settling period. |
| Computational Load | Total CPU time per scan for all loops $< 60\%$ of $T$ (rule of thumb for schedulability). |
| Output Hold | Zero-Order-Hold introduces effective delay of $T/2$ — keep $T$ small for fast loops. |

## 4.1 Block Diagram — DDC System with SCC Layer (Hierarchical Architecture)

The following Mermaid block-level architecture diagram shows the **physical and functional** interaction between the **SCC layer (Level 3)**, the **DDC layer (Level 2)**, and the **field (Level 0/1)**.

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef scclayer fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ddclayer fill:#A9D0F5,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fieldlayer fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrlblock fill:#FFE082,stroke:#5D4037,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    SCC[SUPERVISORY COMPUTER LEVEL 3]:::scclayer
    SCREEN[OPERATOR HMI WORKSTATION]:::scclayer
    DB[PROCESS DATABASE AND HISTORIAN]:::scclayer
    OPT[SET POINT OPTIMIZER MODULE]:::scclayer

    DDC1[DDC CONTROLLER CPU PID ALGORITHM]:::ddclayer
    DDC2[DDC CONTROLLER CPU PID ALGORITHM LOOP 2]:::ddclayer
    DDCN[DDC CONTROLLER CPU PID ALGORITHM LOOP N]:::ddclayer

    MUX[MULTIPLEXER AND SAMPLE HOLD]:::ddclayer
    ADC[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]:::ddclayer
    DAC[DIGITAL TO ANALOG CONVERTER ZOH]:::ddclayer
    DMUX[OUTPUT DEMULTIPLEXER]:::ddclayer

    SENS1[TEMPERATURE TRANSMITTER]:::fieldlayer
    SENS2[PRESSURE TRANSMITTER]:::fieldlayer
    SENS3[FLOW TRANSMITTER]:::fieldlayer
    VALVE1[CONTROL VALVE LOOP 1]:::fieldlayer
    VALVE2[CONTROL VALVE LOOP 2]:::fieldlayer
    VALVEN[CONTROL VALVE LOOP N]:::fieldlayer

    SCC -->|SETPOINT AND MODE COMMANDS| DDC1
    SCC -->|SETPOINT AND MODE COMMANDS| DDC2
    SCC -->|SETPOINT AND MODE COMMANDS| DDCN
    SCREEN <-->|OPERATOR INPUTS AND ALARMS| SCC
    DB <-->|ARCHIVAL AND TRENDING| SCC
    OPT <-->|OPTIMIZATION DATA| SCC

    SENS1 --> MUX
    SENS2 --> MUX
    SENS3 --> MUX
    MUX --> ADC
    ADC -->|DIGITAL PV SIGNALS| DDC1
    ADC -->|DIGITAL PV SIGNALS| DDC2
    ADC -->|DIGITAL PV SIGNALS| DDCN

    DDC1 -->|MV1| DAC
    DDC2 -->|MV2| DAC
    DDCN -->|MVN| DAC
    DAC --> DMUX
    DMUX --> VALVE1
    DMUX --> VALVE2
    DMUX --> VALVEN

    VALVE1 --> PROC
    VALVE2 --> PROC
    VALVEN --> PROC
    PROC --> SENS1
    PROC --> SENS2
    PROC --> SENS3
```

**Reading the diagram** — The supervisory computer (top) sets targets for the DDC controllers. Each DDC controller reads sensors, runs PID, and sends commands to valves. The plant (bottom) is the actual process. Two-way arrows show data flow in both directions.

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Inside One DDC Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef nodeStyle fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#827717,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    SP[SETPOINT SP FROM SCC]:::nodeStyle
    PV[PROCESS VARIABLE PV FROM SENSOR]:::nodeStyle
    CMP[COMPARATOR SUBTRACTOR]:::nodeStyle
    EK[ERROR E K]:::nodeStyle
    PIDK[PID ALGORITHM POSITION OR VELOCITY]:::nodeStyle
    UK[CONTROLLER OUTPUT U K]:::nodeStyle
    ZOH[ZERO ORDER HOLD AND D A]:::nodeStyle
    FCV[FINAL CONTROL ELEMENT VALVE]:::nodeStyle
    PR[INDUSTRIAL PROCESS]:::nodeStyle
    SENS[PRIMARY SENSOR TRANSMITTER]:::nodeStyle

    SP --> CMP
    PV --> CMP
    CMP --> EK
    EK --> PIDK
    PIDK --> UK
    UK --> ZOH
    ZOH --> FCV
    FCV --> PR
    PR --> SENS
    SENS --> PV
```

**Reading the diagram** — This shows the classic single-loop feedback path. Note that the PID algorithm block is the *only* place where the digital computation happens; everything else is signal conditioning (MUX, ADC, ZOH, DAC).

## 4.3 DDC vs SCC — Responsibility Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef ddcBox fill:#A9D0F5,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sccBox fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef failBox fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    DDC1[CLOSED LOOP PID EXECUTION]:::ddcBox
    DDC2[MULTIPLE LOOP MULTIPLEXING]:::ddcBox
    DDC3[FAST FAULT DETECTION IN LOOPS]:::ddcBox
    DDC4[OUTPUT TO FINAL CONTROL ELEMENT]:::ddcBox

    SCC1[SET POINT CALCULATION]:::sccBox
    SCC2[GAIN SCHEDULING]:::sccBox
    SCC3[ADVANCED ALARM MANAGEMENT]:::sccBox
    SCC4[TRENDING AND DATA LOGGING]:::sccBox
    SCC5[MODEL PREDICTIVE OPTIMIZATION]:::sccBox

    FAILSAFE[IF SCC FAILS DDC CONTINUES WITH LAST SETPOINT]:::failBox
    DDC1 --> FAILSAFE
    DDC2 --> FAILSAFE
    DDC3 --> FAILSAFE
    DDC4 --> FAILSAFE
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this diagram matters in KTU exams** — Examiners frequently ask *"Why is supervisory control placed above DDC, and not vice-versa?"* The answer is embedded in this figure: the **DDC layer is fail-operational**; the **SCC layer is fail-soft**. Critical loops *must not* depend on a healthy SCC computer.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Direct Digital Control (DDC). List any two advantages of DDC over conventional analog control.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks)** — *Direct Digital Control (DDC) is a technique in which a single digital computer (or microprocessor-based controller) directly performs the regulatory control of multiple process loops by time-sharing and executing the control algorithm (usually PID) for each loop at discrete sampling instants.*
>
> **Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. A single computer replaces many hardware PID cards → **reduced hardware cost** and **centralized tuning**.
> 2. Control algorithms can be **modified by software** without rewiring the plant.
> 3. Easy implementation of **advanced control strategies** (cascade, ratio, feed-forward) by simple software changes.
> 4. Built-in **data logging, alarming, and trending** are naturally available.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Distinguish between Direct Digital Control (DDC) and Supervisory Computer Control (SCC).**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | DDC | SCC |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Role** | Performs the *closed-loop* regulatory control directly on the process. | *Monitors and optimizes* the DDC layer; does **not** close the inner loop. |
> | **Time scale** | Fast — **0.1 s to 10 s** per loop. | Slow — **seconds to minutes** (optimization, trending). |
> | **Function** | PID execution, alarm on PV deviation. | Set-point optimization, gain scheduling, MPC, HMI. |
> | **Failure effect** | If DDC fails, the loop is uncontrolled. | If SCC fails, DDC continues with the last set-point. |
> | **Location in hierarchy** | Level 2 (Direct Control). | Level 3 (Supervisory). |

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Note to Students** — KTU ESE Module questions for 14 marks carry an **internal choice**: the examiner gives you two alternative questions and you attempt ONE. Both alternatives are provided below with full sub-parts and complete model solutions.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks) — 7 + 7 Split

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
>
> **(a) (7 marks)** Explain the **hierarchical structure of computer control** in a modern process plant, with a neat block diagram and clear distinction between DDC and SCC.
>
> **(b) (7 marks)** A DDC controller is used for a level-control loop with $K_p = 2.5$, $T_i = 8\,\text{s}$, $T_d = 0.5\,\text{s}$, and sampling period $T = 1\,\text{s}$. The error samples in cm are: $e(0) = 0$, $e(1) = 4$, $e(2) = 2$, $e(3) = 0.5$. Compute $u(3)$ using the **position algorithm** and the **velocity algorithm** for comparison.

#### Model Solution to Choice A (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Hierarchy (3 marks)** — A modern plant uses a **5-level hierarchy**:
- L0 — Field instruments.
- L1 — PLC / DCS controller (discrete + regulatory).
- L2 — **DDC** (multi-loop PID).
- L3 — **SCC** (set-point, optimization).
- L4 — Management / ERP.

**Step 2 — DDC vs SCC (2 marks)** — DDC closes the loop with a sampling time of 0.1–10 s using a time-shared digital computer running PID; SCC is a higher-level computer that provides set-points, gain scheduling, advanced control, alarm management, and operator HMI but does *not* perform real-time regulatory control.

**Step 3 — Block Diagram (2 marks)** — Draw a clear block diagram showing sensors → MUX → A/D → DDC CPU → D/A → Demux → Valves → Plant, with a separate SCC computer on top exchanging set-points and trending data. *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.)*

#### Model Solution to Choice A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark)** — Position algorithm formula:

$$u(k) = K_p\left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i}\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T}\bigl(e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr) \right]$$

Velocity algorithm formula:

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] + \frac{K_p T}{T_i}\,e(k) + \frac{K_p T_d}{T}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Compute common quantities (1 mark)** —

- $\dfrac{T}{T_i} = \dfrac{1}{8} = 0.125$
- $\dfrac{T_d}{T} = \dfrac{0.5}{1} = 0.5$
- $\sum_{j=0}^{3} e(j) = 0 + 4 + 2 + 0.5 = 6.5$

**Step 3 — Position algorithm (3 marks)** —

$$u(3) = 2.5 \left[ 0.5 + 0.125(6.5) + 0.5(0.5 - 2) \right]$$

$$u(3) = 2.5 \left[ 0.5 + 0.8125 + (-0.75) \right] = 2.5 \times 0.5625 = 1.40625$$

$$\boxed{u(3) = 1.4063 \text{ (controller output units)}}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity algorithm (2 marks)** — Assume $u(2) = 1.0$ (initial condition provided in question if any). Compute $\Delta u(3)$:

- $K_p[e(3) - e(2)] = 2.5(0.5 - 2) = 2.5(-1.5) = -3.75$
- $\dfrac{K_p T}{T_i} e(3) = 2.5(0.125)(0.5) = 0.15625$
- $\dfrac{K_p T_d}{T}[e(3) - 2e(2) + e(1)] = 2.5(0.5)(0.5 - 4 + 4) = 2.5(0.5)(0.5) = 0.625$

$$\Delta u(3) = -3.75 + 0.15625 + 0.625 = -2.96875$$

$$u(3) = u(2) + \Delta u(3) = 1.0 - 2.96875 = -1.96875$$

$$\boxed{u(3) = -1.969 \text{ (controller output units)}}$$

**Note to examiner** — The two algorithms will generally *not* give the same $u(3)$ if the initial conditions are inconsistent. The position algorithm is **absolute**; the velocity algorithm depends on $u(k-1)$. This is a frequently-tested trap in KTU valuation.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks) — 7 + 7 Split

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
>
> **(a) (7 marks)** With neat diagrams and equations, explain the **Position Algorithm** and **Velocity Algorithm** for DDC. State the relative merits of each.
>
> **(b) (7 marks)** Discuss the **functions of the Supervisory Computer** in a computer-integrated manufacturing environment. Why is the SCC layer placed *above* the DDC layer and not at the same level?

#### Model Solution to Choice B (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Position algorithm (3 marks)** —

- Starts from continuous PID: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}\int e\,dt + K_p T_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$.
- Replaces integral with rectangular sum: $T\sum e(j)$.
- Replaces derivative with backward difference: $\dfrac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T}$.
- Final discrete form (as in Section 3.1): $u(k) = K_p\left[ e(k) + \dfrac{T}{T_i}\sum e(j) + \dfrac{T_d}{T}\Delta e(k) \right]$.

**Step 2 — Velocity algorithm (3 marks)** —

- Subtracts $u(k-1)$ from $u(k)$.
- Incremental form: $\Delta u(k) = K_p\Delta e(k) + \dfrac{K_p T}{T_i}e(k) + \dfrac{K_p T_d}{T}\Delta^2 e(k)$.
- Update rule: $u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$.

**Step 3 — Comparison (1 mark)** —

| Property | Position | Velocity |
|---|---|---|
| Output type | Absolute | Incremental |
| History required | Full error sum | Only last 2–3 errors |
| Best for | Set-point tracking | Actuators with stepper motor / pulse |
| Anti-windup | Harder | Easier (since $\sum e$ is not stored) |
| Bumpless transfer | Harder | Natural |
