#### Model Solution to Choice B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — SCC Functions (4 marks)** —

1. **Set-point optimization** — computes the *best* SP for each DDC loop (e.g., maximize yield, minimize energy).
2. **Gain scheduling** — changes $K_p, T_i, T_d$ of the DDC loop with operating conditions.
3. **Advanced control** — implements feed-forward, cascade, override, model-predictive control.
4. **Alarm management** — detects, logs, prioritizes, and acknowledges process alarms.
5. **HMI / trending** — provides graphical user interface for the operator.
6. **Data archival** — stores historical data for analysis and reporting.
7. **Production reporting and ERP integration.**

**Step 2 — Why SCC is *above* DDC (3 marks)** —

- **Time-scale separation** — DDC operates at **0.1 s – 10 s**; SCC at **seconds to minutes**. Mixing them causes CPU starvation and missed deadlines.
- **Reliability / fault isolation** — If SCC fails, the DDC layer continues to control the plant at the *last good set-point*. This is essential for safety-critical plants. If the SCC were at the same level, its failure would crash the DDC layer too.
- **Architectural clarity** — The pyramid enforces **one-way data-flow** (SCC down to DDC down to field) and **structured responsibilities**, simplifying software development and maintenance.
- **Scalability** — Hundreds of DDC loops can be supervised by a small number of SCC workstations, following the master–slave topology.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
>
> 1. **Wrong sampling period sign** — Students often write $T/T_d$ instead of $T_d/T$ in the derivative term. **Mark loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Summing past errors wrongly** — In the position algorithm, the sum is from $j = 0$ to $k$, **inclusive**. Omitting the current sample costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing position and velocity outputs** — Position gives $u(k)$ directly; velocity gives $\Delta u(k)$, and you **must add it to $u(k-1)$**. Forgetting this gives incorrect answers in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 4. **Drawing the hierarchy upside down** — Always draw the pyramid with **field (Level 0) at the bottom and management (Level 4) at the top**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for a confused diagram.
> 5. **Writing "SCC performs real-time control"** — This is a conceptual error. SCC performs **supervisory and optimization** tasks; **DDC** is the one that closes the loop in real time. Examiners are strict on this distinction.
> 6. **Forgetting units** — $K_p$ is **dimensionless** (controller output / error units), $T_i$ and $T_d$ are in **seconds**, $T$ is in **seconds**. Units are checked in numerical problems.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module-3 Focus):**
>
> - **DDC** = digital computer (or PLC/DCS controller) directly computes PID for *multiple* loops in a time-shared manner; sampling period typically **0.1 s to 10 s**.
> - **SCC** = top-level host computer that *supervises* DDC — issues set-points, gain scheduling, advanced control, alarming, trending.
> - **Hierarchy** — Field (L0) → PLC (L1) → **DDC (L2)** → **SCC (L3)** → Management (L4).
> - **Position Algorithm** — outputs **absolute** $u(k)$ using full history of errors; **Velocity Algorithm** — outputs **increment** $\Delta u(k)$ using only last 2–3 errors.
> - **Position Formula:** $u(k) = K_p\left[ e(k) + \dfrac{T}{T_i}\sum e(j) + \dfrac{T_d}{T}(e(k) - e(k-1)) \right]$.
> - **Velocity Formula:** $\Delta u(k) = K_p\Delta e(k) + \dfrac{K_p T}{T_i} e(k) + \dfrac{K_p T_d}{T}\Delta^2 e(k)$, with $u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$.
> - **Sampling Theorem** — $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid **aliasing**.
> - **Sampling Period Rule** — $T \approx 0.1\,\tau_d$ to $0.5\,\tau_d$ where $\tau_d$ is the dominant process time-constant.
> - **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** — output is held constant between samples; introduces a delay of $T/2$.
> - **Discrete Gains** — $K_i = K_p T / T_i$ and $K_d = K_p T_d / T$ — use these to convert from continuous to discrete forms.
> - **DDC is fail-operational**; **SCC is fail-soft** — if SCC dies, DDC keeps running.
> - **DDC advantages** — flexibility (software tuning), advanced control strategies, data logging, lower hardware cost.
> - **DDC disadvantages** — single point of failure, finite word-length effects (quantization), sampling-induced delay, complex tuning.
> - **SCC functions** — set-point scheduling, gain scheduling, MPC, alarm management, HMI, trending, ERP integration.
> - **Architecture** — Sensors → MUX → S/H → ADC → CPU (PID) → DAC (ZOH) → Demux → Valves → Plant.
> - **Velocity algorithm is preferred in PLCs** because it does not require storing the full error history and is naturally bump-less.
> - **Position algorithm is preferred in DCS / set-point tracking** applications.
> - **Industrial examples** — DCS in petrochemical plants, BMS in HVAC, power-plant turbine governors, robot joint controllers, automotive ECUs.
> - **Numerical-tip** — Always write the formula symbolically, then plug in the **numerical values with units** at the end. This makes partial marking easier.
> - **Diagram-tip** — Always label the **sampling period T**, the **set-point**, the **PV**, the **MV**, and the **error direction** on your block diagram. Examiners love a well-labeled diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SCADA - Programming of Robot (Pick and Place)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT612 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SCADA - Programming of Robot (Pick and Place)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 SCADA — Formal Definition

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a centralized industrial control architecture that uses computers, networked data communications, and graphical Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs) to remotely monitor and control geographically distributed or shop-floor processes. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, SCADA is treated as the **supervisory layer** that sits *above* the PLC and drives field devices such as **industrial robots** performing repetitive tasks like **Pick and Place**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SCADA does NOT replace the PLC — it **supervises** it. The PLC executes the real-time ladder logic, while SCADA provides visualization, trending, alarm management, and operator commands.

## 1.2 Pick and Place Robot — Formal Definition

A **Pick and Place Robot** is a programmable electro-mechanical manipulator (typically a 4-axis or 6-axis articulated arm, or a Cartesian gantry) designed to grasp an object from one defined location and release it at another defined location. In SCADA-driven automation cells, the PLC executes the motion sequence, while SCADA supervises cycle counts, faults, and operator overrides.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sorting Clerk at the Post Office"

Imagine a **post office sorting clerk**:

| Human Sorting Clerk | SCADA-Controlled Pick & Place Robot |
|---|---|
| Eyes check parcel → conveyor sensor | Photoelectric sensor detects object |
| Brain decides sequence | PLC executes ladder logic |
| Hand reaches to pick | Robot arm moves to pick position |
| Hand grips | Pneumatic gripper closes |
| Hand carries parcel | Robot arm moves to place position |
| Hand releases | Gripper opens |
| Supervisor watches over | **SCADA HMI monitors** the whole process |

The clerk does the **physical work**, but a **supervisor** stands behind watching every parcel, counting them, alerting if something goes wrong, and showing live progress on a screen. That supervisor = **SCADA**.

## 1.4 Why Integrate SCADA with Pick and Place Robots?

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Justification (KTU Board Expectation):**
> - **Visibility** — Operator sees cycle count, fault status on HMI.
> - **Data Logging** — Production statistics stored for OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness).
> - **Remote Control** — Start/Stop/Emergency from HMI instead of physical push-buttons.
> - **Alarm Management** — Sensor failure, gripper jam, or position error triggers audible/visual alarms.
> - **Trend Analysis** — Cycle time plotted over a shift for bottleneck detection.

## 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Repeatability:** $\pm 0.02$ mm (high-precision articulated robot)
- **Typical Payload:** **3 kg to 50 kg** for industrial pick-and-place cells
- **Cycle Time:** **2 – 6 seconds** per pick-and-place operation
- **Communication Protocol:** **Modbus TCP / RS-485**, **EtherNet/IP**, **PROFINET**
- **SCADA Sampling Rate:** **100 ms – 1 s** (slower than PLC scan time of **10 ms**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SCADA Architecture for a Pick-and-Place Cell

The architecture is **hierarchical** (the classic **Purdue / ISA-95 model**):

| Level | Layer | Component in Pick-and-Place Cell |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** | Field Devices | Conveyor motor, pneumatic gripper, photo-electric sensor, limit switches |
| **Level 1** | Control | PLC (ladder logic for robot sequencing) |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory | **SCADA HMI** (operator screen, alarms, trends) |
| **Level 3** | Operations | MES / Production database |
| **Level 4** | Enterprise | ERP system |

## 2.2 Components of a SCADA-Controlled Pick-and-Place Cell

### A. Field Input Devices (Sensors)
- **Inductive Proximity Sensor** — detects metallic object on conveyor.
- **Photoelectric Sensor (Diffuse Reflective)** — detects object regardless of material.
- **Limit Switches (Home, Pick, Place positions)** — confirm robot has reached each location.
- **Gripper Position Sensors** — open-limit and close-limit switches.

### B. Field Output Devices (Actuators)
- **Conveyor Motor** (via motor contactor / VFD).
- **Pneumatic Cylinder / Gripper** (via 5/2 solenoid valve).
- **Robot Servo Drives** (X, Y, Z, and rotational axes).

### C. Controller
- **PLC** (e.g., Allen-Bradley MicroLogix, Siemens S7-1200, Mitsubishi FX-series).

### D. Supervisory Layer
- **SCADA Software** (e.g., WinCC, Wonderware InTouch, Ignition, RSView32).

## 2.3 Programming Languages Used (KTU Frequently Asked)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Programmable Controller Languages (IEC 61131-3):**
> 1. **Ladder Diagram (LD)** — most common in industry, KTU exam default.
> 2. **Structured Text (ST)** — high-level, Pascal-like.
> 3. **Function Block Diagram (FBD)**.
> 4. **Instruction List (IL)** — deprecated but historical.
> 5. **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** — *best suited for Pick and Place*.

## 2.4 Pick and Place Operational Sequence

The classic **6-step sequence** for any pick-and-place robot:

1. **Step 1 — Detection:** Object sensor ON → triggers robot cycle.
2. **Step 2 — Pick Motion:** Robot arm moves from HOME → PICK position.
3. **Step 3 — Grip:** Pneumatic gripper closes (solenoid energized).
4. **Step 4 — Place Motion:** Robot arm moves from PICK → PLACE position.
5. **Step 5 — Release:** Pneumatic gripper opens (solenoid de-energized).
6. **Step 6 — Return:** Robot arm returns HOME, waiting for next object.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Cycle Time $T_c$ | $T_c = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 + t_5 + t_6$ | Total time for one pick-place cycle (seconds) |
| Throughput $\Phi$ | $\Phi = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | Parts handled per hour (parts/hour) |
| OEE | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Availability × Performance × Quality (%) |
| Production Count $N$ | $N = \Phi \times t_{\text{shift}}$ | Parts in a shift (parts/shift) |
| Air Pressure $P_g$ for gripper | $F_{\text{grip}} = P_g \times A_{\text{piston}}$ | Grip force (N) = Pressure × Piston area |
| Modbus Register Size | $1 \text{ Register} = 16 \text{ bits}$ | Standard SCADA tag size |
| SCADA Scan Time | $T_{scan} = \text{PLC scan} + \text{Network latency}$ | Typically **100 ms – 1 s** |
| Robot Repeatability $R$ | $\vert x_{actual} - x_{commanded} \vert \leq R$ | Accuracy limit (mm) |

> [!NOTE]
> The Modbus / SCADA register width is **16 bits**, meaning each tag (e.g., "Cycle_Count") occupies one holding register (4xxxx address range).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry** — Engine block pick and place on conveyor.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — Vial pick and place on packaging line.
- **Food & Beverage** — Sorting fruits/vegetables on conveyor belts.
- **Electronics PCB assembly** — SMD component pick and place (though here SMT machines are specialized).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 I/O Address Allocation Table (Analytical Matrix)

This is the **most critical step** in any KTU PLC exam. You must always declare the I/O list **before** writing ladder logic.

| Address | Symbol | Device | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| I:0/0 | START_PB | Start Push-Button (NO) | Digital IN |
| I:0/1 | STOP_PB | Stop Push-Button (NC) | Digital IN |
| I:0/2 | OBJ_SENSE | Object Sensor (Photoelectric) | Digital IN |
| I:0/3 | LIM_HOME | Home Position Limit Switch | Digital IN |
| I:0/4 | LIM_PICK | Pick Position Limit Switch | Digital IN |
| I:0/5 | LIM_PLACE | Place Position Limit Switch | Digital IN |
| I:0/6 | GRIP_OPEN | Gripper Open Limit | Digital IN |
| I:0/7 | GRIP_CLOSE | Gripper Close Limit | Digital IN |
| O:0/0 | CONVEYOR | Conveyor Motor Contactor | Digital OUT |
| O:0/1 | ROBOT_PICK | Robot Pick Solenoid | Digital OUT |
| O:0/2 | ROBOT_PLACE | Robot Place Solenoid | Digital OUT |
| O:0/3 | GRIPPER | Gripper Solenoid (Close) | Digital OUT |
| O:0/4 | ALARM | HMI Alarm Output | Digital OUT |
| O:0/5 | CYCLE_DONE | Cycle Complete Lamp | Digital OUT |
| N7:0 | CYCLE_CTR | Cycle Counter (Integer) | Word Memory |

## 3.2 Boolean Logic Derivation — Latching the Cycle

The system needs **latching** because the start push-button is momentary.

### Step 1 — Latch Equation
Define internal relay **M_RUN** (master run coil).

$$
M\_RUN = (START\_PB \;\lor\; M\_RUN) \;\land\; \lnot STOP\_PB \;\land\; \lnot FAULT
$$

**Explanation:** 
- $START\_PB \lor M\_RUN$ — start button OR already latched.
- $\land \lnot STOP\_PB$ — AND NOT stop.
- $\land \lnot FAULT$ — AND NOT fault condition.

### Step 2 — Sequence Initialization
Define **S_START** (sequence start condition).

$$
S\_START = M\_RUN \;\land\; OBJ\_SENSE \;\land\; LIM\_HOME
$$

**Explanation:** Run is active AND an object is sensed AND robot is at home.

## 3.3 Sequential Function Chart (SFC) Logic Derivation

The SFC has **5 steps** with **4 transitions**. Each transition is a Boolean AND of:
- The previous step being active.
- The corresponding position sensor being TRUE.

### Step-by-Step Transition Equations

**Transition T1 (HOME → PICK):**
$$
T_1 = S_1 \;\land\; START\_PB \;\land\; OBJ\_SENSE \;\land\; LIM\_HOME
$$

**Transition T2 (PICK_DONE → PLACE):**
$$
T_2 = S_2 \;\land\; LIM\_PICK \;\land\; GRIP\_CLOSE
$$

**Transition T3 (PLACE_DONE → RELEASE):**
$$
T_3 = S_3 \;\land\; LIM\_PLACE
$$

**Transition T4 (RELEASE_DONE → HOME):**
$$
T_4 = S_4 \;\land\; GRIP\_OPEN
$$

## 3.4 Ladder Logic Rungs — Full Program

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **canonical KTU-style ladder logic** you must reproduce in exams. Use the rungs **in order**.

**Rung 1 — Master Latch (Start/Stop with Seal-in):**
```
|----[ START_PB ]----[/ STOP_PB ]----[/ FAULT ]----( M_RUN )----|
|                          [ M_RUN ]                              |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------|
```

**Rung 2 — Conveyor Run:**
```
|----[ M_RUN ]----[ OBJ_SENSE ]----( CONVEYOR )------------------|
```

**Rung 3 — Step 1: Home Position Achieved:**
```
|----[ M_RUN ]----[ LIM_HOME ]----( S1_HOME_OK )-----------------|
```

**Rung 4 — Initiate Pick Motion:**
```
|----[ S1_HOME_OK ]----[ OBJ_SENSE ]----[ NOT LIM_PICK ]----( ROBOT_PICK )----|
```

**Rung 5 — Gripper Close at Pick Position:**
```
|----[ LIM_PICK ]----[ TIMER(0.5s) ]----( GRIPPER )-------------|
|                                                                   |
|----[ LIM_PICK ]----[ NOT GRIP_CLOSE ]----( GRIPPER )-------------|   (redundant safety)
```

**Rung 6 — Initiate Place Motion:**
```
|----[ GRIP_CLOSE ]----[ NOT LIM_PLACE ]----( ROBOT_PLACE )------|
```

**Rung 7 — Release Object at Place Position:**
```
|----[ LIM_PLACE ]----[ TIMER(0.3s) ]----[/ GRIPPER ]-----------|
```

**Rung 8 — Return to Home:**
```
|----[ NOT GRIP_OPEN ]----[ NOT LIM_HOME ]----( ROBOT_PICK )----| (Reverse motion)
```

**Rung 9 — Cycle Counter Increment:**
```
|----[ GRIP_OPEN ]----[ LIM_HOME ]----[ OSR(1-shot) ]----( CTU CYCLE_CTR )----|
```

**Rung 10 — Cycle Complete Lamp & SCADA Tag Update:**
```
|----[ LIM_HOME ]----[ NOT OBJ_SENSE ]----( CYCLE_DONE )---------|
```

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Throughput Calculation

> **Problem:** A SCADA-supervised pick-and-place robot has the following step times: $t_1 = 0.4$ s, $t_2 = 0.8$ s, $t_3 = 0.3$ s, $t_4 = 0.8$ s, $t_5 = 0.3$ s, $t_6 = 0.6$ s. The shift is 8 hours. Calculate the **cycle time**, **throughput per hour**, and **total parts per shift**.

### Step 1 — Cycle Time
$$
T_c = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 + t_5 + t_6
$$

$$
T_c = 0.4 + 0.8 + 0.3 + 0.8 + 0.3 + 0.6 = 3.2 \text{ s}
$$

**Explanation:** Sum all six sequential step times; this is the total elapsed time for one complete pick-and-place cycle.

### Step 2 — Throughput per Hour
$$
\Phi = \dfrac{3600}{T_c} = \dfrac{3600}{3.2} = 1125 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

**Explanation:** One hour = 3600 seconds. Divide by cycle time to get parts per hour.

### Step 3 — Parts per 8-hour Shift
$$
N = \Phi \times t_{\text{shift}} = 1125 \times 8 = 9000 \text{ parts/shift}
$$

**Explanation:** Multiply hourly throughput by shift duration in hours.

## 3.6 SCADA Tag Mapping (Modbus Holding Registers)

| SCADA Tag | PLC Address | Modbus Address | Data Type | Update Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Cycle_Count` | N7:0 | 40001 | INT (16-bit) | 1 s |
| `Robot_Status` | B3:0/0 | 40002 bit | BOOL | 500 ms |
| `Fault_Code` | N7:1 | 40003 | INT | On change |
| `Cycle_Time` | F8:0 | 40004 | REAL | 1 s |
| `Auto_Manual` | B3:0/1 | 40005 bit | BOOL | On change |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Pick and Place Operational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA((Start)) --> nodeB[Power ON and Initialize]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Object on Conveyor?}
    nodeC -->|No| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD[Move Robot to PICK Position]
    nodeD --> nodeE{PICK Limit Reached?}
    nodeE -->|No| nodeD
    nodeE -->|Yes| nodeF[Activate GRIPPER Close Solenoid]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Gripper CLOSED Feedback?}
    nodeG -->|No| nodeF
    nodeG -->|Yes| nodeH[Move Robot to PLACE Position]
    nodeH --> nodeI{PLACE Limit Reached?}
    nodeI -->|No| nodeH
    nodeI -->|Yes| nodeJ[Deactivate GRIPPER Release Object]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{Gripper OPEN Feedback?}
    nodeK -->|No| nodeJ
    nodeK -->|Yes| nodeL[Move Robot back to HOME]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Increment Cycle Counter]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Update SCADA HMI Display]
    nodeN --> nodeC
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — SCADA Architecture for Pick and Place

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeERP[ERP Server Level 4] --> nodeMES[MES Production DB Level 3]
    nodeMES --> nodeSCADA[SCADA HMI Server Level 2]
    nodeSCADA --> nodePLC[PLC Controller Level 1]
    nodePLC --> nodeField0[Conveyor Motor Level 0]
    nodePLC --> nodeField1[Robot Pick Solenoid Level 0]
    nodePLC --> nodeField2[Robot Place Solenoid Level 0]
    nodePLC --> nodeField3[Gripper Solenoid Level 0]
    nodePLC --> nodeField4[Object Sensor Level 0]
    nodePLC --> nodeField5[Limit Switches Level 0]
    nodeSCADA --> nodeOP[Operator Workstation]
    nodeSCADA --> nodeALARM[Alarm Management System]
    nodeSCADA --> nodeTREND[Historical Trend Database]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Ladder Logic Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Start Push Button NO] --> nodeOR{OR Gate Seal In}
    nodeM[Memory Coil M RUN] --> nodeOR
    nodeOR --> nodeAND{AND Gate}
    nodeStop[Stop Push Button NC Inverted] --> nodeAND
    nodeFault[Fault Contact NC Inverted] --> nodeAND
    nodeAND --> nodeCoil[Master Run Coil Energized]
    nodeCoil --> nodeConveyor[Conveyor Motor Output ON]
    nodeCoil --> nodeSeq[Enable Sequence Step 1]
```

## 4.4 Sequence Step Transition Diagram (SFC Style)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stepInit
    stepInit --> stepHome : Power ON
    stepHome --> stepPick : Object Sensed AND START Pressed
    stepPick --> stepGrip : PICK Limit Reached
    stepGrip --> stepPlace : Gripper CLOSED Feedback
    stepPlace --> stepRelease : PLACE Limit Reached
    stepRelease --> stepReturn : Gripper OPEN Feedback
    stepReturn --> stepHome : HOME Limit Reached
    stepHome --> [*] : Emergency Stop
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define SCADA. List any four components of a SCADA system used to supervise a pick-and-place robot cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) is a computer-based industrial control system that monitors and controls geographically dispersed or shop-floor processes from a centralized location. It collects real-time data from field devices via PLCs and presents it on a graphical HMI for operator action.

Four components of a SCADA system for a pick-and-place cell:
1. **HMI Workstation** — Operator interface with mimic screens.
2. **SCADA Server** — Runs the supervisory software (WinCC / InTouch / Ignition).
3. **PLC** — Real-time controller running ladder logic.
4. **Communication Network** — Modbus / EtherNet / PROFINET link between PLC and SCADA.

> [!TIP]
> Always state the *purpose* in the first sentence and then *enlist*. A common 1-mark loss is missing the purpose definition.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between PLC and SCADA in a pick-and-place application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | PLC | SCADA |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Real-time control | Supervision & monitoring |
| Response Time | 10 – 50 ms (fast) | 100 ms – 1 s (slow) |
| Programming | Ladder / SFC | SCADA tag configuration |
| Role | "Muscle / hands" | "Eyes and brain of supervisor" |
| Failure Impact | Process stops | Only visualization lost |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Always draw the **I/O allocation table FIRST** before ladder logic. (2 marks reserved)
> - Every rung must be numbered and explained in one line below.
> - Do NOT forget the **seal-in (latch) contact** — losing this costs 2 marks.
> - Mention **SCADA tag mapping** to fetch full 14 marks; without it you cap at 10.
> - Convert any timing requirements into **TON timer blocks** explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Design a PLC ladder logic program for a SCADA-supervised pick-and-place robot. The robot must:**
- Wait for an object at the pick station.
- Move to pick position, close gripper.
- Move to place position, open gripper.
- Return to home and increment a cycle counter.
- A SCADA HMI must display the cycle count and allow START/STOP control.

**(7 marks for ladder logic + 7 marks for SCADA integration and explanation)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — I/O Address Allocation (2 marks):**

| Address | Symbol | Device |
|---|---|---|
| I:0/0 | START_PB | Start PB |
| I:0/1 | STOP_PB | Stop PB |
| I:0/2 | OBJ_SENSE | Object Sensor |
| I:0/3 | LIM_HOME | Home Limit |
| I:0/4 | LIM_PICK | Pick Limit |
| I:0/5 | LIM_PLACE | Place Limit |
| I:0/6 | GRIP_OPEN | Gripper Open LS |
| I:0/7 | GRIP_CLOSE | Gripper Close LS |
| O:0/0 | ROBOT_PICK | Robot Pick Solenoid |
| O:0/1 | ROBOT_PLACE | Robot Place Solenoid |
| O:0/2 | GRIPPER | Gripper Solenoid |
| O:0/3 | ALARM | Alarm Output |
| N7:0 | CYCLE_CTR | Cycle Counter |
| B3:0/0 | M_RUN | Master Run Bit |

**Step 2 — Ladder Logic (5 marks):**

**Rung 1 — Master Latch:**
```
|----[ START_PB ]----[/ STOP_PB ]----[/ FAULT ]----( M_RUN )----|
|                              [ M_RUN ]                         |
```
*When START is pressed, M_RUN latches. STOP or FAULT breaks the seal.*

**Rung 2 — Pick Motion:**
```
|----[ M_RUN ]----[ OBJ_SENSE ]----[ LIM_HOME ]----[/ LIM_PICK ]----( ROBOT_PICK )----|
```
*Energizes pick solenoid until pick position limit is reached.*

**Rung 3 — Gripper Close (with 1-s timer for safety):**
```
|----[ LIM_PICK ]----[ TON T1, 1.0s ]----( GRIPPER )-------------|
```
*Holds object 1 s for firm grip.*

**Rung 4 — Place Motion:**
```
|----[ GRIP_CLOSE ]----[/ LIM_PLACE ]----( ROBOT_PLACE )-------|
```

**Rung 5 — Release Object:**
```
|----[ LIM_PLACE ]----[ TON T2, 0.5s ]----[/ GRIPPER ]----------|
```
*De-energizes gripper solenoid (uses NC contact logic) after 0.5 s.*

**Rung 6 — Return Home:**
```
|----[/ GRIP_OPEN ]----[/ LIM_HOME ]----( ROBOT_PICK )----------|
```
*Reverse-direction motion until home limit is hit.*

**Rung 7 — Cycle Counter:**
```
|----[ GRIP_OPEN ]----[ LIM_HOME ]----[ OSR ]----( CTU C7:0 )----|
```

**(b) Explain how the SCADA HMI interfaces with the PLC for this robot. Draw the SCADA tag mapping table and describe one alarm configuration. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**SCADA Tag Mapping (3 marks):**

| HMI Display | PLC Address | Tag Type | Update |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cycle Count Numeric Display | N7:0 | INT | 1 s |
| Robot Status Indicator | B3:0/0 | BOOL | 500 ms |
| START Button | B3:1/0 | BOOL | On click |
| STOP Button | B3:1/1 | BOOL | On click |
| Alarm Banner | B3:2/0 | BOOL | On event |

**Communication Protocol (2 marks):**
SCADA polls the PLC via **Modbus TCP/IP** over Ethernet. The SCADA server acts as the Modbus master, the PLC as the slave. Poll interval = **500 ms**. Cycle count is read from holding register **40001**, START/STOP bits are written to coils.

**Alarm Configuration (2 marks):**
An alarm **"Gripper Jam"** is configured in SCADA:
- **Trigger:** GRIP_CLOSE sensor not TRUE within **2 seconds** of GRIPPER coil energization.
- **Priority:** HIGH (red banner + audible buzzer).
- **Acknowledgment:** Operator must press ACK on HMI to silence.
- **Action:** SCADA writes **M_RUN = 0** to stop the cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss:** Students often forget to show the **seal-in contact in Rung 1**. Without it, the robot only moves while START is held — losing 2 full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat flowchart, explain the sequence of operations for a pick-and-place robot under SCADA supervision. Calculate the throughput if the cycle time is 4.5 s and the shift is 8 hours. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Sequence Flowchart (4 marks):**
The complete 6-step flowchart was shown in Section 4.1. Key steps:
1. Object detection → 2. Move to pick → 3. Gripper close → 4. Move to place → 5. Gripper open → 6. Return home.

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\Phi = \dfrac{3600}{T_c} = \dfrac{3600}{4.5} = 800 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

$$
N = \Phi \times t_{\text{shift}} = 800 \times 8 = 6400 \text{ parts per shift}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** If the firm requires 10,000 parts/shift, either the cycle time must reduce to ≤ 2.88 s, or a second robot cell must be added.

**(b) Explain the role of sensors (object sensor, limit switches, gripper feedback) and actuators (solenoid valves, motor) in a SCADA-supervised pick-and-place cell. Discuss any two safety interlocks. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Sensors (3 marks):**
1. **Object Sensor (Photoelectric):** Detects incoming part; rising edge triggers robot cycle.
2. **Limit Switches (HOME / PICK / PLACE):** Provide position feedback — critical for SCADA display of "Robot at Home" indicator.
3. **Gripper Feedback (Open/Close LS):** Confirms mechanical state of gripper; used for both completion logic and fault detection.

**Actuators (2 marks):**
1. **Solenoid Valves (5/2 way):** Direct pneumatic flow to gripper cylinder for open/close.
2. **Conveyor Motor (via VFD):** Stops conveyor when robot enters pick zone to prevent part drift.

**Safety Interlocks (2 marks):**
1. **Two-Hand Anti-Start:** Both operator hands must be off the conveyor during Auto mode (prevents hand crushing).
2. **Light Curtain at Pick Zone:** If light curtain breaks during robot motion, E-STOP is triggered and M_RUN is forced OFF by SCADA.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SCADA Programming of Robot (Pick and Place)**

- **SCADA** supervises; **PLC** controls. Never reverse this in your exam answer.
- Always draw the **I/O allocation table** before writing ladder logic — it carries 2 marks minimum.
- A canonical pick-and-place sequence has **6 steps**: Detect → Pick Motion → Grip → Place Motion → Release → Return.
- **Latching (seal-in)** is mandatory — use a parallel contact around the START rung.
- Each motion must terminate on a **limit switch** signal — never use a fixed timer alone.
- **Gripper action** uses a TON timer (0.3 – 1.0 s) for firm grip / release.
- **Cycle counter** must increment on the rising edge of (GRIP_OPEN AND LIM_HOME) — use **OSR (one-shot rising)**.
- **SCADA tag mapping** uses **Modbus holding registers (4xxxx)** for 16-bit integers and **coils (0xxxx)** for booleans.
- Throughput formula: $\Phi = 3600 / T_c$.
- OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality (commonly tested 3-mark definition).
- **Two safety interlocks** to mention in any exam: light curtain + two-hand anti-start, OR E-stop + guard door interlock.
- **Communication:** SCADA ↔ PLC is typically **Modbus TCP/IP**, poll interval **500 ms**.
- Robot **repeatability** $\pm 0.02$ mm; payload **3–50 kg**; cycle time **2–6 s** in industrial cells.
- The **ladder logic rungs MUST be numbered Rung 1, Rung 2 …** in your answer sheet.
- A **flowchart** of the sequence carries 3–4 marks if drawn neatly with arrows.
- **Fault handling:** always include a FAULT contact in the master latch rung.
- For KTU 2024 scheme: align your answer to the **Course Outcome** (e.g., CO3: "Develop PLC and SCADA programs for industrial automation").

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Acquisition Systems - Sampling Theorem - Sampling and Digitizing - Aliasing - Sample and Hold Circuit - Signal Conditioning of Strain Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT612 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Acquisition Systems - Sampling Theorem - Sampling and Digitizing - Aliasing - Sample and Hold Circuit - Signal Conditioning of Strain Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Data Acquisition System (DAS) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A *Data Acquisition System (DAS)* is an integrated electronic instrumentation assembly that converts real-world physical phenomena (analog signals from sensors/transducers) into a stream of digital data suitable for storage, display, processing, and analysis by a digital computer or controller.

A complete DAS captures, conditions, converts, and delivers measurement data with prescribed accuracy, resolution, and speed. The acronym is sometimes expanded as **DAQS** (Data Acquisition System) or **DAQ** (Data Acquisition).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Function of DAS:** To bridge the **analog physical world** (continuous-time, continuous-amplitude) with the **digital processing world** (discrete-time, discrete-amplitude) **without losing critical information**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Cameraman"
Imagine a sports cameraman recording a 100 m sprint. He cannot shoot video every millisecond — he takes snapshots at fixed intervals (e.g., 30 frames per second). To later reconstruct the runner's motion accurately, he must follow the **Sampling Theorem**:
- If the runner's legs move at most 5 times per second (5 Hz), he should take **at least 10 pictures per second** to clearly capture the motion.
- If he shoots too slowly (say, 5 photos per second), the runner appears to move *backward* — this is **aliasing**.
- Each photo is captured, frozen briefly (Sample & Hold), and digitized into pixels (ADC conversion).
- The cameraman's lens (Signal Conditioning) cleans up the image — adjusting brightness, contrast, and removing noise — so the final stored file is sharp.

This is exactly what a DAS does — but for electrical signals instead of visual scenes.

---

## 1.2 Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon Theorem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Statement:** A bandlimited continuous-time signal x(t), whose highest frequency component is **f_m** Hz, can be completely reconstructed from its uniformly spaced samples x(nT_s) **if and only if** the sampling frequency **f_s ≥ 2 f_m**. The minimum sampling rate f_s = 2 f_m is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

- **f_s** → Sampling frequency (Hz)
- **f_m** → Maximum (highest) frequency component of the signal (Hz)
- **T_s = 1 / f_s** → Sampling period (seconds)

### Intuition
If you sample a sine wave at least **twice per cycle**, you have enough points to mathematically reconstruct its shape. One sample per cycle is ambiguous (could be any frequency), and zero or one sample per cycle destroys information.

---

## 1.3 Sampling and Digitizing

**Sampling** is the process of reading the instantaneous amplitude of an analog signal at **discrete, equally spaced time intervals**. It converts a **continuous-time** signal into a **discrete-time** signal.

**Digitizing** has two sub-steps:
1. **Quantization** — mapping each sample amplitude to the nearest level from a finite set of $2^n$ discrete levels (n = ADC resolution in bits).
2. **Encoding (Coding)** — assigning a unique binary code (n-bit word) to each quantization level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling = discretization in TIME**   |   **Quantization = discretization in AMPLITUDE**

---

## 1.4 Aliasing

> [!WARNING]
> **Aliasing is a FUNDAMENTAL DANGER** in every DAS. It is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a signal, when undersampled, **masquerade as lower frequencies** in the reconstructed output.

**Mathematical statement:** If a signal has frequency f and is sampled at f_s, the *apparent* (aliased) frequency seen in the reconstructed signal is:

$$f_{alias} = \vert f - N \cdot f_s \vert$$

where N is the integer that makes $f_{alias} \in [0, f_s/2]$.

### Famous Real-World Example
The **wagon-wheel effect** in movies — rotating wheels appear to spin backward or stand still because the frame rate (sampling frequency) is too low compared to wheel rotation speed.

**Solution:** Insert a **low-pass anti-aliasing filter** (analog filter) *before* the sampler, with cutoff frequency **f_c ≤ f_s / 2**.

---

## 1.5 Sample and Hold (S/H) Circuit

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A Sample and Hold (S/H or SHA — Sample-and-Hold Amplifier) circuit samples an analog input voltage and *holds* it at a constant level during the time required by the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** to complete its conversion.

**Why is S/H necessary?** ADCs take a finite **conversion time** (microseconds to milliseconds). During this time, if the input signal changes, the ADC digitizes an *averaged* or *wrong* value, producing large errors — especially for fast signals. The S/H "freezes" the signal, giving the ADC a stable DC voltage to convert.

**Key Specifications:**
- **Acquisition Time (t_acq)** — time to acquire the new input after the switch closes.
- **Aperture Time (t_ap)** — small delay between the hold command and actual opening of the switch.
- **Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter)** — variation in aperture time from sample to sample.
- **Droop Rate** — rate at which the held voltage decays during hold mode (due to capacitor leakage).

---

## 1.6 Signal Conditioning of a Strain Gauge

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Signal conditioning is the stage in a DAS that **amplifies, filters, linearizes, isolates, and converts** the raw transducer output into a form suitable for the ADC. For a strain gauge, the conditioning is typically done using a **Wheatstone bridge** followed by an **instrumentation amplifier**.

A **strain gauge** is a passive resistive transducer whose resistance changes when subjected to mechanical strain. The fractional change $\Delta R / R$ is related to strain $\epsilon$ by the **Gauge Factor (G_F)**:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \epsilon$$

Typical gauge factor $G_F \approx 2.0$ for metallic foil gauges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A bare strain gauge produces a *very small* resistance change (often $\Delta R < 1 \, \Omega$ for full-scale strain).** Without signal conditioning, the resulting voltage signal is too small (microvolts to millivolts) and too noise-sensitive for direct ADC conversion. The bridge + instrumentation amplifier combination converts this small change into a robust, scaled, linear voltage signal.

---

## 1.7 Summary of the DAS Signal Chain

| Stage | Function | Typical Component |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| Sensor / Transducer | Converts physical quantity to electrical signal | Strain gauge, thermocouple, RTD |
| Signal Conditioning | Amplifies, filters, linearizes | Wheatstone bridge + INA |
| Anti-Aliasing Filter | Removes frequencies above f_s/2 | Active LPF (e.g., Butterworth) |
| Multiplexer (MUX) | Routes multiple channels to one ADC | CD4051, 74HC4051 |
| Sample & Hold | Freezes signal during ADC conversion | LF398, AD585 |
| ADC | Converts analog voltage to digital code | Successive Approximation ADC |
| Computer / Controller | Stores, displays, processes data | PLC, PC, Microcontroller |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Data Acquisition System

A standard *general-purpose* DAS consists of the following functional blocks (in strict signal-flow order):

1. **Sensor / Transducer** — converts physical parameter (strain, temperature, pressure) to electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit** — amplification, filtering, linearization, isolation, cold-junction compensation (for thermocouples), bridge completion (for strain gauges).
3. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter** — analog LPF that bandlimits the signal to $\le f_s / 2$ to prevent aliasing.
4. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — time-shares one ADC among multiple input channels.
5. **Sample and Hold (S/H) Amplifier** — holds instantaneous signal value during ADC conversion.
6. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — produces n-bit digital word corresponding to input voltage.
7. **Digital Interface / Controller** — transfers digital data to a computer, PLC, or microcontroller via parallel bus, USB, RS-485, Ethernet, etc.

### Two Principal DAS Architectures

**A. Simultaneous (Parallel) Sample & Hold DAS**
- Each channel has its own dedicated S/H and (often) its own ADC.
- All channels sampled at the *same instant* — critical for phase-sensitive measurements (e.g., vibration analysis, three-phase power).
- *Disadvantage:* expensive (one ADC per channel).

**B. Multiplexed (Time-Shared) DAS**
- Single S/H and single ADC shared via analog MUX across N channels.
- Channels are sampled sequentially with small time skew.
- *Advantage:* low cost, compact.
- *Disadvantage:* inter-channel time skew, requires fast ADC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks for a labeled block diagram of a generalized DAS.** Memorize the order: **Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Anti-Aliasing Filter → MUX → S/H → ADC → Computer.**

---

## 2.2 The Sampling Theorem — Mathematical Foundation

### Theorem Statement (Rigorous)
Let x(t) be a *bandlimited* continuous-time signal with **Fourier transform X(f)** such that:

$$X(f) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert > f_m$$

Then x(t) is **uniquely determined** by its samples x(nT_s), n ∈ ℤ, provided:

$$f_s = \frac{1}{T_s} \geq 2 f_m$$

The original signal can be exactly reconstructed using the **Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula**:

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$

where $\text{sinc}(x) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$.

### Spectrum-Domain Explanation
- Sampling in the time domain is equivalent to **periodic replication** of the spectrum X(f) at intervals of f_s.
- If $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, the replicated spectra **do not overlap** — a low-pass filter can recover the original.
- If $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectra **overlap (fold over)** — this overlap region is the **aliasing distortion**.

---

## 2.3 Sampling Methods — Three Types

| Type | Pulse Shape | Description | Use Case |
|------|------------|-------------|----------|
| **Ideal / Impulse Sampling** | Mathematical impulse | Theoretical; instantaneous value capture | Mathematical analysis only |
| **Natural Sampling (Chopper)** | Rectangular pulse whose top follows signal | Practical pulse modulation | PAM systems |
| **Sample-and-Hold (Flat-Top)** | Rectangular pulse with flat top held at sample value | Used in all modern DAS | ADC interfacing |

> [!NOTE]
> **In all real DAS hardware, sampling is *flat-top* (S/H).** The amplitude is captured at sampling instant nT_s and held constant for the entire sample period T_s.

---

## 2.4 Quantization and Digitizing

### Quantization Error
For an n-bit ADC with full-scale range $V_{FS}$:

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} \quad \text{(Quantization Step Size or LSB)}$$

$$-\frac{Q}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{Q}{2} \quad \text{(Quantization Error bound)}$$

### Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)
For a full-scale sinusoidal input:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \, n + 1.76 \quad \text{dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every additional bit of ADC resolution improves the SQNR by ~6 dB.** This is the single most useful formula for ADC selection in KTU problems.

---

## 2.5 Aliasing — Detailed Treatment

### Mathematical Origin
Sampling a sinusoid $\cos(2\pi f t)$ at frequency $f_s$ yields sampled values that are *indistinguishable* from $\cos(2\pi f_a t)$ where:

$$f_a = \vert f - k \cdot f_s \vert, \quad k = \text{round}\left(\frac{f}{f_s}\right)$$

### Frequency Folding Diagram
- Frequencies in $[0, f_s/2]$ → appear correctly.
- Frequencies in $[f_s/2, f_s]$ → fold down into $[0, f_s/2]$.
- Frequencies in $[f_s, 3f_s/2]$ → fold down again, and so on.

### Worked KTU-Style Example
A signal contains components at **80 Hz, 150 Hz, and 320 Hz**, sampled at **f_s = 200 Hz**.

- 80 Hz: in band [0, 100] → appears as **80 Hz** ✓
- 150 Hz: in band [100, 200] → folds to $|150 - 200| = \mathbf{50 \text{ Hz}}$
- 320 Hz: in band [200, 300] → folds to $|320 - 400| = \mathbf{80 \text{ Hz}}$ (collides with true 80 Hz!)

### Anti-Aliasing Filter
- A **low-pass filter** placed *before* the sampler with cutoff **f_c ≤ f_s/2**.
- Typical filters: **Butterworth** (maximally flat passband), **Bessel** (linear phase), **Chebyshev** (steeper roll-off with ripple).
- **Order selection:** higher order → sharper cutoff → less filter-induced signal distortion in passband.

---

## 2.6 Sample and Hold Circuit — Operation and Types

### Functional Operation (Two Modes)
1. **Sample (Track) Mode** — analog switch closed; output tracks input continuously.
2. **Hold Mode** — analog switch open; capacitor retains last sampled voltage.

### Equivalent Circuit
- **Analog switch** (JFET, MOSFET, or CMOS transmission gate) controlled by a digital logic signal.
- **Hold capacitor** C_H (typically 10 pF – 1 nF) — stores the voltage.
- **Buffer amplifier** (high input impedance op-amp like JFET-input) — prevents C_H from discharging into load.

### Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop S/H
- **Open-loop:** switch → capacitor → unity-gain buffer. Simple, fast, but switch resistance introduces gain error.
- **Closed-loop (Feedback):** switch → capacitor → op-amp in feedback loop. Slower but accurate, with very low droop.

### Key Specifications (KTU Exam Favorites)
- **Acquisition Time (t_acq):** time for output to settle within specified accuracy (e.g., 0.1%) of final value after switch closure. Limited by RC time constant.
- **Aperture Time (t_ap):** small fixed delay between hold command and actual switch opening.
- **Aperture Uncertainty (Δt_ap):** jitter in aperture time — limits maximum signal frequency.
- **Hold Mode Droop:** rate of decay of held voltage due to capacitor leakage and buffer input bias current.
  $$\frac{dV}{dt}\bigg|_{droop} = \frac{I_{leakage}}{C_H}$$
- **Hold Step (Pedestal Error):** small voltage jump when entering hold mode due to switch capacitance (feedthrough).

### Maximum Allowable Signal Frequency
For a signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$, the maximum allowed aperture jitter to keep amplitude error $\le \pm \epsilon$:

$$\Delta t_{ap} \leq \frac{\epsilon}{\pi f_{max}}$$

For 1% error: $\Delta t_{ap} \leq \dfrac{0.01}{\pi f_{max}}$

---

## 2.7 Strain Gauge Signal Conditioning — The Wheatstone Bridge

### Strain Gauge Basics
- A **foil or wire resistor** bonded to the structure.
- Resistance changes with strain: $R = R_0 (1 + G_F \epsilon)$.
- Typical $G_F = 2.0$ to 2.2 for constantan/isoelastic alloys.

### Quarter-Bridge Configuration
- One active strain gauge, three fixed precision resistors.
- Excitation voltage $V_{EX}$ applied across the bridge.

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{EX}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \epsilon \quad \text{(for small strain)}$$

### Half-Bridge Configuration
- Two active gauges: one in tension, one in compression (adjacent arms).
- Doubles sensitivity; provides **temperature compensation**.

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{EX}}{2} \cdot G_F \cdot \epsilon$$

### Full-Bridge Configuration
- Four active gauges — two in tension, two in compression.
- Maximum sensitivity: **4×** the quarter-bridge value.

$$V_{out} = V_{EX} \cdot G_F \cdot \epsilon$$

### Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)
- Three op-amp topology: two input buffer stages + difference amplifier.
- **High CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio)** — essential because the bridge output sits on top of large common-mode voltage $V_{EX}/2$.
- Gain set by single external resistor $R_G$:

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In all KTU exam problems on strain gauges, the signal chain is:** *Bridge → Instrumentation Amplifier → Anti-Aliasing LPF → S/H → ADC.* Each stage is required; the bridge alone is **not sufficient** because the output is too small.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Nyquist (Minimum) Sampling Rate | $f_s = 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 2 | Sampling Period | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | seconds |
| 3 | Quantization Step (LSB) | $Q = V_{FS} / 2^n$ | Volts |
| 4 | SQNR for full-scale sine | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB |
| 5 | Aliased Frequency | $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ | Hz, k = integer |
| 6 | Maximum input frequency (anti-alias) | $f_c \le f_s / 2$ | Hz |
| 7 | Strain gauge resistance change | $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \epsilon$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Quarter-bridge output | $V_o = (V_{EX}/4) G_F \epsilon$ | Volts |
| 9 | Full-bridge output | $V_o = V_{EX} \cdot G_F \epsilon$ | Volts |
| 10 | INA gain (3-op-amp) | $A_v = 1 + 2R/R_G$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | S/H droop rate | $dV/dt = I_{leak}/C_H$ | V/s |
| 12 | Aperture jitter limit (error ≤ ε) | $\Delta t_{ap} \le \epsilon/(\pi f_{max})$ | seconds |
| 13 | Reconstruction (Whittaker–Shannon) | $x(t) = \Sigma x(nT_s) \text{sinc}[(t - nT_s)/T_s]$ | continuous time |
| 14 | % Resolution of n-bit ADC | $\%R = (1/2^n) \times 100$ | % |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | DAS Use Case | Critical DAS Specification |
|--------|--------------|----------------------------|
| **Vibration Analysis** (rotating machinery) | Bearing fault diagnosis | High sampling rate, simultaneous S/H |
| **Strain Measurement** (structural health) | Bridge load testing | Full-bridge + INA + high CMRR |
| **Medical ECG/EEG** | Cardiac monitoring | Anti-alias filter, 12-bit ADC typical |
| **Industrial Process Control** | PLC analog inputs | 4–20 mA current loop, 16-bit ADC |
| **Audio Recording** | Digital audio | 44.1 kHz / 16-bit (CD standard) |
| **Seismic Monitoring** | Earthquake detection | Very high dynamic range, low noise floor |
| **Automotive Sensors** | Engine control unit (ECU) | Multiplexed high-speed ADC |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Spectrum Replication Due to Impulse Sampling

**Goal:** Prove that sampling a bandlimited signal in time produces a periodic replication of its spectrum in frequency.

### Step 1 — Define the Sampling Operation Mathematically

An ideal impulse sampler multiplies the input signal x(t) by a periodic train of Dirac impulses $\delta_{T_s}(t)$:

$$\delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

The sampled signal $x_s(t)$ is therefore:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

### Step 2 — Use the Sifting Property of the Impulse

Because the Dirac delta samples x(t) at each impulse location:

$$\boxed{x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \, \delta(t - nT_s)}$$

This expresses the sampled signal as a weighted sum of impulses, where each impulse at $t = nT_s$ has strength equal to the sampled value $x(nT_s)$.

### Step 3 — Express the Impulse Train as a Fourier Series

The impulse train $\delta_{T_s}(t)$ has Fourier series expansion:

$$\delta_{T_s}(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}$$

where $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the sampling frequency.

### Step 4 — Substitute into Sampled Signal

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}$$

$$x_s(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}$$

### Step 5 — Take the Fourier Transform of Both Sides

Using the **frequency-shift property** of the Fourier Transform:

$$\mathcal{F}\{x(t) e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}\} = X(f - k f_s)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)}$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the Result

This is the **fundamental equation of the sampling theorem**: the spectrum of the sampled signal consists of the original spectrum $X(f)$ replicated at every integer multiple of the sampling frequency $f_s$, scaled by $1/T_s$.

- If **$f_s \geq 2 f_m$**, the replicas do not overlap → a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c \in [f_m, f_s - f_m]$ recovers $X(f)$ exactly.
- If **$f_s < 2 f_m$**, the replicas overlap → **aliasing** → original signal cannot be recovered.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Quantization Error Bound and SQNR

### Step 1 — Define Quantization Error

An n-bit quantizer maps each sample to the nearest of $2^n$ discrete levels, with step size Q. The error is:

$$e_q = x_{quantized} - x_{actual}$$

For uniform mid-tread quantization, this error is uniformly distributed in $[-Q/2, +Q/2]$.

### Step 2 — Compute the Mean-Square Quantization Noise

For a uniform distribution over an interval of width Q:

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-Q/2}^{Q/2} \frac{e_q^2}{Q} \, de_q = \frac{1}{Q} \left[\frac{e_q^3}{3}\right]_{-Q/2}^{Q/2}$$

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{1}{Q} \cdot \frac{2 (Q/2)^3}{3} = \frac{Q^2}{12}$$

### Step 3 — Signal Power of a Full-Scale Sinusoid

For a sine wave of peak amplitude $V_p = V_{FS}/2$:

$$P_{signal} = \frac{V_p^2}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$

### Step 4 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{\overline{e_q^2}} = \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{Q^2 / 12} = \frac{12}{8} \cdot \frac{V_{FS}^2}{Q^2}$$

Since $Q = V_{FS}/2^n$:

$$\frac{V_{FS}^2}{Q^2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{(V_{FS}/2^n)^2} = 2^{2n}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Decibels

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(2^{2n})$$

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 20 n \log_{10}(2)$$

Since $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.30103$:

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \, n}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Full-Bridge Strain Gauge Output Voltage

### Step 1 — Wheatstone Bridge Equation

For a Wheatstone bridge with four arms R1, R2, R3, R4 and excitation $V_{EX}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{EX} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Strain-Dependent Resistances

In a full bridge, all four arms are active strain gauges:
- $R_1 = R_0(1 + G_F \epsilon)$ — arm 1 (tension)
- $R_2 = R_0(1 - G_F \epsilon)$ — arm 2 (compression)
- $R_3 = R_0(1 + G_F \epsilon)$ — arm 3 (tension)
- $R_4 = R_0(1 - G_F \epsilon)$ — arm 4 (compression)

### Step 3 — Plug Into Bridge Equation

$$V_{out} = V_{EX} \left[ \frac{R_0(1 + G_F \epsilon)}{R_0(1 + G_F \epsilon) + R_0(1 - G_F \epsilon)} - \frac{R_0(1 - G_F \epsilon)}{R_0(1 + G_F \epsilon) + R_0(1 - G_F \epsilon)} \right]$$

The denominators are equal: $2 R_0$. So:

$$V_{out} = V_{EX} \left[ \frac{1 + G_F \epsilon}{2} - \frac{1 - G_F \epsilon}{2} \right]$$

$$V_{out} = V_{EX} \left[ \frac{(1 + G_F \epsilon) - (1 - G_F \epsilon)}{2} \right]$$

$$V_{out} = V_{EX} \left[ \frac{2 G_F \epsilon}{2} \right]$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{EX} \cdot G_F \cdot \epsilon}$$

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Problem Walkthrough — Typical 7-Mark Problem

**Problem:** A signal $x(t) = 5 \sin(2\pi \cdot 600 \, t) + 3 \cos(2\pi \cdot 1400 \, t)$ volts is sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz.

**(i)** Identify the Nyquist rate and determine whether aliasing occurs.

**Solution:**
- Highest frequency $f_m = 1400$ Hz.
- Nyquist rate $f_N = 2 \times 1400 = \mathbf{2800 \text{ Hz}}$.
- Since $f_s = 1000 < 2800$, **aliasing occurs**.

**(ii)** Compute the aliased frequencies of both components.

**For the 600 Hz component:**
- $f_a = \vert 600 - 1000 \vert = \mathbf{400 \text{ Hz}}$

**For the 1400 Hz component:**
- $f_a = \vert 1400 - 1000 \vert = \mathbf{400 \text{ Hz}}$ — both components **collide** at 400 Hz! Catastrophic aliasing.

**(iii)** What minimum sampling rate would prevent aliasing?

**Solution:** Minimum $f_s = 2 f_m = \mathbf{2800 \text{ Hz}}$. To leave a *guard band*, choose $f_s \geq 2.5 f_m = \mathbf{3500 \text{ Hz}}$ (good engineering practice).

**(iv)** If the 1400 Hz signal is sampled at $f_s = 2800$ Hz and digitized by a 10-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 10$ V, find the quantization step size and SQNR.

**Solution:**
- Step size: $Q = V_{FS}/2^n = 10 / 1024 = \mathbf{9.77 \text{ mV}}$
- SQNR: $\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76 = \mathbf{61.96 \text{ dB}}$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bridge Configurations

| Parameter | Quarter Bridge | Half Bridge | Full Bridge |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| Active gauges | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| Sensitivity factor | 1× | 2× | 4× |
| Temperature compensation | Poor | Good (adjacent arms) | Excellent |
| Sensitivity to bending | Yes | Reduced | Cancelled |
| Common-mode error rejection | Limited | Better | Best |
| Output equation | $V_o = (V_{EX}/4) G_F \epsilon$ | $V_o = (V_{EX}/2) G_F \epsilon$ | $V_o = V_{EX} G_F \epsilon$ |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |

---

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix — Sampling Methods

| Property | Ideal (Impulse) | Natural (Chopper) | Flat-Top (S/H) |
|----------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Mathematical tractability | High (textbook) | Medium | Low |
| Implementation | Impossible | Switch + filter | Real, used in all DAS |
| Pulse shape | δ(t) | Follows signal | Constant over T_s |
| Spectral distortion | None (if f_s ≥ 2f_m) | Slight | **Aperture effect** (sinx/x droop) |
| Amplitude accuracy | Exact at sample instant | Slight averaging | Exact (held value) |
| Used in ADC interfacing? | No | No | **Yes** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generalized Data Acquisition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSOR["Sensor or Transducer"]
    SC["Signal Conditioner"]
    AAF["Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter"]
    MUX["Analog Multiplexer MUX"]
    SH["Sample and Hold S H Circuit"]
    ADC["Analog to Digital Converter ADC"]
    CTRL["Digital Controller or Computer"]
    DISP["Display Storage Analysis"]

    SENSOR --> SC
    SC --> AAF
    AAF --> MUX
    MUX --> SH
    SH --> ADC
    ADC --> CTRL
    CTRL --> DISP
```

**Reading order:** Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Anti-Aliasing Filter → Multiplexer → Sample & Hold → ADC → Computer → Display.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Detailed Sampling Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ANALOG["Analog Input Signal x of t"]
    AAS["Anti Aliasing LPF cutoff f c equals f s divided by 2"]
    SAH["Sample and Hold controlled by clock f s"]
    QNT["Quantizer n bits"]
    ENC["Binary Encoder"]
    DIG["Digital Output Word"]

    ANALOG --> AAS
    AAS --> SAH
    SAH --> QNT
    QNT --> ENC
    ENC --> DIG
```

---

## 4.3 Spectrum Replication Illustration (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIG["Original Spectrum X of f"]
        OF1["Triangular lobe centered at 0 Hz width 2 f m"]
    end
    subgraph SAMP["Sampled Spectrum X s of f replicated every f s"]
        SP1["Replicate 1 around 0"]
        SP2["Replicate 2 around plus f s"]
        SP3["Replicate 3 around minus f s"]
        SP4["Replicate 4 around plus 2 f s"]
    end
    ORIG --> SAMP
```

**Note:** If $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, the replicas do **not** overlap. If $f_s < 2 f_m$, they overlap → aliasing distortion.

---

## 4.4 Sample and Hold Circuit — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN["Vin analog input"]
    SW["Analog Switch MOSFET controlled by logic"]
    CHOLD["Hold Capacitor C H"]
    BUF["Buffer Amplifier unity gain"]
    VOUT["Vout held voltage"]

    VIN --> SW
    SW --> CHOLD
    CHOLD --> BUF
    BUF --> VOUT

    CTRL["Control Logic Sample or Hold"] -.->|closes during sample| SW
    CTRL -.->|opens during hold| SW
```

---

## 4.5 Strain Gauge Signal Conditioning — Full System Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    STRAIN["Mechanical Strain epsilon applied to specimen"]
    SG["Strain Gauge bonded to specimen resistance R changes"]
    EXV["Excitation Voltage V EX"]
    BRIDGE["Wheatstone Bridge Quarter Half or Full"]
    INA["Instrumentation Amplifier gain A v equals 1 plus 2R divided by R G"]
    FILT["Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter"]
    SHOLD["Sample and Hold"]
    A2D["ADC n bits"]
    PROC["Microcontroller or PLC for display and processing"]

    STRAIN --> SG
    SG --> BRIDGE
    EXV --> BRIDGE
    BRIDGE --> INA
    INA --> FILT
    FILT --> SHOLD
    SHOLD --> A2D
    A2D --> PROC
```

---

## 4.6 Wheatstone Bridge — Full-Bridge Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VEX["V EX excitation plus"]
    R1["R 1 active gauge plus strain"]
    R2["R 2 active gauge minus strain"]
    R3["R 3 active gauge plus strain"]
    R4["R 4 active gauge minus strain"]
    GND["Ground"]
    VOP["V out positive"]
    VON["V out negative"]

    VEX --- R1
    R1 --- VOP
    VOP --- R3
    R3 --- GND
    VEX --- R4
    R4 --- VON
    VON --- R2
    R2 --- GND
```

**Operation:** Adjacent arms experience opposite strain (tension/compression). The differential output $V_{out} = V_{OP} - V_{ON}$ is amplified by the INA.

---

## 4.7 Aliasing — Frequency Folding Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["True Signal Frequencies"]
        F1["0 to f s divided by 2 unchanged"]
        F2["f s divided by 2 to f s folds to 0 to f s divided by 2"]
        F3["f s to 3 f s divided by 2 folds to f s divided by 2 to 0 reversed"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Apparent Frequencies After Sampling"]
        A1["Lower half of spectrum only"]
    end
    INPUT --> OUTPUT
```

**Reading:** Any input frequency above $f_s/2$ is *folded* back into the baseband $[0, f_s/2]$ via aliasing.

---

## 4.8 Instrumentation Amplifier Topology (3-Op-Amp Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1["V in plus"]
    V2["V in minus"]
    OPA["Op Amp A1 input buffer plus"]
    OPB["Op Amp A2 input buffer minus"]
    OPC["Op Amp A3 difference amplifier output"]
    RG["External Gain Resistor R G between A1 and A2"]
    R1A["R"]
    R1B["R"]
    R2A["R feedback"]
    R2B["R feedback"]
    R3A["R"]
    R3B["R"]
    VOUT["V out differential and amplified"]

    V1 --> OPA
    V2 --> OPB
    OPA --> R1A --> RG --> R1B --> OPB
    OPA --> R2A --> OPC
    OPB --> R2B --> OPC
    OPC --> R3A --> VOUT
    OPC --> R3B --> VOUT
```

**Gain:** $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}$

**CMRR:** Very high (>100 dB) because the input buffer stage provides symmetrical high-impedance inputs and the difference stage rejects common-mode voltage (typically $V_{EX}/2$ from a bridge).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the Sampling Theorem. What is meant by Nyquist rate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling Theorem:** A bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its uniformly spaced samples provided the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.
>
> **Nyquist Rate:** The minimum sampling rate that just satisfies this condition is called the Nyquist rate, $f_N = 2 f_m$. Sampling below this rate causes aliasing.
>
> **Keywords for full marks:** bandlimited, $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, reconstruction, no information loss.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is aliasing? How is it prevented in a Data Acquisition System?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which frequency components of an input signal that are higher than half the sampling frequency appear as false lower-frequency components in the reconstructed output. Mathematically, an input frequency $f > f_s/2$ appears as $f_a = |f - k f_s|$ for some integer $k$.
>
> **Prevention:** An **analog low-pass anti-aliasing filter** is placed *before* the sampler, with its cutoff frequency set at or below $f_s/2$. This bandlimits the input signal so that no frequency component above $f_s/2$ reaches the sampler, thereby eliminating aliasing distortion.
>
> **Alternative preventive measures:** (i) increase sampling rate, (ii) choose ADC with sufficient over-sampling capability.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of a generalized Data Acquisition System. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A generalized DAS converts real-world physical phenomena into digital data using the following functional blocks in order:

1. **Sensor / Transducer:** Converts the physical quantity (temperature, pressure, strain, etc.) into an electrical signal (voltage or current). Example: thermocouple for temperature, strain gauge for force.

2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit:** Amplifies weak sensor signals (often millivolts) to volt-level, filters noise, linearizes nonlinear transducer outputs, and provides electrical isolation. For strain gauges, this stage includes a Wheatstone bridge and an instrumentation amplifier.

3. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter:** A low-pass filter (typically Butterworth or Bessel) with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$ that removes all frequency components above the Nyquist limit to prevent aliasing during sampling.

4. **Multiplexer (MUX):** An analog switch network that sequentially connects multiple input channels to a single shared S/H and ADC, reducing system cost in multi-channel applications.

5. **Sample and Hold (S/H) Circuit:** Captures the instantaneous value of the conditioned analog signal at each sampling instant and holds it constant during the ADC's finite conversion time, preventing conversion errors from a moving input.

6. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Quantizes each held sample into one of $2^n$ discrete levels and encodes it as an n-bit digital word (typically 8, 12, 16, or 24 bits).

7. **Digital Controller / Interface:** Receives the digital data via parallel bus, USB, RS-485, or Ethernet, and routes it to a computer or PLC for storage, display, alarm generation, and closed-loop control.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the labeled diagram.)

**Signal flow:** Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Anti-Aliasing Filter → MUX → S/H → ADC → Controller → Display/Storage.

**(b)** A signal $x(t) = 4 \sin(200\pi t) + 2 \cos(1400\pi t)$ volts is sampled at $f_s = 800$ Hz. **(i)** Find the Nyquist rate. **(ii)** Determine the apparent (aliased) frequencies of both components. **(iii)** Will there be aliasing? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Nyquist Rate:**
- Frequency of first component: $f_1 = 200\pi / (2\pi) = 100$ Hz
- Frequency of second component: $f_2 = 1400\pi / (2\pi) = 700$ Hz
- Maximum frequency: $f_m = 700$ Hz
- Nyquist Rate: $f_N = 2 \times 700 = \mathbf{1400 \text{ Hz}}$

**(ii) Aliased Frequencies:**

For the 100 Hz component:
- Since $100 \le 400$ (= f_s/2), this lies in the baseband and **does not alias**.
- Apparent frequency = $\mathbf{100 \text{ Hz}}$

For the 700 Hz component:
- $f_s/2 = 400$ Hz. Since $700 > 400$, aliasing occurs.
- Apparent frequency = $|700 - 800| = \mathbf{100 \text{ Hz}}$

**(iii) Aliasing Verdict:**
- Yes, **aliasing occurs**. Reason: $f_s = 800$ Hz < Nyquist rate = 1400 Hz, so the 700 Hz component falls outside the baseband and folds back to 100 Hz.
- **Critical consequence:** The 100 Hz *alias* of the 700 Hz component *coincides with* the original 100 Hz component. The reconstructed signal will be distorted — its amplitude at 100 Hz will be **6 V** (4 + 2) instead of the original 4 V. **Information loss is irreversible.**

**Remedy:** Use $f_s \geq 1400$ Hz, or insert a low-pass filter with cutoff ≤ 400 Hz before sampling.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a Sample and Hold circuit with a neat diagram. Define acquisition time, aperture time, and droop rate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**

A Sample and Hold (S/H) circuit consists of an **analog switch** (MOSFET or JFET), a **hold capacitor** $C_H$, and a **high-input-impedance buffer amplifier**.

```
Vin → [Analog Switch] → [C_H] → [Buffer] → Vout
           ↑                ↑
        Logic Control    Stores charge
```

**Two Operating Modes:**

1. **Sample (Track) Mode:** The control logic closes the switch, connecting the input directly to the capacitor. The capacitor charges rapidly to the input voltage through the switch's low ON-resistance. The buffer output tracks the input continuously.

2. **Hold Mode:** The control logic opens the switch. The capacitor is now isolated from the input and *retains* its charge. The buffer amplifier (with JFET or MOSFET input) draws negligible current from the capacitor, so the output remains a stable DC value equal to the sampled voltage — for the entire ADC conversion period.

**Key Specifications:**

- **Acquisition Time (t_acq):** Time required, after the switch closes (sample command), for the output to settle within a specified accuracy (typically ±0.1% or ±1 LSB) of the final input value. Limited by the RC time constant: $t_{acq} \approx t_{settling} \approx k \cdot R_{ON} \cdot C_H$.

- **Aperture Time (t_ap):** Small fixed delay between the application of the hold command and the actual opening of the switch. Caused by propagation delay of the control logic and mechanical/electrical response time of the switch.

- **Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter):** Random variation in aperture time from sample to sample. This is the **true limitation** on maximum input frequency because it introduces amplitude uncertainty.

- **Droop Rate:** Rate at which the held voltage decays during hold mode, given by:
$$\text{Droop} = \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I_{leakage}}{C_H}$$
where $I_{leakage}$ includes switch leakage current and buffer input bias current.

**(b)** A strain gauge with gauge factor $G_F = 2.1$ and nominal resistance $R_0 = 350 \, \Omega$ is used in a quarter-bridge configuration with three fixed $350 \, \Omega$ resistors. The excitation voltage is $V_{EX} = 5$ V and the applied strain is $\epsilon = 800 \, \mu\epsilon$ (microstrain). **(i)** Compute the bridge output voltage. **(ii)** If this output is fed to an instrumentation amplifier of gain 100, what is the final output voltage sent to the ADC? **(iii)** What is the percentage change in resistance of the gauge? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Quarter-Bridge Output Voltage:**

Formula for quarter bridge:
$$V_o = \frac{V_{EX}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \epsilon$$

Substituting:
$$V_o = \frac{5}{4} \times 2.1 \times (800 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$V_o = 1.25 \times 2.1 \times 8 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$V_o = 2.625 \times 8 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{V_o = 2.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2.1 \text{ mV}}$$

**(ii) Amplifier Output:**

$$V_{out,INA} = V_o \times A_v = 2.1 \text{ mV} \times 100 = \boxed{210 \text{ mV}}$$

**(iii) Percentage Resistance Change:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = G_F \cdot \epsilon = 2.1 \times 800 \times 10^{-6} = 1.68 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\% \frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = 1.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 = \boxed{0.168\%}$$

This corresponds to:
$$\Delta R = 0.00168 \times 350 = \mathbf{0.588 \, \Omega}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Strain $\epsilon$ must be in dimensionless form, not $\mu\epsilon$. Always convert microstrain to strain: $800 \, \mu\epsilon = 800 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-4}$.
> 2. **Bridge formula confusion:** Many students wrongly use the full-bridge formula $V_o = V_{EX} G_F \epsilon$ for a quarter-bridge, getting **four times** the correct answer.
> 3. **Missing the INA stage:** The bridge output alone is too small (2.1 mV). Students who stop at the bridge output without amplification lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting anti-aliasing filter and S/H:** In a *complete* DAS solution, the signal chain is Bridge → INA → LPF → S/H → ADC. Skipping the LPF/S/H steps costs marks in 14-mark problems.
> 5. **No mention of common-mode voltage:** In a real exam, the bridge output is *superimposed* on $V_{EX}/2 = 2.5$ V common-mode voltage. The INA's high CMRR is essential to reject this. Mentioning this earns bonus marks.

---

## 5.3 Additional Concept-Builder Short Questions (Quick Recall)

| Q# | Question | One-Line Answer |
|----|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Define quantization step Q | $Q = V_{FS} / 2^n$ |
| 2 | What is SQNR for a 12-bit ADC? | $6.02(12) + 1.76 = 74$ dB |
| 3 | Define gauge factor | $G_F = (\Delta R/R) / \epsilon$ |
| 4 | Why is INA used with bridge? | High CMRR + differential gain |
| 5 | Define Nyquist frequency | Maximum input frequency without aliasing = $f_s/2$ |
| 6 | Define aperture uncertainty | Variation in aperture time from sample to sample |
| 7 | What is flat-top sampling? | Pulse amplitude held constant over entire sample period |
| 8 | Name two anti-aliasing filter types | Butterworth, Bessel, Chebyshev |
| 9 | Why is S/H needed before ADC? | To prevent ADC error from input signal change during conversion |
| 10 | Full-bridge sensitivity vs quarter-bridge | Full-bridge is **4× more sensitive** |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — Memorize These Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$; Nyquist rate = $2 f_m$.
- [ ] **Whittaker–Shannon Reconstruction:** Sinc-interpolation formula.
- [ ] **Aliasing condition:** Occurs when $f > f_s/2$; folds via $f_a = |f - k f_s|$.
- [ ] **Anti-aliasing filter:** Analog LPF with $f_c \leq f_s/2$ placed **before** sampler.
- [ ] **Quantization step:** $Q = V_{FS}/2^n$.
- [ ] **SQNR formula:** $6.02n + 1.76$ dB (sine wave).
- [ ] **Sample & Hold functions:** Sample (track) and Hold (freeze).
- [ ] **Key S/H specs:** Acquisition time, Aperture time, Aperture jitter, Droop rate.
- [ ] **Droop rate equation:** $dV/dt = I_{leak}/C_H$.
- [ ] **Strain gauge equation:** $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \epsilon$.
- [ ] **Bridge outputs:** Quarter = $V_{EX} G_F \epsilon / 4$; Full = $V_{EX} G_F \epsilon$.
- [ ] **INA gain (3-op-amp):** $A_v = 1 + 2R/R_G$.
- [ ] **Full DAS block order:** Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Anti-Aliasing Filter → MUX → S/H → ADC → Computer.
- [ ] **Two DAS architectures:** Simultaneous (parallel) S/H vs Multiplexed (time-shared).
- [ ] **Flat-top sampling** is used in all real DAS (not ideal impulse sampling).
- [ ] **Common-mode voltage** $V_{EX}/2$ in bridge requires INA with **high CMRR**.

> [!WARNING]
> **FINAL EXAM ADVICE:** In any 14-mark Part-B question on this module, the examiner expects you to:
> 1. Draw a **labeled block diagram** (3 marks).
> 2. State the relevant **formula(s)** with units (3 marks).
> 3. Perform **numerical computation** with proper unit handling (5 marks).
> 4. Discuss **practical implications** (aliasing, droop, temperature compensation, etc.) (3 marks).
> A perfect answer requires **all four elements** — diagrams alone or formulas alone will not secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMRT612 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** LVDT # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **LVDT — Linear Variable Differential Transformer** > An **LVDT** (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) is a passive inductive transducer that converts linear mechanical displacement into a corresponding, proportional electrical (AC voltage) signal. It operates on the principle of mutual induction between a single primary winding and two symmetrically placed secondary windings wound on a cylindrical, hollow former. A movable soft-iron core, mechanically coupled to the measurand, alters the mutual flux linkage between the primary and the two secondaries, producing a differential output voltage that is a linear function of core displacement over a calibrated range. The LVDT is classified under **electromechanical passive transducers**, specifically the **mutual-inductance displacement sensor** family. It requires an external AC excitation (typically **1 V to 24 V RMS** at a carrier frequency between **1 kHz to 10 kHz**) and produces an **AC output** proportional to displacement — it is *not* a self-generating device. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic See-Saw" Imagine a **floating magnetic see-saw** inside a hollow tube: - The **center pivot** is the primary coil (constantly "shouting" an AC magnetic signal into the tube). - The **two ends** of the see-saw are the two secondary coils (each "listening" to the primary's signal). - The **rider sitting on the see-saw** is the soft-iron core. When the rider sits exactly in the middle (null position), both secondary coils hear the primary *equally loudly* → **output = 0**. - When the rider slides to the **left**, the left secondary hears a louder signal than the right → **output is positive**. - When the rider slides to the **right**, the right secondary hears more → **output is negative but proportional**. This "see-saw of magnetic coupling" is the entire working philosophy of the LVDT. The displacement of the core literally *unbalances* the magnetic coupling, and the difference in the two secondary voltages becomes a precise electrical signature of *how far* and *in which direction* the core moved. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 OEMRT612):** LVDT is grouped with **other displacement/motion transducers** (such as RTD, thermistors, and piezoelectric sensors) and is universally tested under **Module 4 — Signal Conditioning & Data Acquisition Transducers**. Examiner focus is on (i) construction, (ii) working + null position concept, (iii) output voltage equation, (iv) advantages/disadvantages, and (v) phase-sensitive demodulation interpretation. ## 1.3 Physical Construction Overview The LVDT consists of the following physical elements: 1. **One Primary Winding (P):** Wound centrally on a hollow non-magnetic (often stainless-steel or ceramic) cylindrical former. Energized by an external AC excitation source. 2. **Two Identical Secondary Windings (S₁ and S₂):** Wound symmetrically on either side of the primary. They are connected in **series opposition** (i.e., S₁ and S₂ are wired so their EMFs subtract). 3. **Movable Soft-Iron Core:** A cylindrical nickel-iron or permalloy core that slides freely inside the hollow former, mechanically coupled to the element whose displacement is being measured. 4. **Shielding:** A magnetic shield (mu-metal) surrounding the assembly to reject external EMI. > [!NOTE] > **Symbol Conventions Used Throughout This Note** > - $e_p$ = RMS voltage applied to the primary > - $E_{s1}, E_{s2}$ = RMS EMFs induced in secondary coils S₁ and S₂ > - $x$ = displacement of core from null (electrical null) position > - $x_{max}$ = full-scale rated displacement (mechanical limit of linear region) > - $K$ = sensitivity constant (V/mm) - **Standard carrier frequencies:** **1 kHz, 2.5 kHz, 5 kHz, 10 kHz** (carrier must be 5–10× the highest expected mechanical motion frequency to satisfy Nyquist-like anti-aliasing for demodulation). # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Stages (The "Why" Behind Each Step) ### Stage 1 — Excitation of the Primary A sinusoidal AC voltage $e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$ is applied to the primary winding. This generates an alternating magnetic flux $\phi_p$ inside the hollow former along its longitudinal axis. Because the primary is **uniformly wound at the center**, the flux density is essentially uniform across the active length when no core is present — both secondaries would receive *identical* coupling. ### Stage 2 — Mutual Induction at Null Position When the **ferromagnetic core is exactly centered** between the two secondaries, the mutual inductance between the primary and S₁ is exactly equal to the mutual inductance between the primary and S₂. Therefore: - Flux linking S₁ = Flux linking S₂ - $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ - Because they are wound in **series opposition**, the differential output $e_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = 0$ → this is the **electrical null position**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Concept — Null Position:** Examiners repeatedly test this. At the *null position* the **differential output is zero** but the **individual secondary EMFs are at their maximum**. Null is the most stable reference point and is the basis for **zero-based calibration**. ### Stage 3 — Displacement of the Core When the core shifts by a small displacement $x$ toward secondary S₁: - The mutual coupling between primary and S₁ **increases** → $E_{s1}$ rises. - The mutual coupling between primary and S₂ **decreases** → $E_{s2}$ falls. - The differential output $e_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ becomes **positive and proportional to $x$**. When the core shifts in the opposite direction (toward S₂), the situation mirrors exactly, and $e_o$ becomes **negative but of the same magnitude** for the same $|x|$.
